subtitle: 姫路城〜姫路駅 · ベジ・グルテンフリー

places.name: 和ノ食 奏 (Wa no Shoku Kanade)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese kitchen: soy sauce contains wheat, tempura batter is wheat-based (including the signature conger eel tempura), and dashi may contain fish stock processed at facilities using wheat. Nigiri rice itself is gluten-free, and some sashimi dishes could be requested without soy sauce, but no dedicated GF protocol or allergen disclosures are published. Celiac diners must contact the restaurant directly before visiting — the kitchen has not demonstrated GF awareness and cross-contamination risk is high from tempura frying.
places.vegan_detail: This is a sushi and conger eel specialist. The menu is built around fish, seafood, egg custard (chawanmushi), wagyu, and chicken. Dashi is almost certainly fish-based (katsuo/iriko), and nearly every dish contains animal products. No vegan options are listed, and no indication that accommodations are available. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: Closed Mon. Tue–Thu 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Fri–Sun 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30, reservation required), 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Some reports say no Sunday dinner (please confirm).
places.neighborhood: Nishinikaimachi (north of Himeji Stn, along Otemae-dori)
places.cuisine: Sushi, anago (conger eel) cuisine, creative Japanese
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born 1978 in Hyogo Prefecture. Trained at high-end Himeji establishments including hotel restaurants and Michelin-listed counters before opening Wa no Shoku Kanade in March 2022 as owner-chef.

chef_bio.background: Born 1978 in Hyogo Prefecture. Trained at high-end Himeji establishments including hotel restaurants and Michelin-listed counters before opening Wa no Shoku Kanade in March 2022 as owner-chef.
chef_bio.philosophy: A welcoming space (居心地のよい空間づくり) where conversation flows naturally across every seat — counter, sunken-hearth private room, or second-floor banquet — paired with fresh seasonal fish and a hand-picked selection of over 20 Hyogo sake labels.
chef_bio.specialty: Steamed conger eel nigiri (蒸し穴子握り) — anago sourced locally from the Seto Inland Sea, steamed and served with a reduction sauce made from the eel's own braising liquid; the texture is described by guests as melting immediately on the palate.

anecdotes.text: The Friday-to-Sunday lunch, called 奏の遊び膳 (Kanade no Asobizen), rotates monthly and presents seasonal ingredients in playful multi-preparation trays — a format distinct from the more formal dinner omakase.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen actively fries tempura — including the signature conger eel tempura with wheat batter — making shared-fryer cross-contamination a realistic concern. No dedicated GF fryer is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing) is the presumed base for sashimi service, nigiri, and cooked sauces. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is listed on the menu or website.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi underpins virtually every dish in a traditional washoku kitchen of this type; no plant-based dashi option is disclosed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, no published allergy protocol, and no review mentions of allergy accommodation were found across Tabelog, Hitosara, Gnavi, or the official website. Advance telephone inquiry is strongly recommended.

positives.text: Some sashimi items are naturally free of added sauces and could in principle be ordered with rice only, keeping wheat exposure minimal if the kitchen is briefed in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate counter and tatami private-room setting with Japanese-language service only; online reservation possible via Tabelog or AutoReserve but communication with staff will be in Japanese.

places.name: 讃岐うどん めんめ (Sanuki Udon Menme)
places.gf_detail: Udon noodles are made from wheat flour — the core product of the restaurant. There is no gluten-free alternative and no dedicated fryer. The dashi soy sauce (だし醤油) used in the signature dish contains wheat. Cross-contamination risk is extremely high given the wheat-only kitchen. Unsafe for celiac or wheat-allergic diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is not a vegan-friendly restaurant. The signature menme udon is built around bonito flakes (鰹節) and egg yolk mixed into the bowl. The dashi broth is fish-based (katsuo). No plant-based or animal-free options are documented anywhere in the menu.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu–Sun 11:30–19:00, closed Wed
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-running family operation in Himeji's Honcho district, steps from the approach to Himeji Castle. Accounts from regular visitors describe roughly 40 years of continuous operation, run by a female proprietor with family involvement. The shop follows a simple, old-school rhyth

chef_bio.background: A long-running family operation in Himeji's Honcho district, steps from the approach to Himeji Castle. Accounts from regular visitors describe roughly 40 years of continuous operation, run by a female proprietor with family involvement. The shop follows a simple, old-school rhythm: noodles are hand-made on the premises each day and the kitchen closes when the daily batch runs out, sometimes well before the posted closing time.
chef_bio.philosophy: Straightforward, ingredient-led cooking at an accessible price point. The menu is short, the space is unpretentious, and the focus is entirely on the noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Menme udon — the house signature: a bowl of hand-pulled noodles topped with a generous mound of bonito flakes and a raw egg yolk, finished with dashi soy sauce and green onion; diners mix everything together before eating. The noodles are notably thicker than standard Sanuki udon and softer in texture, giving the bowl a distinctly gentle character.

anecdotes.text: Regulars note that each bowl includes 'one particularly thick end piece' (1本だけ太い端っこ) — an artifact of hand-cutting the noodles that has become a beloved marker of an authentic Menme experience.
One long-time patron described the owners as having been in operation for approximately 40 years; as of a February 2026 visit the shop was busy with international tourists around 2 PM.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the kitchen's only product — udon noodles are made from wheat flour on-site daily. Cross-contamination with wheat is unavoidable throughout the entire operation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature menme udon uses dashi soy sauce (だし醤油), which is wheat-containing. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito flakes (鰹節) are a primary topping on the signature dish and are present throughout the kitchen. Fish-based dashi is the foundation of the broth. No vegan-safe preparation path exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen sheet, or staff allergy-handling procedure is documented on any source reviewed. Given the language environment (Japanese-only) and the simplicity of the operation, allergy communication would be very difficult.

cultural_comfort.note: A tightly run, cash-only counter shop with no English menu or reservation system; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu board will get you by, but expect to navigate entirely in Japanese.

places.name: おもてなしダイニング 福亭 (Omotenashi Dining Fukutei)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu exists. One TripAdvisor reviewer reported that a preset meal was successfully adapted for a celiac diner with only a few substitutions, suggesting staff willingness to accommodate. However, standard Japanese cuisine relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi stock that may include soy sauce; tempura batter uses wheat flour; Himeji oden broth is not confirmed wheat-free. Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. Advance reservation and explicit communication in Japanese recommended for any GF request.
places.vegan_detail: No dedicated vegan menu. The restaurant is fish-focused (anago/conger eel is the signature) and the halal menu centers on fish and vegetables. One reviewer noted an oden dish was adjusted to tofu and vegetables on request, excluding meat. Hidden dashi (fish stock) is very likely in most dishes; bonito-based dashi is standard in Japanese cooking. Vegetarian icon appears on the Halal Gourmet Japan listing, suggesting vegetable-only options are possible with advance notice, but full vegan accommodation (no dashi, no fish sauces) requires direct confirmation with the kitchen. Advance reservation required.
places.neighborhood: Kameimachi (5 min from Himeji Stn north exit)
places.cuisine: Anago (conger eel) cuisine, seasonal seafood, izakaya
places.notes: Fukutei is operated under the Seiko Club (清交倶楽部) hospitality group and has been a fixture of Himeji's dining scene for at least a decade, situated a five-minute walk north of Himeji Station along the Miyuki-dori shopping street. The restaurant built an unusual dual reputation: as

chef_bio.origin: 姫路市亀井町、瀬戸内海の恵みを活かした海鮮料理店
chef_bio.background: Fukutei is operated under the Seiko Club (清交倶楽部) hospitality group and has been a fixture of Himeji's dining scene for at least a decade, situated a five-minute walk north of Himeji Station along the Miyuki-dori shopping street. The restaurant built an unusual dual reputation: as a go-to neighborhood spot for seasonal Seto Inland Sea seafood, and as one of Japan's early adopters of halal-certified fish cuisine—reportedly the first in Japan to offer halal conger-eel hitsumabushi (a layered rice dish) and halal anago tempura.
chef_bio.philosophy: The chef visits the fish market each morning to hand-select the day's catch by sight and touch, refusing to compromise on ingredient quality. The stated mission is to make delicious seasonal fish accessible to as many guests as possible and to create smiles through food (食を通して笑顔になってほしい).
chef_bio.specialty: Conger eel (穴子) from Harima-nada in multiple preparations—hitsumabushi, tempura, clay-pot rice with sea bream—alongside daily-selected sashimi and Himeji oden

anecdotes.text: Fukutei is cited by Halal Gourmet Japan as serving Japan's first halal conger-eel hitsumabushi and conger-eel tempura, with dates offered as dessert—a nod to Muslim dining customs.

A TripAdvisor reviewer reported that the kitchen successfully adapted a fixed-course meal for a celiac diner with only minor substitutions, and separately that a meat-free oden was arranged on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about dedicated fryers or separate prep surfaces. Tempura (wheat batter) is a menu staple, raising shared-fryer cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is almost certainly used throughout the kitchen; no mention of tamari or GF shoyu substitutes anywhere on the menu or official site.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-centric kitchen; dashi stock (bonito/fish-based) is standard across Japanese cuisine and likely used as a base in most dishes including oden broth, rice dishes, and sauces.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A TripAdvisor reviewer reported that staff successfully modified a preset meal for a celiac guest with a few substitutions, and adapted an oden dish to exclude meat on request—suggesting genuine willingness to accommodate, but no formal allergy protocol is documented.
Hitosara lists food allergy considerations as accepted (食物アレルギー対応可). Advance reservation and explicit communication required.

positives.text: Halal-certified with a dedicated halal menu (anago hitsumabushi, anago tempura); no pork used in halal dishes; on-site prayer space provided—showing serious dietary-protocol awareness.
Fully non-smoking establishment.
Hitosara lists gluten-free accommodations as available (グルテンフリー対応可).

cultural_comfort.note: Staff have been observed helping foreign diners with menu translation and dietary substitutions; English menus likely available given halal-certified tourist-oriented positioning and TripAdvisor presence.

places.name: 停主 (Teishu)
places.gf_detail: Himeji oden is served with ginger soy sauce — conventional Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and is the defining flavoring of this dish. The oden broth itself is soy-sauce-based. Yakisoba is on the menu. No dedicated gluten-free options or alternatives have been documented. Celiacs should avoid unless they can clearly communicate needs in Japanese and staff confirm wheat-free substitution is possible, which is unlikely given the core broth.
places.vegan_detail: Teishu is a chicken-and-oden izakaya. The oden broth is a long-simmered dashi base; the grilled-chicken menu dominates. No plant-based alternatives are offered, and the kitchen is structured entirely around animal proteins.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun 18:00–23:00 (closed Mon)
places.neighborhood: 本町 (Honmachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Teishu traces its roots to roughly 60 years ago when it opened as Yougetsu Shokudo (ようげつ食堂) near the Himeji Civic Hall. The founder chose the name 停主 — meaning something like 'a host who bids guests to pause and rest' — to express a philosophy of unhurried welcome. The current ow

chef_bio.background: Teishu traces its roots to roughly 60 years ago when it opened as Yougetsu Shokudo (ようげつ食堂) near the Himeji Civic Hall. The founder chose the name 停主 — meaning something like 'a host who bids guests to pause and rest' — to express a philosophy of unhurried welcome. The current owner is the second generation. The shop stood for decades on a lane facing Himeji Castle until an April 2024 fire destroyed the building completely. With no insurance payout (coverage had lapsed due to an adjuster's hospitalization), the owner relied on community support — including a charity T-shirt campaign at ¥4,000 per piece — and his son's help to clear the site and begin reconstruction. In May 2025 he reopened in a makeshift outdoor space on the parking lot, described by visitors as evoking a nostalgic Showa-era beer garden atmosphere. As of June 2026 the kitchen is still serving from that temporary setup while the permanent rebuild awaits Cultural Affairs Agency clearance, required because the site lies along the official approach path to Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Traditional homestyle cooking rooted in place — the kind of food the founder described as 'okafukuro no aji' (the taste of mother's cooking) — served in an atmosphere meant to make strangers feel like regulars.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji oden — twelve varieties of slow-simmered oden presented in a deep, richly flavored broth, finished tableside with ginger soy sauce that balances the sweetness with heat. The other pillar is charcoal-grilled Banshu local chicken: the off-menu matsuba cut (a sliver of collarbone cartilage, one per bird) is a cult favourite, and hineppon — aged hen dressed in ponzu — is the benchmark order.

anecdotes.text: After the 2024 fire levelled the building, the owner discovered his insurance had lapsed because the adjuster handling renewal had been hospitalised — leaving him without any payout. The community responded: a charity T-shirt sold for ¥4,000 helped fund the rebuild, and a temporary outdoor kitchen opened in May 2025 on the scorched parking lot.
The walls of the original shop were lined with signed photographs from visiting celebrities, including actress Haruka Ayase, testament to the restaurant's draw beyond the local neighbourhood.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares yakisoba (wheat noodles) alongside oden and grilled items in what is currently a compact temporary outdoor setup, making cross-contamination a realistic concern. No separation protocols have been documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Himeji oden is defined by its ginger soy sauce finish (生姜醤油). Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat as a primary ingredient. The oden broth itself is soy-sauce-based, so gluten is embedded in the dish's identity, not just a condiment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around chicken and dashi-based broth. There is no plant-based cooking line and no documented effort to keep any item free of animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling protocols or English-language allergy communication have been documented for this restaurant. The staff environment is Japanese-only and the kitchen is a temporary setup post-fire.

positives.text: The restaurant accepts credit cards (JCB, AMEX, Diners) and PayPay, reducing one barrier for foreign visitors, and single diners are explicitly welcome at the counter.

cultural_comfort.note: Menu, staff, and signage are entirely in Japanese; a phone reservation is expected and essentially requires Japanese, though pointing at dishes once seated is manageable.

places.name: 亜米麻辣湯 姫路店 (Yummy Mala Tang Himeji)
places.gf_detail: Six noodle types are offered: sweet potato glass noodles, corn noodles, potato noodles, rice noodles (potentially wheat-free), and Chinese wheat noodles plus udon (wheat). The soup base and condiments (black vinegar, Sichuan peppercorn oil, sesame sauce) have unknown soy sauce and wheat content — the chain publishes no allergen sheet. No dedicated gluten-free handling or separate fryer confirmed. Celiac diners should inquire directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The soup base is beef bone broth (牛骨だし) simmered with 20+ medicinal herbs — not vegan. Vegetable and tofu ingredients can be selected, but they cook in the same beef broth. No plant-based broth alternative is listed on any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun & holidays 11:00–21:00, open daily
places.neighborhood: Around Himeji Stn, Gofukumachi
places.cuisine: Mala tang (Sichuan spicy hotpot soup), Korean-style
places.notes: Korean mala tang chain originally operating approximately 80 locations in South Korea, recognized with the Korean Consumer Brand Award in the mala tang franchise category for two consecutive years (2022 and 2023). The Himeji location, which opened February 21, 2026, is the chain'

chef_bio.background: Korean mala tang chain originally operating approximately 80 locations in South Korea, recognized with the Korean Consumer Brand Award in the mala tang franchise category for two consecutive years (2022 and 2023). The Himeji location, which opened February 21, 2026, is the chain's first Hyogo Prefecture outpost, following Japan entries in Tokyo's Shin-Okubo and Osaka's Tsuruhashi.
chef_bio.philosophy: Medicinal herb cuisine (薬膳) meets modern build-your-own customization — over 40 ingredient choices let each diner tailor heat, texture, and nutrition to their preference.
chef_bio.specialty: Mala tang — a customizable spicy hot pot where diners pick ingredients from a refrigerated showcase on the ground floor, priced by weight at ¥400 per 100g. The broth is beef-bone stock slow-simmered with over 20 medicinal herbs including jujubes and goji berries, offered across five spice levels from 0 (clear broth, child-friendly) to 4 (extra spicy).

anecdotes.text: The chain's signature broth recipe reportedly took approximately one year to develop, blending beef bones with more than 20 medicinal herbs to achieve a depth that builds as you eat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All ingredients — including those that may contain wheat — are cooked in a shared beef-bone broth pot. No separation of wheat-containing and gluten-free noodles is confirmed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soup base ingredient list (beef bone broth, 20+ medicinal herbs and spices) is not fully disclosed. Condiments include sesame sauce and Sichuan peppercorn oil of unknown composition; soy sauce presence in the broth cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All ingredients are cooked in beef-bone broth; there is no plant-based broth option, so vegan cross-contact is certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen sheet or staff allergy protocol is published on Tabelog, HotPepper, or the official website. Diners with allergies should call ahead (090-9615-5959) to discuss their needs.

positives.text: Multiple wheat-free noodle options are available (sweet potato glass noodles, corn noodles, potato noodles, rice noodles) allowing diners to avoid wheat noodles specifically — though the broth wheat status remains unconfirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: Korean chain with QR-code ordering, self-service ingredient selection, and payment via international cards and QR apps (Alipay, WeChat Pay) — visitors without Japanese are likely comfortable here.

places.name: 姫路おでん 地料理 居酒屋 じごろ小廣 (Jigoro Kohiro)
places.gf_detail: Gluten is pervasive throughout the menu. Himeji oden is served with homemade ginger soy sauce (wheat-based shoyu). The menu includes multiple tempura items, karaage, chikuwa (fish cake with wheat binder), and noodle dishes. The dashi base likely contains bonito and kombu but the soy sauce used in seasoning is standard wheat-containing shoyu. No GF menu, no allergen labeling, and no indication that wheat-free soy sauce is available. Celiac or wheat-allergic diners should avoid this restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: The kitchen is built around bonito-and-kombu dashi, seafood (octopus, conger eel, sea bream, oysters), eggs, and meat. No plant-based alternatives were identified on the menu. Vegan dining is not possible here.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sat 11:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:30); Sun/holidays 11:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). No regular weekly closing day (irregular days off); occasional irregular closures.
places.neighborhood: JR姫路駅前 (Himeji Station front, Ekimae-cho)
places.cuisine: Himeji oden, Banshu local cuisine, anago
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Jigoro Kohiro is a small independent izakaya occupying the ground floor of the Himeji Station Front Building, about a two-minute walk north of JR Himeji Station along the Omizo-dori shopping arcade. The restaurant centers its identity on Himeji oden — a regional variant of Japan'

chef_bio.background: Jigoro Kohiro is a small independent izakaya occupying the ground floor of the Himeji Station Front Building, about a two-minute walk north of JR Himeji Station along the Omizo-dori shopping arcade. The restaurant centers its identity on Himeji oden — a regional variant of Japan's beloved hot-pot dish that sets itself apart by being eaten with homemade ginger soy sauce rather than mustard. The ginger-soy tradition has roots in Himeji's early Showa-era waterfront districts and was formally named 'Himeji oden' in 2006 by a local revitalization group.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcase Banshu and Himeji's local larder — Murotsu octopus, Ichikawa-town eggs, regional sake from labels such as Okuharima — in a relaxed counter-and-table atmosphere suited to solo diners and couples alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji oden served with homemade ginger soy sauce; grilled and tempura anago (conger eel); the 'White Heron Platter' (白鷺盛り) featuring local Murotsu octopus, daikon, and ichikawa-town boiled eggs, with eel steamed then briefly simmered in broth.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's oden broth is built from bonito and kombu dashi and simmered for one to two days before service; the White Heron Platter eel is steamed first, then briefly passed through the broth to achieve tenderness and a cleaner aroma.

Himeji oden's defining ginger-soy sauce tradition likely began in the city's early Showa-period waterfront district, where locals used ginger soy sauce to temper the sweetness of Kanto-style oden — a practice that only a handful of regions in all of Japan share.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Multiple tempura items are listed (conger eel tempura, lotus root tempura, yam tempura, octopus tempura, surume tempura, kisu tempura) indicating active wheat-batter frying; no separate fryer for gluten-free items is noted.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature Himeji oden is served with homemade ginger soy sauce; multiple dishes reference soy-based sauces, ponzu, dashi soy, and wasabi mayo — all made with standard wheat-containing shoyu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito-and-kombu dashi is used as the base broth simmered one to two days; it permeates the oden and likely touches most hot dishes. Fish, seafood, eggs, and meat are cooked throughout the kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy inquiry system, or staff allergy-handling procedure is documented on any available source. Japanese communication will be required to ask questions, and the kitchen is unlikely to have protocols for celiac-safe preparation.

positives.text: The restaurant is a small owner-operated counter with a focused menu, meaning staff likely have detailed knowledge of their ingredients and preparations — a Japanese-speaking diner may be able to get honest answers about specific dishes.

cultural_comfort.note: A small local izakaya near Himeji Station with no English-language menu or signage noted; Japanese communication will be needed to order and ask about ingredients.

places.name: 更科 一喜 (Sarashina Itsuki)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners. Sarashina (更科) soba uses refined white buckwheat flour that is routinely blended with wheat flour — this is not a 100% buckwheat (juwari) shop. The tsuyu broth contains wheat-based soy sauce (standard shoyu). Tempura is battered with wheat flour. There is no dedicated GF fryer, no GF labeling, and no mention of allergen accommodation on any source. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small kitchen making noodles daily.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options confirmed. Sarashina-style soba tsuyu is almost certainly made with katsuo (bonito) dashi, which is standard for this style. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned on any listing. The menu includes tempura dishes (shrimp, vegetables) indicating mixed animal/plant frying. Nothing on the menu or any review suggests vegan accommodation.
places.neighborhood: Shirogane-cho (around Sanyo-Himeji Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, long-established soba shop in Himeji's Shirogane-cho district, run by a husband-and-wife team. The restaurant sits about 240 meters from Sanyo Himeji Station and serves Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles — a refined white soba made from the inner heart of the buckwheat gr

chef_bio.background: A small, long-established soba shop in Himeji's Shirogane-cho district, run by a husband-and-wife team. The restaurant sits about 240 meters from Sanyo Himeji Station and serves Sarashina-style buckwheat noodles — a refined white soba made from the inner heart of the buckwheat grain, prized for its delicate flavor and smooth texture.
chef_bio.specialty: 天ぷらそば（Tempura Soba）— crispy-battered shrimp, shishito pepper, sweet potato, and carrot over soba in hot broth

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe the atmosphere as clean and homey (清潔でアットホームな雰囲気), with the tempura praised for its light, crispy batter (サクサクの衣).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sarashina soba typically contains wheat flour mixed with buckwheat; wheat flour is also used for tempura batter in the same small kitchen, creating high cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tsuyu (soba broth/dipping sauce) at traditional soba restaurants is made with standard wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu); no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Katsuo (bonito) dashi is the standard base for soba tsuyu at Sarashina-style shops; shrimp tempura is also on the menu, making animal-product contact unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen labeling, or accommodation policy is mentioned on any platform. Cash-only, no-reservation shop with no English materials; allergy communication would require Japanese.

positives.text: Buckwheat (soba) itself is inherently gluten-free, and zaru soba (cold plain buckwheat without tsuyu) could reduce exposure — but staff communication barriers and shared kitchen make this unreliable for celiac diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A cash-only neighborhood soba shop with no English menu and no reservation system; basic Japanese or pointing at a menu board will get you through.

places.name: ネパールインド料理 ダルバル (DARBAR (Nepal India Ryori Darubaru))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free certification or handling. Rice (白米) and rice-based curry sets are the safest GF choice — rice is served with all set menus. Naan is made from wheat flour in a clay tandoor oven and is not GF. Momo (dumpling) wrappers contain wheat. Pakoda (fritters) use chickpea batter but shared fry oil is likely. Spice mixes used in curry should be wheat-free but cross-contamination from naan flour in the kitchen is unverified. Soy sauce is not a feature of Nepalese-Indian curry cuisine, so shoyu-wheat is not a concern here. Celiacs should ask about shared fryer and kitchen flour handling before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Vegetable curry (ミックスベジタブルカレー) and dal/lentil curry (ダルマサラ) are on the lunch menu and are likely vegan by default — Indian curry bases here use no dashi or katsuo. However, ghee (clarified butter) is commonly used in Indian cooking and may appear in curry bases or to finish naan; this is not confirmed as absent. Momo (steamed dumplings) wrappers are wheat-based but the filling can be vegetable. Confirm with the kitchen whether any butter or dairy is used in curry prep if strict vegan.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), dinner 15:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30). No regular closing day (some sources say closed Mon).
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, Otemae-dori (toward Himeji Castle)
places.notes: DARBAR is a Nepalese and Indian cuisine restaurant located in the Himekoji building on Otemae-dori, the main boulevard leading from Himeji Station to Himeji Castle. It is run by chefs with origins in Nepal and India. A second location (DARBAR 兼田店) opened in Himeji's Kaneda distri

chef_bio.background: DARBAR is a Nepalese and Indian cuisine restaurant located in the Himekoji building on Otemae-dori, the main boulevard leading from Himeji Station to Himeji Castle. It is run by chefs with origins in Nepal and India. A second location (DARBAR 兼田店) opened in Himeji's Kaneda district in July 2024, suggesting the original Honmachi shop has become established enough to expand.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-baked naan from a large clay tandoor oven — reviewers specifically note the naan as oversized and freshly blistered. Curry sets allow choosing from chicken, mixed vegetable, or lentil dal options.

anecdotes.text: A 2022 reviewer described the interior as 'simple for an Indian-style restaurant, with touches of Japanese design elements in various spots,' finding it an interesting combination.
Another visitor ordered momo (steamed dumplings) and Mix Pakoda, noting the momo resembled 'curry-flavored xiaolongbao' — pointing to the Nepalese influence alongside the Indian menu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naan is baked in a clay tandoor that is also used for wheat-flour breads; airborne wheat flour in the kitchen is a realistic concern for celiac diners. No information on separate prep areas was found.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is not used in Nepalese or Indian curry cooking. No soy sauce wheat risk identified for curry dishes here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Ghee (clarified butter) is commonly used in Indian restaurant cooking and may appear in curry bases or applied to naan; no source confirms it is absent from the vegetable curries. Ask the kitchen directly.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, allergen chart, or staff allergy-handling protocol was found on any source. Direct inquiry in Japanese is necessary before ordering.

positives.text: Indian and Nepalese curry bases are naturally free of Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), dashi, mirin, and other common Japanese hidden-wheat or hidden-fish ingredients. Rice is available with all set menus as a wheat-free starch alternative to naan.

cultural_comfort.note: The restaurant appears to be run by Nepalese and Indian chefs; Japanese is the working language and English is likely limited, but ordering by pointing at the menu and basic Japanese phrases should work fine.

places.name: 蕎麦切り 谷口 (Sobakiri Taniguchi)
places.gf_detail: Not safe for celiac or gluten-avoiders. The soba is a 9:1 buckwheat-to-wheat flour blend (いちきゅうそば / ichi-ku soba) — wheat is a deliberate ingredient. Tempura is also served, adding further wheat exposure. The kitchen is a shared small space (~33 m²) with no dedicated GF area. No gluten-free claims or allergen accommodations appear in any source.
places.vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Courses include duck (charcoal-grilled option), tempura (including seafood kakiage), and dashi broth that reviewers describe as freshly made — almost certainly bonito-based (鰹だし) in the traditional style. No plant-based alternatives are mentioned in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat & holidays 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Uomachi / Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Taniguchi Takuya previously worked at soba restaurants and sought to open independently. He connected with Masato Imanishi (今西政人), owner of the evening kaiseki venture 割烹 一意専心, who proposed a dual-operation model: Imanishi would teach Taniguchi Japanese cooking in exchange for Ta

chef_bio.background: Taniguchi Takuya previously worked at soba restaurants and sought to open independently. He connected with Masato Imanishi (今西政人), owner of the evening kaiseki venture 割烹 一意専心, who proposed a dual-operation model: Imanishi would teach Taniguchi Japanese cooking in exchange for Taniguchi running the daytime soba service in his space.
chef_bio.philosophy: Taniguchi wanted customers to experience soba through fragrance and mouthfeel rather than noodles alone, building a course format that frames the bowl as a destination rather than a standalone dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Ichi-ku soba — a 90% buckwheat / 10% wheat blend chosen to maximize the grain's natural aroma while keeping a smooth texture; served in multi-course lunches starting with nine small appetizers and tempura before the soba itself.

anecdotes.text: The shop uses a reversible sign that physically flips between the daytime soba identity and the nighttime Japanese cuisine identity — a literal two-faced storefront that has become a talking point among Himeji diners.
Despite opening in June 2025, the restaurant was already extremely difficult to book within the first month, with one food blogger noting they 'barely managed a reservation' after trying repeatedly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a shared, compact space (~33 m²) serving wheat-containing soba and tempura; no dedicated GF zone or fryer is mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy-based tsuyu is used for soba dipping broth. One review notes 'salt and wasabi' as an optional seasoning (塩と山葵で), but ordinary wheat-containing soy sauce is implied as the default.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Freshly made dashi (作りたてのお出汁) is cited by a reviewer — in this traditional context almost certainly bonito (鰹節) based. No plant-based dashi alternative is mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter soba specialist in a small, intimate 10-tsubo space; no English materials confirmed, but the course-based lunch format is navigable by pointing at a menu.

places.name: 小料理 葉月 (Koryori Hazuki)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Japanese cuisine using wheat-containing soy sauce (standard shoyu) throughout marinades, glazes, and dipping sauces. Miso-marinated fish (西京漬け) uses Kyoto white miso — miso itself is usually wheat-free but cross-use with soy sauce is the norm here. Fried dishes (karaage, croquette, oyster fry, tempura) use wheat batter; no dedicated fryer or GF frying oil noted. No GF labeling, no allergen menu published. Not a safe destination for celiac diners without detailed advance coordination in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: Menu is firmly built around fish, seafood, and meat. Dashi base is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito) or fish-based given the cuisine style; no plant-based dashi mentioned. Saikyo miso marinade uses Kyoto white miso, which may be vegan in itself, but is applied to fish. Vegetable dishes exist (salads, eggplant dengaku, seasonal clay pot rice) but no vegan labeling or accommodation noted anywhere. Guests would need to inquire in Japanese.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:30–22:30 (L.O. 22:00), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: 魚町 (Uocho), Himeji — approx. 7–10 min walk from JR Himeji St
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born 1964 in Himeji into a family where parents, relatives, and siblings all worked in the food industry. Initially uncertain of his own path, he eventually followed family tradition into professional cooking. He trained across multiple Japanese culinary disciplines — traditional

chef_bio.background: Born 1964 in Himeji into a family where parents, relatives, and siblings all worked in the food industry. Initially uncertain of his own path, he eventually followed family tradition into professional cooking. He trained across multiple Japanese culinary disciplines — traditional washoku restaurants, yakitori, and sushi — accumulating over 43 years of professional experience before opening his own creative restaurant in 2000. The current incarnation as 小料理 葉月 emerged through a subsequent relocation and renovation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Deliberately named the restaurant 小料理 (small cuisine) rather than 割烹 (formal kaiseki) to signal an intention to transcend rigid genre boundaries. The chef aims to actively converse with guests and explain ingredients personally, treating each dish — down to garnishes — as handcrafted.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji lotus root dumpling with crab ankake sauce (姫路れんこん饅頭 蟹あんかけ), and A5 Japanese black beef fillet steak. Also known for house-made Saikyo miso-marinated fish using fish the chef catches himself.

anecdotes.text: The chef makes his own Saikyo miso marinade using Kyoto-sourced white miso and applies it to fish he has caught himself — a detail he shares openly with guests at the counter.
When asked about the name, the chef explained he chose 小料理 over 割烹 precisely because he wanted freedom to move across culinary genres and serve whatever is excellent that season.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried dishes including karaage, croquette, oyster fry, and tempura are on the menu with no mention of a dedicated fryer or separate oil for GF cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing shoyu is the baseline seasoning in this style of Japanese cuisine; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned on any published menu or listing.
Saikyo miso marinade (西京漬け) uses Kyoto white miso — likely wheat-free — but is used exclusively on fish, and cross-contact with regular soy sauce during prep cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No plant-based dashi is indicated; standard Japanese dashi using katsuobushi (bonito flakes) is the expected stock base for sauces, soups, and braised dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen menu, GF protocol, or allergy-handling statement found across Tabelog, Hitosara, HotPepper, or Gurunavi listings. Guests with serious dietary restrictions would need to call or inquire in person in Japanese.

positives.text: The chef personally engages with counter guests and explains ingredient sourcing in detail — making direct, in-person communication about dietary needs more feasible than at a large impersonal restaurant.

Vegetable-forward dishes exist on the menu (Hazuki-style vegetable salad with housemade dressing, eggplant dengaku, seasonal clay pot rice with mountain vegetables) that may be adaptable with communication.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate counter-focused small restaurant where the chef converses actively with guests in Japanese; no English-language accommodations indicated.

places.name: イタリアン食堂 ピザマリア 姫路店 (Italian Shokudo Pizza Maria Himeji)
places.gf_detail: All pizzas use standard wheat-flour Neapolitan dough baked in a shared wood-fired stone oven. All pasta dishes contain wheat. No gluten-free dough alternative is listed anywhere on the menu or any third-party source. Cross-contamination in the shared oven is near-certain. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is dairy- and meat-heavy throughout. The Marinara pizza (tomato, garlic, oregano, olive oil — no cheese listed, ¥1,100) is the one potentially vegan item, but the kitchen uses cheese and cured meats extensively and no vegan designation exists. Confirm at time of order whether anchovies or lard are in the dough or sauce.
places.hours_raw: Open Mon; Tue–Sun 17:00–23:30 (L.O. 23:00); closed Thu
places.neighborhood: Minamimachi, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: Opened September 2015 in Minamicho, a short walk from both JR Himeji Station and Sanyo Himeji Station. The restaurant occupies three floors, with a casual bar-style ground floor, intimate table dining on the second floor, and a party room on the third.

chef_bio.background: Opened September 2015 in Minamicho, a short walk from both JR Himeji Station and Sanyo Himeji Station. The restaurant occupies three floors, with a casual bar-style ground floor, intimate table dining on the second floor, and a party room on the third.
chef_bio.philosophy: Casual Italian shokudo (dining hall) ethos: accessible price points, broad pizza selection, and an all-you-can-drink wine and cocktail menu.
chef_bio.specialty: Wood-fired Neapolitan pizza baked at 400–500°C in a Vesuvius-stone oven — roughly 40 varieties including a buffalo-mozzarella upgrade option.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All pizzas share a single wood-fired stone oven; wheat flour from dough will be present on the oven floor throughout service — cross-contamination for anyone avoiding gluten is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce or Japanese dashi-based elements identified in the Italian menu, but standard Italian sauces and dressings have not been confirmed allergen-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cheese, cured meats, and anchovies are used throughout the kitchen; vegan cross-contact risk is high and no vegan protocols were identified.

cultural_comfort.note: Standard local Italian bar with no English menu found; staff comfort with English is unknown, though a simple pizza order environment with a QR ordering system should be manageable.

places.name: 花そば・料理 ゆう (Hana Soba Ryori Yuu)
places.gf_detail: Soba noodles are buckwheat but the house tsuyu/dipping sauce uses a wheat-based soy sauce (kaeshi) aged in-house. Tempura batter contains wheat. Virtually all broth dishes contain both dashi and wheat-soy shoyu. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or separate fryer is mentioned anywhere. Celiacs should inquire directly and should treat all soba broth and dipping sauces as containing wheat unless confirmed otherwise.
places.vegan_detail: Almost every dish is built around dashi made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu; the house kaeshi (soy tare) is fish-based. The closest vegan-adjacent items are chilled tofu (冷奴), daikon salad, and tofu salad, but cross-contact with fish-based broth throughout the kitchen is very likely. Ask staff specifically; do not assume any soba broth dish is vegan.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30) daily; Dinner 17:30–22:00 (L.O. 21:30) except Thursday evenings closed
places.neighborhood: 西二階町商店街 (Nishi-Nikai-cho Shopping Arcade), near Sanyo-Himeji
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The proprietor is a second-generation master of the Hanshu Shizushizugama ceramic kiln. About 17 years ago he took over a site that had been a traditional Japanese clothing accessories shop for over 100 years and transformed it into a handmade soba restaurant, intending to keep J

chef_bio.background: The proprietor is a second-generation master of the Hanshu Shizushizugama ceramic kiln. About 17 years ago he took over a site that had been a traditional Japanese clothing accessories shop for over 100 years and transformed it into a handmade soba restaurant, intending to keep Japanese cultural traditions alive in the historic Nishi-Nikai-cho arcade. He hand-throws most of the ceramic tableware used in the restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Prioritising aroma and freshness (香りや打ちたてにこだわって); flour is chosen by fragrance, noodles are made twice daily, and the ceramic vessels are made by the same hands that pull the soba.
chef_bio.specialty: 手打ち蕎麦 — freshly hand-pulled buckwheat soba made every morning and evening from seasonally rotated flour sourced from Hokkaido, Fukui, and Hitachi; the 千姫御膳 (Senhime Gozen) set is the signature lunch

anecdotes.text: The owner participates in the neighbourhood 西二階町まつり festival and has cooked local Himeji speciality 華姫さわら (Spanish mackerel) live on stage for the community.

The house kaeshi (soy tare base for the dipping sauce) is aged for two months in-house, blended with Rishiri kombu and bonito stock — a labour-intensive process typical of high-end Tokyo soba-ya imported here to provincial Himeji.
The restaurant is listed in the Michelin Guide Hyogo and has received Five Stars Hyogo certification, rare recognition for a soba-ya outside the Kobe/Osaka axis.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free fryer or preparation area is mentioned in any source. Tempura (wheat batter) is a core menu item prepared in the same kitchen as all other dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house kaeshi (soy tare) is made with wheat-containing soy sauce, aged two months in-house, and used as the base for all dipping sauces and many broth dishes.
Virtually all soba broth and dipping sauces on the Tabelog menu listing explicitly contain dashi and soy sauce; wheat-free alternatives are not mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu is used across the entire menu including hot soups, noodle broths, oden, ankake sauces, and side dishes. Even tofu preparations (agedashi tofu, hot tofu) are served with dashi-based sauce.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or staff training for dietary restrictions is mentioned on the official website or any third-party review source reviewed. Phone reservation line is available (079-285-0888) and is the recommended channel for dietary queries.

positives.text: Soba noodles themselves are made from buckwheat flour — the base noodle is gluten-free grain — so the wheat risk is in sauces and accompaniments, not the noodle itself.
The chilled plain soba (もりそば) served with dipping sauce on the side allows some control over sauce exposure, though the sauce still contains wheat-soy kaeshi.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese soba-ya in a historic shopping arcade; menus and signage primarily in Japanese, though the refined setting and unhurried pace are welcoming — basic Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu will get you far.

places.name: 英洋軒 姫路駅前店 (Eiyoken Himeji Ekimae)
places.gf_detail: Gyoza wrappers contain wheat flour. Karaage (fried chicken) uses wheat-based batter. Banshu soy sauce (播州しょうゆ) is standard wheat-containing shoyu. No dedicated fryer, no gluten-free alternatives, and no allergen information is published. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small open kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu revolves around pork-filled gyoza, karaage (fried chicken), and egg omelette. No vegetarian or vegan dishes have been documented. Banshu (播州) soy sauce is listed as a key flavoring agent, and all signature items contain meat or egg. Vegan diners have nothing to eat here.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 11:30–21:00, closed Tue
places.neighborhood: Himeji Ekimae, Kyowa Shotengai (shopping arcade)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Eiyoken was founded in 1982 (Showa 57), originating from its main shop in Yobe, Himeji. The Ekimae (station-front) branch was opened in October 2012 by the founder's husband, and today the founding family runs four locations across Himeji. It is known for its stall-like standing-drinking style, tucked into a corner of the Kyowa Shotengai arcade.

roles: 家族経営（創業者一族が各店舗を分担）
chef_bio.background: 英洋軒の創業は1982年（昭和57年）、姫路市余部の本店が起源。 駅前店は2012年10月に創業者の夫が開いた支店で、現在は創業家による家族経営のもと姫路市内に4店舗を展開する。 共和商店街の一角に構える屋台のような立ち飲みスタイルが特徴。
chef_bio.philosophy: 安くてうまい、気軽に立ち寄れる街の中華立ち飲み屋として地元に根付いてきた。

anecdotes.text: 2017年に訪れた東京餃子通信の記者は「きれいな焼き色が付いた餃子で、ガツンとしたニンニク風味ではなく上品な味わい」と評している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small open kitchen with no separation between gyoza (wheat wrappers) and other items; cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Banshu soy sauce (播州しょうゆ) is explicitly cited as a core seasoning — this is standard wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes involve pork (gyoza filling), chicken (karaage), or egg (omelette); no plant-only cooking surfaces or ingredients documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen information is published and no allergy-handling procedures are documented. Staff communication is entirely in Japanese.

cultural_comfort.note: Compact standing bar with verbal ordering and handwritten bills — no English menu or English-speaking staff noted; one TripAdvisor reviewer flagged a staff interaction issue, so non-Japanese speakers should be prepared for a fully Japanese-language environment.

places.name: 森重 (Moriju)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant's core product is unagi no kabayaki, which relies on a house tare sauce containing wheat-based soy sauce (shoyu) that has been continuously replenished since the founding in 1848. Standard Japanese wheat shoyu is used throughout Japanese eel cuisine. No gluten-free alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocols are documented anywhere. The restaurant has never advertised GF options and no review or source mentions any accommodation. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: This is a dedicated eel and oyster restaurant. All signature dishes are animal-based; no plant-based mains or sides are listed. Dashi is fish-based (katsuobushi standard for Japanese restaurants of this type). There are no vegan options.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 11:30–14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 17:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:30); closed Sun & holidays. In July, unagi (eel) summer menu only; lunch sets may be suspended in Jul–Aug.
places.neighborhood: 魚町 (Uomachi) — Sanyo-Himeji Station area
places.notes: Founded in Kaei 1 (1848) by a man known as Jugesuke, who learned the eel trade in Edo's Fukagawa district — then the center of Tokyo's unagi culture — before returning west to open in Himeji under the patronage of clan officials. The restaurant has operated continuously for over

chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — originally near Sakamoto-cho, now at Uomachi 126
chef_bio.background: Founded in Kaei 1 (1848) by a man known as Jugesuke, who learned the eel trade in Edo's Fukagawa district — then the center of Tokyo's unagi culture — before returning west to open in Himeji under the patronage of clan officials. The restaurant has operated continuously for over 175 years across seven or more generations, making it one of the oldest dining establishments in Hyogo Prefecture.
chef_bio.philosophy: Faithfulness to Edo-period technique and an unbroken house sauce are the throughlines. The restaurant has resisted modernizing its core method across generations.
chef_bio.specialty: Unagi no kabayaki prepared in the Kansai style: grilled directly over charcoal without pre-steaming, producing a crispy, caramelized exterior and a soft interior. The house tare (glaze) has been continuously replenished — '継ぎ足し' — since the restaurant's founding, giving it extraordinary depth. Winter brings seasonal oyster dishes (late October to late March).

anecdotes.text: When Emperor Meiji visited Himeji, Moriju was designated an official imperial kitchen supplier and prepared eel for the imperial party — a distinction the family still references.
The restaurant's Meiji-era customer ledger reportedly includes the name of Sakamoto Ryoma, the celebrated Tosa samurai and modernizer.
Moriju was awarded a Bib Gourmand in the Michelin Guide Hyogo 2016, recognizing quality at a reasonable price point.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination controls. The kitchen handles wheat-based soy sauce throughout all cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare (glaze) used on all eel dishes is wheat-based soy sauce that has been continuously replenished since 1848. Wheat soy sauce is inherent to the product; no tamari or GF alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are animal-based (eel, oyster, fish dashi). There is no plant-based cooking track and therefore unavoidable animal cross-contact throughout.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling protocols or allergen menus are documented on any source including the official website, Tabelog, or Gnavi listings.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available and staff have been described as friendly and accommodating to foreign visitors, though the restaurant is conducted in Japanese.

places.name: 五臓六腑 (Gozouroppu)
places.gf_detail: Handmade soba is the signature dish, but no information on wheat ratio (二八 vs 十割) has been confirmed — standard izakaya soba often contains wheat. Wheat-based soy sauce is used throughout the kitchen (marinades, dipping sauces, oden). Fried items (e.g. chikuwa isobe-age) are on the menu with no dedicated GF fryer mentioned. No allergen menu or GF designation exists. Celiac-unsafe without direct confirmation from the owner.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu exists. Oden broth and most sauces rely on katsuobushi (bonito) dashi. Sashimi and some obanzai (daily small plates) may be animal-product-free in ingredients, but hidden fish stock is nearly certain across hot dishes. Ask the owner before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–03:00 (next day), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: 塩町 (Shiocho), Sanyo-Himeji Station area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Saito Eriko opened 五臓六腑 on May 9, 2024, realizing a long-held ambition to run an establishment dedicated to fine Japanese sake and handmade soba. The 14-tsubo room on the ground floor of the Hakuokan building in Shiocho sits in Himeji's late-night entertainment district, where Sa

chef_bio.background: Saito Eriko opened 五臓六腑 on May 9, 2024, realizing a long-held ambition to run an establishment dedicated to fine Japanese sake and handmade soba. The 14-tsubo room on the ground floor of the Hakuokan building in Shiocho sits in Himeji's late-night entertainment district, where Saito noted that fewer places had kept late hours after the pandemic — a gap she set out to fill.
chef_bio.philosophy: Centered on quality nihonshu — 150 labels including sought-after bottles like Juyondai and Niimata — paired with honest izakaya cooking: daily obanzai small plates, fresh sashimi, oden, and soba to finish.
chef_bio.specialty: 〆蕎麦 (finishing soba): hand-cut noodles whose flour origin rotates seasonally, served as a set (¥1,610) to close out an evening of sake drinking.

anecdotes.text: Saito positions herself as helping restore the vitality of Himeji's after-hours dining scene: 'After the pandemic, fewer establishments maintained late hours,' she said at opening, pointing to the 6 PM – 3 AM schedule as a deliberate commitment to night-owl diners.
A soba-making station is visible from the entrance, making the hand-cutting process part of the ambiance rather than hidden in a back kitchen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (chikuwa isobe-age) are listed on the menu with no mention of a dedicated fryer or GF protocols. Cross-contamination with wheat via shared frying oil is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen menu exists. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is expected throughout izakaya cooking — in sauces, dipping broths, marinades, and oden tare.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth and most cooked dishes at a traditional izakaya rely on katsuobushi (bonito flake) dashi. No plant-based broth option is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy menu, staff allergy training, or special dietary accommodation policy has been documented for this restaurant. The owner-operated format means direct conversation with Saito Eriko at the counter is the only route to clarifying ingredients.

positives.text: Counter seating directly in front of the owner allows for direct, personal conversation about ingredients — easier than at a larger izakaya chain.
Sashimi (fresh fish and local chicken tataki) are naturally wheat-free in their base form, though dipping sauces (ponzu, soy) typically contain wheat.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, late-night counter izakaya run by a solo owner with no English menu; basic Japanese will help, but regulars are welcomed warmly at the counter.

places.name: 小さな町のなかのレストラン bistro (Chiisana Machi no Naka no Restaurant Bistro)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options or allergen accommodation noted anywhere. Pasta is a centerpiece of the menu; bread (focaccia, baguette), and flour-heavy dishes are standard. No dedicated fryer information, no GF soy sauce, no staff allergen protocols mentioned. Wheat is pervasive throughout the menu. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct prior contact with the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu centers on charcuterie (prosciutto, pâté, rillettes), meat mains, pasta with egg-based or cream sauces, and cheese. No plant-based or vegan labeling found on any source.
places.hours_raw: Dinner Tue–Sun & holidays 17:30–23:00 (food LO 22:00, drinks LO 22:30). Cafe service (since Feb 2025) Mon, Wed, Thu, Fri 12:30–16:00. Closed Mon (dinner).
places.neighborhood: Tachimachi (north of Sanyo Himeji Stn)
places.notes: After years of training at an Italian restaurant in Tokyo, the owner-chef returned to his home Harima region and opened this bistro on April 12, 2023, on the 3rd floor of the Artemis Building in Tachimachi. He chose the bistro format to work at his own pace while making use of the skills he had learned, embodying a philosophy of "enjoying truly delicious food without pretension."

chef_bio.background: オーナーシェフは東京のイタリアンレストランで長年修業を積んだ後、故郷の播磨地域に戻り、2023年4月12日に立町のアルテミスビル3階でこのビストロをオープンした。 ビストロ形式を選んだのは、習得した料理の腕を活かしながら自分のペースで働けるスタイルを求めてのことで、「気取らず本当に美味しいものを楽しむ」という哲学を体現している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 手が届く価格で本格的な料理を。 シェフとゲストの距離感を大切にした親密なダイニング体験を提供する。 ワインは100種以上を揃え、ソムリエ的なセレクションが可能。
chef_bio.specialty: 焼きたてフィナンシェ（6個 ¥1,500、オーダーから15〜30分）と「貧乏人のパスタ 白トリュフの香り」（カチョエペペ風、¥1,580）

anecdotes.text: オーナーはフィナンシェのレシピを徹底的に研究し納得のいく配合にたどり着いた。注文を受けてから焼き上げる焼きたてのフィナンシェは、カフェ営業開始後も看板商品となっている。
2025年2月からカフェ営業（月・水〜金 12:30〜16:00）をスタート。ディナー専業だった隠れ家ビストロが昼間も開くようになり、スイーツやカフェ限定ドリンクを楽しめるようになった。

cultural_comfort.note: Japanese-language menus and interface; no English menu or staff language explicitly noted, though the cuisine style (Italian/French bistro) and a la carte ordering are internationally familiar formats.

places.name: il riccio（イルリッチョ） (il riccio)
places.gf_detail: Not gluten-free dedicated. Wheat is present: documented menu items include お麩のフライ (fried wheat gluten), organic carrot cake made with whole wheat flour, and vegan pancakes whose flour base is unspecified. Macrobiotic cooking routinely uses wheat-based products (fu, seitan, whole wheat). No GF certification or GF-specific menu found across any source. Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: 100% plant-based across the entire menu. No meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey used in any dish or sweet. Macrobiotic philosophy underpins the cooking. Brown rice or 5-grain rice served with set meals. Soy milk used for puddings and gelato. Menu rotates monthly (lunch) and seasonally (sweets). Natural food shop on the same premises stocks the same organic ingredients used in the kitchen.
places.hours_raw: Shop 10:30–18:30; Café 11:30–18:30 (last order 17:30, lunch last order 15:30). Closed Mondays and the 3rd Sunday of each month.
places.neighborhood: Gofukumachi (near Himeji Castle, 6–7 min walk from Sanyo-Himeji Stn)
places.notes: The business traces its roots to 1972, when the current owner's family opened a natural foods store in Himeji's Gofukucho shopping district. The present operator — the shop's third-generation owner and a qualified macrobiotic cooking instructor — relaunched the space as a plant-b

chef_bio.background: The business traces its roots to 1972, when the current owner's family opened a natural foods store in Himeji's Gofukucho shopping district. The present operator — the shop's third-generation owner and a qualified macrobiotic cooking instructor — relaunched the space as a plant-based café called il riccio in November 2019, grafting a dining room onto the existing retail operation.
chef_bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic principles guide every dish: organic, naturally farmed, or low-pesticide vegetables; seasonings free of synthetic additives. The owner's stated aim is to let visitors to Himeji Castle experience Japanese home-style vegan cuisine rather than defaulting to sandwiches or foreign food. The name 'il riccio' is Italian for hedgehog — a hedgehog figure marks the storefront.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal vegan Japanese-style set lunch (季節のランチ): a teishoku of rice (brown or 5-grain), miso soup, a main dish, and two or three sides, priced around ¥1,650–¥1,980. The main rotates monthly — past examples include chickpea curry croquette and soy-meat dishes.

anecdotes.text: The café grew directly out of the owner's macrobiotic cooking classes: students who attended the classes encouraged her to open a place where they could eat the food she taught. She recognised that Himeji, a major UNESCO World Heritage destination, had almost no vegan-friendly Japanese dining, and set out to fill that gap.

The interior features a handmade chandelier crafted from vintage cutlery, and the café uses grass straws for beverages — small details that reflect the broader commitment to low-impact, considered living.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about dedicated fryers or separation of gluten-containing and gluten-free preparation is available from any source reviewed. The kitchen uses wheat-containing ingredients (wheat gluten, whole wheat flour) alongside rice-based and vegetable dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce use is not documented in any source, but it is common in Japanese macrobiotic cooking. The restaurant avoids synthetic additives but makes no claim of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce. Assume wheat-based shoyu may be in use unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is 100% plant-based with no animal products; vegan cross-contact from kitchen operations is not a concern here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No formal allergy protocol is described in any source. For celiac or wheat-allergy guests, direct contact (079-288-2439 or Instagram @ilriccio.himeji.plantbasedcafe) is strongly advised before visiting.

positives.text: Entire menu is 100% free of all animal products — no hidden dashi, no fish stock, no dairy, no eggs, no honey — making it one of the most reliably vegan spots in Himeji.
Ingredients are sourced organically or from low-pesticide natural farming; seasonings contain no synthetic additives. The attached shop stocks all the same products used in the kitchen.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available; the owner specifically set out to serve international visitors coming to nearby Himeji Castle.

places.name: ローマピッツァと窯焼きイタリアン 姫路バル (Roma Pizza to Kamayaki Italian Himeji Baru)
places.gf_detail: Pizza is the core product — dough is made in-house with custom yeast, but wheat flour is the base. No gluten-free dough option is advertised or confirmed anywhere. Stone oven is shared with all pizzas, so cross-contamination from flour is near-certain. No dedicated fryer information found. Italian wine bar context: pasta and other wheat-based dishes likely present. Not safe for celiac without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options listed anywhere. Standard Italian bar fare — pizzas contain cheese and meat toppings by default; course menus include ham, shrimp, Caesar salad (likely anchovies/dairy). A plain margherita may be possible without cheese if requested, but nothing is confirmed. Hidden animal ingredients (dairy, meat) are the norm here. Contact before visiting.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Fri 13:00–23:30 (food L.O. 22:30, drink L.O. 23:00), Sat/Sun/holidays 11:30–23:30 (same L.O.), no regular closing day
places.neighborhood: 紺屋町・おみぞ筋商店街 (Sanyo Himeji Station area)
places.notes: 姫路バル is one of the shops within Himeji's Omizo-suji shopping arcade (おみぞ筋商店街), operated by 株式会社otto. The company runs multiple dining concepts under the otto brand. The restaurant opened as a Roman-style pizza and wine bar concept, positioning itself as a neighbourhood Italian ca

chef_bio.background: 姫路バル is one of the shops within Himeji's Omizo-suji shopping arcade (おみぞ筋商店街), operated by 株式会社otto. The company runs multiple dining concepts under the otto brand. The restaurant opened as a Roman-style pizza and wine bar concept, positioning itself as a neighbourhood Italian casual spot in the Sanyo Himeji station area.
chef_bio.philosophy: Affordable, handcrafted Roman pizza paired with a sommelier-curated wine list spanning over 50 labels from around the world, aimed at making wine and quality pizza an everyday pleasure in Himeji.
chef_bio.specialty: Roman-style (thin, crispy) stone-oven pizza — dough fermented in-house with a proprietary yeast blend and adjusted rise times for a distinctively crispy base. Individual pizzas start at ¥500 in-house.

anecdotes.text: The pizza dough is made entirely by hand, using the restaurant's own yeast culture and adjusted fermentation timing to produce the signature thin, crispy Roman-style crust baked in an authentic stone oven.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Pizza is the core product and all dough is wheat-based. A stone oven shared across all pizzas means ambient flour and gluten contact is unavoidable. No gluten-free crust option has been found.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard Italian bar menu — cheese, meat, and seafood are central to the menu. No vegan-safe section or plant-based alternatives confirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual Italian wine bar in a local shopping arcade; no English menu confirmed, but the casual bar format and simple pizza-focused menu make ordering manageable with basic Japanese or pointing.

places.name: ビストロ料理とワインのお店 NicolaS (Bistro Ryori to Wine no Mise NicolaS (Nicola)
places.gf_detail: This is a classic bistro. House-made bread (focaccia and French bread), pasta (tagliatelle, lasagna), quiche, and wheat-dusted sauces are core menu items. No gluten-free options are advertised anywhere. There is no dedicated fryer information, no wheat-free soy sauce, and no cross-contamination protocol documented. Diners with celiac disease should contact the restaurant before visiting — wheat is fundamental to the menu and the kitchen's prep style.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat, seafood, house-cured charcuterie, cheese, and butter-based French-Italian preparations. No vegan options are advertised or noted in any source. Hidden animal products are pervasive (butter, cream, stock, cured meats). Vegans should contact the chef directly before visiting — given the small-kitchen, solo-chef format, custom accommodations may be difficult.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); Fri–Sun & holidays lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 14:00), dinner 17:30–23:00 (L.O. 22:00); closed Monday
places.neighborhood: 駅前町 (Ekimae-cho) — Himeji Station Front
places.notes: The owner-chef, known by the surname Shinoda, ran an earlier bistro of the same name in Himeji's Nibancho district roughly twenty years before reopening. After leaving Himeji he worked as a head chef at a French restaurant in the Kanto region and was involved in food-product deve

chef_bio.background: The owner-chef, known by the surname Shinoda, ran an earlier bistro of the same name in Himeji's Nibancho district roughly twenty years before reopening. After leaving Himeji he worked as a head chef at a French restaurant in the Kanto region and was involved in food-product development at a manufacturer. He returned to his hometown determined to cook again, and opened this iteration of NicolaS on December 12, 2022 in the basement of Himeji Ekimae Dai-ichi Building.
chef_bio.philosophy: 丁寧に作る — meticulous preparation as a guiding principle. The chef blends French and Italian elements rather than adhering strictly to one tradition, and frames the restaurant as a casual wine-and-small-plates destination.
chef_bio.specialty: Roast Australian lamb; duck thigh confit (carried over from the original restaurant); pâté de campagne; house-made focaccia and French bread

anecdotes.text: The lamb roast draws repeat visitors specifically to reorder it — one blogger noted returning 'to eat the lamb again' as the driving motivation.

The chef engaged a solo diner in detailed conversation about the differences between French and Italian culinary technique during service, illustrating his passion for explaining the craft behind each dish.
When explaining his pâté de campagne, the chef noted that 'the size of the meat cuts and the cooking temperature differ with each establishment' — a detail that signals his attention to technique.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination protocols is documented in any source. The kitchen is run solo by one chef in a compact basement space, and house-made bread is prepared on-site, raising surface/utensil contamination concerns for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source mentions wheat-free soy sauce or any soy sauce substitution. French-Italian bistro cooking typically uses wheat-containing sauces, pasta, and bread — no gluten-free accommodations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Animal products (butter, cream, lard, stock, cured meats) are core to every menu category; vegan cross-contact risk is high and no plant-only prep area or labeling is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No reviewer or source mentions allergy awareness, allergen menus, or staff training. Diners with serious allergies should phone ahead (079-244-9082) and discuss needs directly with the chef.

positives.text: The restaurant is run by a single owner-chef who cooks to order in a small kitchen — this one-cook format means custom requests can be discussed directly with the decision-maker, rather than relying on front-of-house staff to relay information.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate 9-seat basement hideaway run solo by the owner-chef; English signage is unlikely, but the counter format invites natural interaction if you can manage basic Japanese.

places.name: Hey. (Hey.)
places.gf_detail: Primarily a drinks-and-sweets café. No gluten-free labeling, dedicated fryer information, or allergy-handling policy has been published anywhere. Sweets may contain wheat. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Ask staff directly before ordering any food item.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan labeling on the menu. Specialty drinks include lattes and a matcha affogato (likely contains milk). No dairy-free or plant-milk option is documented in any source. Ask about milk alternatives before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Sat 13:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); closed Mon–Fri & Sun
places.neighborhood: 二階町商店街 (Nikaichō Shopping Street), Himeji
places.notes: Hey. opened on June 27, 2026 inside the ANGELO二階町 building on Nikaichō Shopping Street, about 464m from Sanyo-Himeji Station. It operates on Saturdays only, positioning itself as a relaxed evening destination in the covered arcade near Himeji Castle.

chef_bio.background: Hey. opened on June 27, 2026 inside the ANGELO二階町 building on Nikaichō Shopping Street, about 464m from Sanyo-Himeji Station. It operates on Saturdays only, positioning itself as a relaxed evening destination in the covered arcade near Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: A calm, unhurried café atmosphere described by early visitors as a hidden-gem retreat within a busy shopping street.
chef_bio.specialty: MELT LATTE — a creamy espresso-based latte described by early visitors as the signature drink, alongside a cheese latte and matcha affogato.

anecdotes.text: An early reviewer noted that the cheese latte's richness paired excellently with the coffee flavor, and praised the young staff as energetic, bright, and courteous.

cultural_comfort.note: A newly opened, youth-staffed neighborhood café with no English signage noted; staff described as energetic and friendly, but no English-language service confirmed.

places.name: 大衆酒場 フレンチマン 姫路店 (Taishuu Sakaba Frenchman Himeji)
places.gf_detail: As a French izakaya, wheat is widely used in pasta, sandwiches, and sauces. Whether soy sauce is used is unknown, but because the food is French-based, the risk from wheat flour (roux, pasta dough, breadcrumbs) is higher than from soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodation is stated. Presence of a dedicated fryer is also unknown. Celiacs are strongly advised to call ahead (079-280-4555). Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on animal products such as foie gras, roast beef, sea urchin, and salmon roe. There are some small vegetable dishes, but French sauces and butter are likely to contain animal ingredients. No vegan accommodation is stated. Direct confirmation when ordering is required. Assessment: not reliably vegan.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–22:00 (food LO 21:00, drinks LO 21:30), Morning Frenchman (weekdays 10:00–13:00)
places.neighborhood: Himeji Ekimae, Grand Festa underground mall
places.notes: Operated by Rakuchin Ltd. (Nakagyo-ku, Kyoto). Founder Tetsuya Watanabe went independent at age 26 and has over 20 years of izakaya management experience in Kyoto. After trying about 20 formats, he identified a market gap—"French has not yet been popularized for the masses"—and pioneered a new French x mass-market izakaya genre. Executive chef Kiyotaka Yoshimaru devised the menu. The "Taishuu Sakaba Frenchman" concept launched in 2020 inside Shin-Osaka Station, and the Himeji location opened on March 14, 2025, as the chain's second in Hyogo Prefecture after Kobe Sannomiya.

roles: 運営会社: 有限会社らくちん
総料理長: 吉丸清孝
chef_bio.origin: 京都発祥。2015年JR京都駅近くに「ビストロ フレンチマン」を開業し、2020年新大阪店から大衆酒場業態に発展。
chef_bio.background: 有限会社らくちん（京都市中京区）が運営。 代表の渡辺哲也氏は26歳で独立し、京都で20年以上居酒屋経営を経験。 約20業態を試みた末、「フレンチはまだ大衆化されていない」という市場の空白を見つけ、フレンチ×大衆酒場という新ジャンルを開拓。 総料理長・吉丸清孝氏がメニューを考案。 2020年に新大阪駅構内から「大衆酒場 フレンチマン」業態をスタートし、姫路店は2025年3月14日に兵庫県で神戸三宮に次ぐ2店舗目としてオープン。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「フレンチを日常使いに」をコンセプトに、フレンチシェフ考案の本格小皿料理や串焼きをリーズナブルに提供。 コース不要でアラカルト注文可能。
chef_bio.specialty: フォアグラとローストビーフ（¥2,178）、ウニとイクラの冷製パスタ（¥2,728）、ホールチーズクリームパスタ（¥880）

anecdotes.text: 姫路店限定の「朝フレンチマン」（平日10:00–13:00、¥770）はホットサンド＋サラダ＋ドリンク10種類から選択。施設の開店時間に合わせた姫路限定モーニングとして話題になった。
新大阪の旗艦店（7坪・16席）は月商650万円を達成。大阪駅前第3ビル店（10坪・19席）は月商1,100万円（坪商100万円超）を記録し、渋谷サクラステージ店では2024年11月に月商1,300万円を突破した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: パスタ（リングイネ）、ホットサンド（朝メニュー）など小麦を含む料理が多数あり、調理場での交差汚染リスクが高いと推定される。公式情報なし。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: フレンチベースのため醤油使用は限定的と思われるが、和風アレンジ料理（串焼き等）でのたれ・醤油使用の有無は未確認。

vegan_cross_contact.text: フォアグラ・バター・生クリームなど動物性食材がメニューの中核を担っており、ヴィーガン対応の区別は困難と推定。公式のアレルギー対応情報なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応に関する公式情報・スタッフ訓練の案内は取材記事・公式サイト・食べログのいずれにも記載がない。来店前に電話（079-280-4555）での確認を推奨。

positives.text: 野菜を活かした小皿料理が複数あり、野菜中心の食事を好む方には選択肢が存在する。

cultural_comfort.note: カウンター中心の活気ある居酒屋スタイルで、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの情報はなし。日本語のみで対応する可能性が高い。

places.name: おだしとワインとお料理と。motto (Odashi to Wine to Oryouri to. Motto)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu exists. The signature ebisoba comes in shoyu (soy sauce) and miso versions — both almost certainly contain wheat. Wonton wrappers and shrimp fritters are fried toppings with wheat. The oden uses bonito broth but wheat-containing soy sauce is likely in the base. The obanzai small plates (egg dashi omelette, roasted beef, vegetable sides) may have lower wheat exposure but no kitchen gluten protocols are documented. Cross-contamination risk is unquantified. Celiac diners should contact the restaurant directly before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Dashi is the core identity of the restaurant — the shrimp-broth ramen (ebisoba), bonito-broth oden (洋風おでん), chashu pork ramen toppings, cured ham plates, and Kobe/Tajima beef dishes all make vegan eating effectively impossible here. No plant-based or vegan options are documented on any source.
places.neighborhood: 駅前町 (Ekimae-cho), Miyuki-dori arcade, ~140m from JR Himeji S
places.cuisine: Dashi-based Japanese dining bar, wine bar
places.notes: Opened March 22, 2024 by Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a Himeji institution founded in 1888 that is credited with inventing ekisoba (Japan's original station noodles served on the Himeji station platform) and an early Makunouchi bento. This restaurant represents a new dining concept

chef_bio.background: Opened March 22, 2024 by Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a Himeji institution founded in 1888 that is credited with inventing ekisoba (Japan's original station noodles served on the Himeji station platform) and an early Makunouchi bento. This restaurant represents a new dining concept from the company — its first attempt at a wine-forward casual dining bar beyond its railway food heritage.
chef_bio.philosophy: Japanese hospitality expressed through dashi: the kitchen frames every dish around carefully prepared broth, pairing it with a curated selection of domestic natural wines chosen with input from Tatsumi Takuo, an actor and chairman of the Japan Wine Lovers Association. The concept was born from conversations between Maneki Foods president Takeda Noriotaka and Tatsumi — a desire to create a space where younger diners could enjoy wine without formality.
chef_bio.specialty: Ebisoba — shrimp ramen in three broth styles (shoyu, shio, miso) using broth extracted from whole shrimp shells, topped with fried wonton, chashu, and a spiced shrimp fritter

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies the former site of a jewelry store on Miyuki-dori and was designed in terracotta and earthy brown tones to evoke the orange color of the signature shrimp broth.
Maneki Foods, the operator, has been making ekisoba at Himeji Station since 1949 and is widely credited as the originator of the station-noodle format now found at railways across Japan.
The wine list was personally curated by actor and wine advocate Tatsumi Takuo, featuring eight domestic natural wines with seasonal rotation and rare bottles offered at accessible prices.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No kitchen gluten protocols or dedicated preparation areas are documented in any source. The menu includes fried items (shrimp fritters, fried wontons) alongside non-fried dishes, suggesting shared frying or preparation areas are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Shoyu (soy sauce) versions of the ebisoba and likely the oden broth base will contain wheat. Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat unless specifically labeled tamari or GF.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shrimp broth, bonito dashi, chashu pork, ham, and beef dishes are core to the menu; plant-based cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable in this kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling procedures, allergen menus, or staff training documentation appear on any source. Phone reservations are accepted, which provides an opportunity to discuss dietary needs before visiting: 079-263-7388.

positives.text: The restaurant is entirely non-smoking, which reduces one environmental irritant for sensitive diners.

cultural_comfort.note: A corporate-backed Himeji dining bar near the station with menu cards and a relaxed casual atmosphere; minimal English expected but staff are accustomed to tourists visiting the area.

places.name: 洋食グリル天平 (Yoshoku Grill Tenpei)
