places.gf_detail: Yoshoku is structurally wheat-heavy: the signature demi-glace sauce is flour-thickened, Hamburg steak typically contains breadcrumbs, tonkatsu is panko-breaded, omelette rice uses soy sauce containing wheat. No gluten-free menu, allergen chart, or dedicated preparation protocol is mentioned on any source — Tabelog, the official site, the tourism board listing, or blog coverage. Cross-contamination risk is high in a 16-seat kitchen with no documented GF workflow. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu revolves around meat — the signature Hamburg steak and beef cutlet are the house pillars, with demi-glace sauce (meat-based), egg-wrapped omelette rice, and tonkatsu. No plant-based or vegan dishes identified on any source. Hidden animal products are pervasive: demi-glace sauce is made with bone stock, egg is used throughout, butter appears in bread service.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 11:30–14:30 / 17:00–21:00; closed Tue and the 3rd Wed of each month
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, Otemae Dai-1 Building (Otemae-dori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The Tenpei name traces back to a post-war izakaya opened by Chef Seki's grandparents. That establishment evolved into Kappo Tenpei before Chef Seki transformed it into Grill TENPEI in 2010, anchoring the family culinary lineage in Western-style cooking.

chef_bio.background: The Tenpei name traces back to a post-war izakaya opened by Chef Seki's grandparents. That establishment evolved into Kappo Tenpei before Chef Seki transformed it into Grill TENPEI in 2010, anchoring the family culinary lineage in Western-style cooking.
chef_bio.philosophy: Chef Seki describes the restaurant as the place he never had growing up in a restaurant family: a spot where three generations can sit down together over a proper meal. The name 'Tenpei' also reflects his personal ethos — meeting the world with flat, unresisting openness and gratitude for nature's bounty. He sources pesticide-free local vegetables, grows some produce himself, and uses Shiga Koshihikari rice.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji Beef Juice Hamburg — a hand-kneaded patty paired with the house three-day demi-glace sauce, designed to release a rush of juices on the first cut.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant bills itself as the world's closest Western restaurant to Himeji Castle — roughly 300 steps from the keep — a distinction that has become a defining part of its identity.
Since 2018, Chef Seki runs a children's food program (子ども食堂) out of the restaurant, accepting donations so local children can share a proper meal and company.
The three-day demi-glace sauce — the foundation of nearly every dish — is made entirely in-house and has been described by a diner as 'rich and deep, worth every minute of the wait.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF fryer or preparation area is documented. The kitchen produces breaded tonkatsu (panko) and flour-thickened demi-glace simultaneously — cross-contamination is an inherent and unaddressed risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is expected in yoshoku cooking, including omelette rice and sauce bases. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat stock, butter, egg, and dairy appear across the entire menu. No plant-based station or vegan workflow is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, staff allergy training, or dietary accommodation protocol is mentioned on Tabelog, the official website, the tourism board listing, or any blog review. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead: 079-280-4697.

positives.text: Chef Seki emphasises minimal use of chemical seasonings and additives, and sources pesticide-free local vegetables — a cleaner ingredient baseline than many casual yoshoku restaurants.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 16-seat counter-and-table shop run by a local family; no evidence of English menus or staff — best suited to diners comfortable ordering in Japanese.

places.name: cafe&izakaya ふぁぶｰる (Cafe & Izakaya Faveur)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen relies heavily on wheat: meunière uses wheat flour coating, katsu (salmon rare cutlet) is breaded, karaage and chicken wings are typically soy-sauce marinated and wheat-floured, and the menu includes pizza. Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is almost certainly used throughout. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodations are mentioned on any platform. This is an unsafe environment for celiac diners without direct staff consultation — and even then, cross-contamination risk is high given the menu style.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat and seafood — wagyu carpaccio, salmon katsu, sea bream meunière, roast pork, spiced chicken wings, and karaage. No plant-based or vegan alternatives appear on any published menu or course listing. Vegans should not expect accommodation here.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi / Otemae-dori
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened in November 2025 on Ōtemae-dōri, the main boulevard linking Himeji Station to Himeji Castle. The name appears to derive from French — the Instagram handle is @faveur_himeji — suggesting a French-bistro inspiration layered onto a Japanese izakaya format.

chef_bio.background: Opened in November 2025 on Ōtemae-dōri, the main boulevard linking Himeji Station to Himeji Castle. The name appears to derive from French — the Instagram handle is @faveur_himeji — suggesting a French-bistro inspiration layered onto a Japanese izakaya format.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept blends daytime café dining with evening izakaya service, leaning on French bistro technique applied to fresh seafood and selected meats.
chef_bio.specialty: Salmon rare cutlet (サーモンのレアカツ) and wagyu seared carpaccio are highlighted signature dishes across multiple platforms; sea bream meunière is consistently praised in early reviews.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares breaded katsu, meunière (wheat-flour coated), karaage, and pizza — all high-gluten preparations. No information on fryer separation or allergen protocols has been published anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is almost certainly used in marinades and seasoning throughout the menu; no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned on any platform.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built on meat and seafood with no plant-based dishes; animal-derived ingredients including dashi, meat stocks, and dairy (Basque cheesecake) are core to the cooking.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling policy, or staff dietary-accommodation language appears on Tabelog, HotPepper, Gurunavi, or Hitosara. Diners with serious allergies should call ahead: 050-8885-9357.

cultural_comfort.note: A small independent spot on the tourist-friendly Ōtemae-dōri corridor between Himeji Station and the castle; basic Japanese or pointing at menus should work, but no English menu or staff language ability has been documented.

places.name: 手打蕎麦処 やえもん (Tedauchi Sobadokoro Yaemon)
places.gf_detail: Hand-made soba is commonly a blend of buckwheat flour and wheat flour, and there is no mention of gluten-free accommodation. The dipping broth (kaeshi) uses wheat-containing soy sauce, and tempura uses a wheat-flour batter. The dashi appears to be bonito-based (possibly wheat-free, but unconfirmed). Assessment: not suitable for celiac/wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The dashi is presumed to be bonito-flake based. The menu features many animal ingredients such as tempura, tempura rice bowls, pork-cutlet rice bowls, shrimp, and scallops. No vegan accommodation is noted. Assessment: not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sun & holidays 11:00–20:30 (LO 20:00), closed Mon. Sources differ on closing time (20:30–22:00). Best to confirm the latest via Instagram @yaemon0301.
places.neighborhood: Around Sanyo Himeji Stn (Konyamachi, Omizosuji Shotengai arcade)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened March 1, 2017, in the Omizosuji Shotengai arcade in Himeji. The owner trained at a soba shop in Izushi and went independent after about 20 years of soba-making experience. He renovated a former cafe, opening with the wish to "become a local shop loved by both office workers and families."

chef_bio.background: 2017年3月1日、姫路のおみぞ筋商店街に開店。 オーナーは出石（いずし）の蕎麦店で修業を積んだ経歴を持ち、約20年の蕎麦打ち経験を経て独立した。 カフェ跡地を改装し、「サラリーマンにも家族にも愛される地元の店になりたい」との思いで開業した。
chef_bio.philosophy: 麺と出汁にこだわった本格的な蕎麦。 毎日手打ちし、大きな窓越しに蕎麦打ちの姿を通りから見せることで、丁寧な仕事を伝える。
chef_bio.specialty: 手打ち蕎麦・サクサク天ぷら・自家製わらび餅

anecdotes.text: 開店以来ランチタイムは満席になるほどの人気。8年経った2025年現在も昼時は行列ができる
開業時からの人気デザート「自家製わらび餅」は食事後に提供され、多くのリピーターに愛されている

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテン交差汚染に関する情報は一切公表されていない。手打ちそばに小麦粉が含まれ、天ぷら（小麦衣）も同一厨房で調理されるため、セリアック患者への対応は期待できない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: そばつゆの返しには一般的に小麦を含む醤油が使用されると推定されるが、店舗から明示的な確認情報はなし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 出汁はかつお節ベースが標準的。ヴィーガン向け出汁への切り替えや分離調理に関する情報はなし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応・グルテンフリー対応に関するスタッフ訓練や情報開示は確認できていない。事前に電話（079-281-9555）で確認を推奨。

cultural_comfort.note: おみぞ筋商店街の地元向け蕎麦屋。英語メニューや英語対応スタッフの情報はなく、日本語のみの対応と思われる。

places.name: クラフトジンとおでん専門店 maruya (Craft Gin to Oden Senmonten Maruya)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims made. Oden is served with ginger soy sauce (shoga joyu), which is standard wheat-containing shoyu — a direct gluten source for celiacs. Dashi uses bonito and tuna flakes (inherently GF) but the shared broth pot and condiments present cross-contamination risk. No dedicated fryer noted (oden is simmered, not fried). Celiac diners should call ahead; safe eating here is not confirmed.
places.vegan_detail: Oden dashi is made from Rausu kombu (kelp), bonito flakes, and tuna flakes — not vegan. The ginger soy sauce served alongside is likely wheat-containing standard shoyu. Vegetable oden pieces (daikon, konnyaku, tofu) may exist but are cooked in the same fish-based broth. No vegan accommodation is mentioned anywhere; ask staff before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu & Sun 17:30–24:00, Fri & Sat 17:30–01:00 (next day), closed Mon. Also closed one Tuesday per month.
places.neighborhood: 立町 (Tatemachi), 339m from Sanyo Himeji Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kotani spent 15 years as a bartender at an English pub in Himeji before opening maruya in October 2023, fulfilling a long-held dream to combine his two passions.

chef_bio.background: Kotani spent 15 years as a bartender at an English pub in Himeji before opening maruya in October 2023, fulfilling a long-held dream to combine his two passions.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kotani draws a philosophical parallel between oden and craft gin: 'Oden varies by region, household, and broth; craft gin creates infinite flavors through botanicals. Both are undefined and free — they share common ground. ' He aims for Japan's finest dashi-driven oden.
chef_bio.specialty: Craft gin paired with Himeji-style oden simmered in a dashi of Rausu kombu, bonito, and tuna — served with ginger soy sauce in the local Himeji tradition.

anecdotes.text: A regular at the counter mistakenly called Kotani by the wrong surname — triggering collective laughter among the other guests, a moment that captures the relaxed, locals-bar warmth of the space.
Kotani stocks around 45 varieties of domestic craft gin and offers an all-you-can-drink plan featuring 40 of them for ¥4,000 — an unusual proposition in a city better known for sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shared oden pot simmers all ingredients together; no separate preparation for allergen-free orders is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden is served with ginger soy sauce (shoga joyu), which is standard wheat-containing shoyu and a direct gluten source. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi broth is made with Rausu kombu, bonito flakes, and tuna flakes. All oden items, including vegetables, are cooked in this fish-based broth — unavoidable cross-contact for vegans.

positives.text: Oden is a simmered dish with no deep-frying involved, eliminating fryer cross-contamination as a concern.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, intimate 7-seat counter bar run by a solo owner; Japanese is the working language and the setting is local izakaya-style, though the casual counter atmosphere is approachable for non-Japanese speakers willing to point and gesture.

places.name: cafe コトナキ (Cafe Kotonaki)
places.gf_detail: Multiple sources note that most items are gluten-free or GF-adaptable: rice-flour-based sweets, multigrain rice sets, and vegan taco rice are cited as GF-friendly. However, no dedicated GF certification exists and no source explicitly confirms wheat-free soy sauce or a dedicated GF preparation zone. Cross-contamination protocols are not documented anywhere. The café is a tiny 9-seat solo-operated kitchen, making strict separation difficult to verify. Celiacs should contact the café directly before visiting — phone 090-9087-0734 or Instagram @cafe_kotonaki.
places.vegan_detail: All lunch sets and muffins are 100% vegan; no animal-derived ingredients used in any menu item. Sweets use rice flour and okara (soy pulp) in place of wheat flour and eggs; almond powder is deliberately excluded to accommodate nut allergies. Soy milk is used in lattes. The kitchen can prepare dishes gokunin-nuki (五葷抜き — without garlic, onions, chives, pickled rakkyo, and shallots) on request, making items suitable for Buddhist-style vegan diners as well. No hidden dashi, katsuo, or bonito noted in any source.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu 11:00–20:00; Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00–22:00; closed Wed (open if a holiday)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (Otemae-dori)
places.notes: Iwasaki Kano honed her plant-based cooking skills at TEEDA CAFE (ティーダカフェ), a cave-themed café near Eiga-bo Station in Himeji, where she handled both savory cooking and pastry. On July 13, 2025, she opened コトナキ solo on the second floor of the 姫小路 building — a 70-year-old renovated

chef_bio.background: Iwasaki Kano honed her plant-based cooking skills at TEEDA CAFE (ティーダカフェ), a cave-themed café near Eiga-bo Station in Himeji, where she handled both savory cooking and pastry. On July 13, 2025, she opened コトナキ solo on the second floor of the 姫小路 building — a 70-year-old renovated wooden structure steps from Himeji Castle on Otemae-dori.
chef_bio.philosophy: The name derives from the Japanese phrase 事なきを得る (kotonaki wo eru) — 'to come through without incident, to be safe. ' Iwasaki's vision is a calm refuge where people of any background — vegan, allergy-affected, health-conscious, or simply curious — can settle in and breathe. She runs the space alone, savoring conversations with travelers from around the world.
chef_bio.specialty: Vegan taco rice (¥1,500) — spiced mung beans and soy protein over multigrain brown rice with housemade salsa; and daily-rotation vegan muffins made with rice flour and okara.

anecdotes.text: Iwasaki deliberately excludes almond powder from her baked goods so that guests with nut allergies can eat freely alongside vegan and allergy-conscious diners.
The glassware used for the gateau au chocolat is made by local Himeji artist Toki Kurogawa, giving each dessert a handcrafted, gallery-like presentation.
At opening, the café forged a cross-venue pairing partnership with nearby KUUHAKU COFFEE HIMEJI, so guests can bring drinks between the two spots.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF preparation zone or cross-contamination protocols have been documented in any published source. The kitchen is a compact solo-operated space; separation cannot be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms whether wheat-free tamari or GF soy sauce is used. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; celiacs should ask explicitly before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All menu items are stated to be 100% plant-based; no animal products are used in the kitchen, so vegan cross-contact risk appears negligible based on the café's stated concept.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner is allergy-aware and deliberately excludes almond powder from sweets; she also accommodates gokunin-nuki (five-pungent-free) requests. However, formal allergen-handling procedures are not documented.

positives.text: Entire menu is 100% plant-based — no fish, meat, dairy, or eggs used anywhere in the kitchen, eliminating the main vegan cross-contact vectors.
Sweets are made with rice flour and okara instead of wheat flour, meaning several dessert items are naturally gluten-free in their base formulation.
English menu available; owner is communicative with foreign guests, making dietary questions easier to raise.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner actively engages international visitors; an English menu is available and the café sits steps from Himeji Castle, welcoming foreign tourists by design.

places.name: 串揚げ きっこう (Kushiage Kikkou)
places.gf_detail: Kushiage is by definition coated in wheat-based panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried; gluten is the core technique. Standard dipping sauces and marinades will contain wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free accommodations, dedicated fryer, or allergen information is mentioned on the official website, Tabelog, or any review source. Not safe for celiac or serious gluten intolerance.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is centered on deep-fried skewers of meat, seafood, and vegetables, all cooked in the same fryer with panko breadcrumbs. No vegan or vegetarian options are listed or suggested anywhere on the menu or official site. Fish carpaccio appears in every course. Hidden dashi in sauces is also probable.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sat 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), Sun & holidays 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30), closed Tue
places.neighborhood: Around Sanyo-Himeji Stn (1–3 min walk from station)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established hidden-gem kushiage specialist in central Himeji, located on the first floor of a small building a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The restaurant is run by a couple and has been operating for well over a decade, earning loyal regulars who return year afte

chef_bio.background: A long-established hidden-gem kushiage specialist in central Himeji, located on the first floor of a small building a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The restaurant is run by a couple and has been operating for well over a decade, earning loyal regulars who return year after year. It describes itself as a pioneer of creative kushiage (創作串揚げ) in the area.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes seasonal ingredients and dishes that can only be found here, pairing the skewers with wines, plum liqueurs, and shochu chosen by the proprietor.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal kushiage skewers — meat, seafood, and vegetables coated in light panko and deep-fried, served in courses of 10–20 pieces. The menu rotates quarterly to follow the seasons.

anecdotes.text: One long-time guest wrote that they first visited just before starting their own business, meaning they have been coming for over a decade — a testament to the restaurant's consistency and warmth.
Reviewers describe the proprietors as a 'pleasant couple' who create a relaxed atmosphere where guests can truly unwind.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushiage items are coated in panko (wheat breadcrumbs) and fried in a shared fryer. Cross-contamination with wheat is inherent and unavoidable across the entire menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce specifications are listed. Standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is virtually certain to be used in marinades and dipping sauces; no tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items are cooked in the same fryer as meat and seafood. No separation of animal products from vegetables is indicated.

cultural_comfort.note: Small local restaurant run by a couple with no English menu or signage evident; basic Japanese helpful for ordering.

places.name: il riccio (il riccio (Iru Riccho))
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free status. The café explicitly states "our foods are not specified as allergy-free" and asks customers with allergies to contact staff in advance. Miso and soy-based proteins appear prominently in the menu; conventional Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is likely used unless additive-free tamari is substituted — this has not been confirmed. Some individual items such as tofu and rice flour desserts could be GF by ingredient, but no dedicated fryer information is available and cross-contamination protocols are not disclosed. Celiac diners should call ahead before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: Fully plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey used anywhere on the menu. All ingredients are organic, naturally farmed, or low-pesticide vegetables with additive-free seasonings sourced through the on-site natural food shop. Menu rotates monthly with seasonal macrobiotic lunch sets, sweets, and plant-based beverages including dandelion coffee, brown rice coffee, and grain drinks.
places.hours_raw: Café 11:30–18:30 (last order 17:30); Shop 10:30–18:30. Closed Mondays and the 3rd Sunday of each month. Lunch last order 15:30.
places.neighborhood: 呉服町 (Gofukucho)
places.notes: The roots of il riccio stretch back to 1972, when the owner's predecessor natural food shop first opened on Miyuki-dori (みゆき通り) in central Himeji — operating for roughly 48 years before relocating. In November 2019, the business moved to Gofukucho and relaunched as 'Plant-Based C

chef_bio.background: The roots of il riccio stretch back to 1972, when the owner's predecessor natural food shop first opened on Miyuki-dori (みゆき通り) in central Himeji — operating for roughly 48 years before relocating. In November 2019, the business moved to Gofukucho and relaunched as 'Plant-Based Café + Natural Food Shop il riccio,' combining the long-running retail side with a new café component. The current owner is a certified macrobiotic cooking instructor who runs regular cooking classes alongside the café.
chef_bio.philosophy: The café embeds macrobiotics into every decision: source organic or naturally farmed local vegetables, use additive-free seasonings from the on-site shop, avoid all animal products, and let food support health and beauty from within. The name 'il riccio' — Italian for hedgehog — was chosen because hedgehogs are a European lucky symbol, and the owner hopes that each person who discovers macrobiotics feels that same sense of good fortune. The secondary meaning of 'riccio' (curly hair) also appealed personally to the owner.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal one-soup-three-sides (一汁三菜) macrobiotic lunch sets built around organic Hyogo rice, handmade miso, and rotating plant-based mains such as soy-meat cutlet or taro gratin; monthly-changing vegan sweets including tofu and rice flour gateau chocolat.

anecdotes.text: The name 'il riccio' (Italian for hedgehog) was chosen deliberately: the owner has long loved hedgehogs and discovered they are considered lucky in European culture. The hope was that anyone who encounters macrobiotics through the shop would feel that same lucky sensation. The Italian language was chosen because the owner loves Italy and the sound of its words — and 'riccio' also means curly hair, which matched the owner's own super-curly hair.
The hedgehog theme carries through to the entrance, where a hedgehog figurine greets visitors at the door of the European-style building in Gofukucho.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is published about cross-contamination controls, shared equipment, or dedicated preparation areas for allergen-free cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Miso (soy-based, handmade) and soy-derived protein products are used extensively. The specific type of soy sauce used — whether wheat-containing shoyu or wheat-free tamari — has not been disclosed in any published source. Macrobiotic philosophy favours traditionally brewed condiments, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No animal products are used anywhere in the café kitchen; the entire menu is 100% plant-based, so vegan cross-contact from cooking is not a concern here.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The café explicitly states: 'Please note that our foods are not specified as allergy-free. Please let our staff know in advance if you have any allergies.' Customers with allergies are asked to contact the café before visiting.

positives.text: Entire menu is 100% plant-based with no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey — eliminating vegan cross-contact risk entirely.
All seasonings and ingredients are sourced from the on-site natural food shop and are certified organic, naturally farmed, or low-pesticide with no synthetic additives.
Some individual items — such as the tofu and rice flour gateau chocolat — are made with inherently gluten-free ingredients, though no GF certification or dedicated preparation claim is made.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu version is available and the café attracts health-conscious visitors including foreign tourists near Himeji Castle, but staff communication is primarily Japanese.

places.name: 姫路氷菓店 (Himeji Hyokaten)
places.gf_detail: Plain shaved ice with fruit syrup is naturally wheat-free. Kinako (roasted soy) and mochi toppings are glutinous-rice-based and wheat-free in isolation, but cross-contamination in a small kitchen is unknown. No allergen disclosure is published, no certified GF claim exists. Soy sauce is not used in kakigori preparation so wheat-in-shoyu is not a concern here. Guests with celiac disease should confirm specific toppings and kitchen handling before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The base shaved ice (Shinshu deep-spring water) is vegan. Fruit-syrup items such as the citrus blend appear dairy-free, but several signature items include condensed milk (練乳), whipped cream, or mochi sauce. No dedicated vegan labeling; hidden dashi is not a concern in a kakigori shop, but dairy presence in popular items is real. Confirm individual flavors on the day.
places.neighborhood: Konyamachi / Omizosuji shopping street — 5 min walk from Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened April 6, 2024, in a renovated former izakaya space (approximately 6 tsubo) in the Omizo-suji shopping arcade, a covered street running north from Himeji Station. The shop went on hiatus and re-opened June 5, 2026.

chef_bio.background: Opened April 6, 2024, in a renovated former izakaya space (approximately 6 tsubo) in the Omizo-suji shopping arcade, a covered street running north from Himeji Station. The shop went on hiatus and re-opened June 5, 2026.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kakigori as a year-round dessert, not a summer-only treat. The proprietor selected the space for its open-ceiling traditional atmosphere and maintains razor-sharp blades to produce ultra-thin ice that melts without causing a head-freeze sensation. Pickled vegetables are served alongside as a palate cleanser, following the salt-with-sweet principle. Future plans include expanding into fruit sandwiches and tea.
chef_bio.specialty: Fluffy kakigori shaved from ultra-high-purity Shinshu deep-groundwater ice (drawn from 250 m depth), dressed with unheated, handmade fresh-fruit syrups.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor uses Shinshu deep-spring natural water for its high transparency and clean flavor, shaving it paper-thin so guests can taste the syrup without the ice overwhelming it.
Instagram followers receive a ¥100 discount — a small gesture that reflects the shop's community-first approach in the local arcade.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen or cross-contamination information is published by the shop. Kitchen size (approximately 6 tsubo total) means separation may be limited.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is not used in kakigori preparation, so wheat-via-shoyu is not a relevant risk for this format.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Condensed milk and whipped cream are used in several items and prepared in the same small counter kitchen; dairy cross-contact cannot be ruled out for fruit-only orders.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information on staff allergy-awareness training or handling protocols has been published. The shop has not advertised any allergen accommodation service.

positives.text: Shaved ice with pure fruit syrup (no dairy topping) is a simple, naturally wheat-free preparation that avoids most common gluten sources.
All syrups are prepared without heat (non-heated raw syrup) from fresh fruit, with no mention of added thickeners or wheat-containing stabilizers.

cultural_comfort.note: Small 8-seat counter in a local shopping arcade; menu and signage appear to be Japanese only, with no evidence of English-language staff or materials.

places.name: 炭火焼 淡州 (Sumibiyaki Danshu)
places.gf_detail: Standard izakaya kitchen: soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used in virtually all preparations — marinades, tare for yakitori, dipping sauces, and dashi bases. Tempura is on the menu (battered with wheat flour). There is no GF claim anywhere. Plain sashimi (sashimi only, no sauce) is the most plausible lower-risk item, but cross-contamination from the shared charcoal grill and kitchen surfaces cannot be ruled out. Celiacs should not eat here without direct staff communication in Japanese.
places.vegan_detail: This is a seafood and grilled-chicken izakaya. Every section of the menu — sashimi, charcoal-grilled fish, yakitori skewers, and small plates — is built around animal proteins. No plant-based alternatives are listed or implied by any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: 南駅前町 (Minami-Ekimae-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A charcoal-fire izakaya that has operated near Himeji Station's south exit for roughly 26 years. The restaurant recently underwent a renovation and added lunch service. It is located on the first floor of the Yamakamibe Building, a one- to two-minute walk from the south exit of J

chef_bio.background: A charcoal-fire izakaya that has operated near Himeji Station's south exit for roughly 26 years. The restaurant recently underwent a renovation and added lunch service. It is located on the first floor of the Yamakamibe Building, a one- to two-minute walk from the south exit of JR Himeji Station.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal seafood sourced from the Harima-nada (Banshu-nada) sea, served as sashimi or over the charcoal grill; yakitori skewers are also a staple.

anecdotes.text: A reviewer described the atmosphere as 'exceptionally calm and relaxing,' noting they felt drawn to return for the comfortable counter seating and the quality of the yellowtail sashimi and one-night-dried grilled flounder.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Shared charcoal grill used for both seafood and chicken; no separation for allergen-sensitive diners mentioned anywhere.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce (wheat-containing) is a fundamental ingredient in izakaya cooking — tare for yakitori, marinades, and dipping sauces all apply here. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce options are indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All menu items involve seafood or chicken; no vegan dishes exist to evaluate for cross-contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu is noted on any platform reviewed. Inquiries would need to be made in Japanese directly with staff.

positives.text: Plain sashimi (without soy sauce) is the single category most likely to be free of added wheat — the kitchen does receive and serve raw seafood that has not been marinated.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-established neighborhood izakaya near Himeji Station with no English menu or signage; staff are likely Japanese-only, but the counter atmosphere is welcoming if you can point and gesture.

places.name: 青海 (Oomi)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is virtually certain to be used in nigiri marinades and side dishes. Edomae-style dashi is fish-based, not wheat-relevant, but tamari or wheat-free shoyu is not mentioned anywhere. No dedicated GF menu or allergen information available. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead — there is no evidence the kitchen can accommodate wheat avoidance.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional Edomae sushi counter built around fish and seafood; no vegan options identified. Sushi rice seasoning, dashi-based side dishes, and fish-centric menu make accommodation highly unlikely without advance arrangement.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), closed Sun & holidays
places.neighborhood: Nishinikaimachi / Otemae area
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established sushi counter in the historic Nishinikaichō shopping street near Himeji Castle, said to be patronized by notable figures in the city. The shop occupies the first floor of the Tobira Building and seats just 14 guests.

chef_bio.background: A long-established sushi counter in the historic Nishinikaichō shopping street near Himeji Castle, said to be patronized by notable figures in the city. The shop occupies the first floor of the Tobira Building and seats just 14 guests.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quiet craftsmanship: the master and his wife offer attentive, unhurried hospitality at the counter, letting seasonal ingredients speak for themselves.
chef_bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri sushi with seasonal seafood — mackerel, uni, matsutake mushroom accompaniments — prepared with refined knife work including the traditional 隠し包丁 (kakushi bōchō) scoring technique.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers consistently praise the master and his wife's hospitality: 'The master and his wife's service was excellent — the sushi and small dishes were outstanding.' The counter's intimate scale means every piece is made to order in front of the guest.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls available. A busy counter kitchen serving multiple courses is high-risk for shared surfaces and utensils.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is standard in Edomae sushi preparation (nigiri brushing, marinades). No tamari or wheat-free shoyu noted anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entire menu centres on fish and seafood; vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy or dietary accommodation information found on any platform. Guests should call the restaurant directly before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: Intimate counter-only sushi bar run by a husband-and-wife team; interaction with the master is part of the experience, and communication is entirely in Japanese.

places.name: 四季と創和の酔い処 ひより (Shiki to Souwa no Yoidokoro Hiyori)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options are advertised. The flagship course includes fresh-fish tempura (wheat batter), and standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is almost certainly used throughout. No dedicated fryer information is available. Celiac diners should not assume safety — direct inquiry in Japanese is essential before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options are listed. The kitchen is built around seafood, wagyu beef, and classic Japanese preparations — virtually every dish will include fish-based dashi (鰹・昆布), and the tempura course uses wheat batter. Vegetarians and vegans should call ahead (080-3818-0270), but accommodation is uncertain for a counter-focused omakase.
places.hours_raw: Mon & Sat 17:30–22:30 (L.O. 22:00); Tue–Fri 11:30–14:00 / 17:30–22:30 (L.O. 22:00); closed Sun, holidays, and the day after a holiday
places.neighborhood: 久保町 (Kubo-cho), near Sanyo-Himeji Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened on 11 February 2026 in the Kubo-cho neighbourhood just a few minutes' walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The owner-chef personally selects seasonal ingredients daily, positioning the restaurant as a sophisticated adult hideaway (大人の隠れ家) in the kappo-izakaya style.

chef_bio.background: Opened on 11 February 2026 in the Kubo-cho neighbourhood just a few minutes' walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The owner-chef personally selects seasonal ingredients daily, positioning the restaurant as a sophisticated adult hideaway (大人の隠れ家) in the kappo-izakaya style.
chef_bio.philosophy: Local sourcing and seasonality (地産地消・旬の食材) — the menu changes each day according to what the owner's eye finds at market.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal seafood with a focus on Himeji's prized conger eel (鱧/hamo) and same-morning-caught fish served as sashimi, tataki, or tempura; also features Japanese wagyu beef.

anecdotes.text: Early visitors described the interior as 'very clean' and the spot as a 'hidden gem,' with the salt-grilled sweetfish (鮎の塩焼き) drawing particular praise shortly after opening.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is a core menu item (fresh fish and seasonal vegetable tempura appear in the standard course), meaning wheat flour is actively used in the kitchen. Cross-contamination for celiacs must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (醤油) is standard in Japanese kappo cooking and almost certainly used across sashimi, grilled, and vinegared preparations.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi (鰹・昆布) is foundational to Japanese kappo cuisine and will be present in soups, vinegared dishes, and sauces. No plant-based dashi alternative is noted anywhere.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy is published. The restaurant is a small 17-seat counter operation; direct phone contact (080-3818-0270) before visiting is the only viable path for allergy inquiries.

positives.text: The owner hand-selects ingredients daily at market, suggesting flexibility around what goes into each dish — a potential opening for custom requests if communicated in advance in Japanese.

cultural_comfort.note: A quiet, adult-oriented kappo hideaway with counter seating and a semi-private room; no English signage or menu noted, and dietary requests in English would require preparation.

places.name: 活水軒 (Kassui-ken)
places.gf_detail: This restaurant is not safe for celiac diners. Wheat is present throughout the menu: soba noodles (buckwheat-wheat blended in standard Japanese soba), tempura batter (wheat flour), udon noodles, and conventional soy sauce used in braised anago preparations. No gluten-free menu or allergen accommodation is listed on the official site or mentioned in any review. No dedicated fryer information found. Inquiring in advance would be necessary, and the kitchen is a high-cross-contamination environment.
places.vegan_detail: No vegetarian menu is offered (explicitly stated by the restaurant on GLTJP). The signature dishes — anago (conger eel) don and tempura — are fish-based. Miso soup, dashi in noodle broths, and sauces almost certainly contain katsuobushi (bonito) or niboshi dashi. One visitor reported their vegetarian partner could only find soybeans and noodles. A zenzai (sweet red bean soup) dessert is noted by one reviewer as likely vegan, but confirmation of dashi-free preparation is unavailable. Suitable only for those with very flexible plant-based preferences who are comfortable eating at an omni restaurant with minimal options.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–16:00 (last order 15:30); meal service 11:00–15:00; seasonal variation (some sources note 9:30–17:30 in Sep–Apr). Closed December 29–30.
places.neighborhood: 好古園内 / 姫路城西 (Koko-en Garden, west of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: Kassui-ken sits inside Koko-en (好古園), a 3.5-hectare traditional Japanese garden built in 1992 on the site of the former Nishi-Oyashiki (west lord's residence) adjacent to Himeji Castle. The restaurant was established as part of the garden complex and is operated by the Himeji Cit

chef_bio.background: Kassui-ken sits inside Koko-en (好古園), a 3. 5-hectare traditional Japanese garden built in 1992 on the site of the former Nishi-Oyashiki (west lord's residence) adjacent to Himeji Castle. The restaurant was established as part of the garden complex and is operated by the Himeji City Urban Development Promotion Organization (公益社団法人姫路市まちづくり振興機構), a civic body that manages the garden. Its name, 活水軒 (Kassui-ken), evokes the classical ideal of 'living water' — flowing, nourishing, and ever-renewing — a fitting name for a pavilion beside garden ponds.
chef_bio.philosophy: Showcasing the traditional taste of Himeji through seasonal, locally-rooted Harima cuisine in a setting where the garden and castle become part of the meal
chef_bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) dishes — particularly the anago don (braised conger eel over rice) and anago tempura sets — using the prized conger eel of Harima Bay, a longtime Himeji culinary tradition

anecdotes.text: Visitors frequently arrive only to find the limited-quantity 'Hime Gozen' and 'Kanbei Bento' sets already sold out by midday — a recurring note in reviews urging early arrival.
During peak autumn foliage season the wait can exceed 35 minutes even after reducing the initial queue through a garden walk — a rhythm regulars have learned to work with.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is a core menu item (anago tempura, vegetable tempura) cooked in shared kitchen oil using wheat-flour batter. No dedicated fryer or allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Braised anago (conger eel) dishes rely on conventional Japanese soy sauce (shoyu), which contains wheat. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dashi stock (likely katsuobushi/bonito-based) is standard in miso soup, noodle broths, and simmered preparations throughout the menu. No plant-based dashi option is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy chart, or documented staff allergy-handling procedure appears on the official site or in any guest review. The restaurant does not list dietary accommodations as a feature.

positives.text: Non-smoking throughout; Himeji-area Wi-Fi available; credit cards and QR code payments accepted, reducing cash-handling stress for visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: A popular tourist destination attracting many international visitors — basic navigation is manageable for non-Japanese speakers, but staff communication beyond standard ordering is Japanese-first; English menus may be available given the tourist context.

places.name: あなご料理 柊 本店 (Anago Ryori Hiiragi Honten)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is pervasive throughout this menu. Standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is used in grilled and simmered preparations; tempura items are battered with wheat flour; dashi is fish-based (anago/konbu) but shoyu marinades add wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce or dedicated fryer is mentioned anywhere. Cross-contamination risk is high given the small kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant before visiting; some plain-grilled (白焼き) preparations may be lower-risk if shoyu can be omitted, but this requires direct confirmation.
places.vegan_detail: This is a fish-specialist restaurant dedicated entirely to conger eel. Every dish centres on anago: sashimi, grilled, steamed, tempura, rice bowls, and kaiseki courses. There are no plant-based mains or sides designed for vegans, and all broths and cooking sauces will contain fish-derived ingredients. Vegans have no practical options here.
places.neighborhood: Junishomaemachi (6 min walk from Sanyo-Himeji Stn, 7 min walk from JR Himeji Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hiiragi Honten is the flagship location of a small Himeji company (株式会社 HILO, representative director Hiroshi Tanaka) dedicated to Harima Himeji's prized conger eel. The restaurant opened in June 2005 along the Juniisho-mae-cho stretch near the castle, drawing on the rich fishing

chef_bio.origin: 姫路市十二所前町、播磨灘産穴子を専門とする
chef_bio.background: Hiiragi Honten is the flagship location of a small Himeji company (株式会社 HILO, representative director Hiroshi Tanaka) dedicated to Harima Himeji's prized conger eel. The restaurant opened in June 2005 along the Juniisho-mae-cho stretch near the castle, drawing on the rich fishing grounds of the Harima Strait — a fishery historically celebrated for producing especially plump, delicately flavoured anago. The brand later expanded to a second site inside the Qwell Himeji shopping complex, but the Honten retains the original intimate feel with tatami rooms, counter seats, and a still-small 35-seat layout. A predecessor establishment called 本家田中の焼あなご (Honke Tanaka's Grilled Anago) is referenced in early reviews, suggesting the current brand grew from an older family trade.
chef_bio.philosophy: 播磨灘の新鮮な穴子を多彩な調理法で提供し、素材本来の風味を最大限に引き出す
chef_bio.specialty: 白焼き穴子（白焼き：素材の旨味を生かした塩焼き）および穴子づくし御膳（穴子の刺身・焼き・蒸し・天ぷら・飯の膳）

anecdotes.text: Reviewers on 4travel single out the 穴子づくし御膳 (all-anago set) — featuring sashimi, white-grilled, steamed, tempura, and rice — as the definitive way to experience the range of Harima anago preparations in one sitting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is a core menu item; wheat flour is used in the batter. No dedicated fryer or separate wheat-free cooking line is mentioned across any source reviewed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is the default condiment and marinade base for grilled and simmered anago preparations. No tamari or gluten-free shoyu alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire menu is built around fish and seafood; all broths and sauces derive from anago and dashi. There is no plant-based cooking line.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling procedures or allergen menu are documented on any available source. Direct contact by phone (079-284-1239) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: White-grilled anago (白焼き) is a minimal-ingredient preparation that showcases the eel without heavy saucing — if diners can confirm no shoyu marinade is applied and the piece is cooked on a clean surface, this could be the lowest-wheat item on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: A traditional Japanese anago specialty house with tatami seating and kaiseki-style courses; English menus are available but the experience and staff interactions are conducted primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 瓢亭 (Hyotei)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free designation. Traditional kaiseki relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) and dashi made from bonito; suppon hot pot courses also typically include soy sauce in broth. The omakase kaiseki notes it can accommodate ingredient preferences and allergies when reserving by phone, but there is no evidence of dedicated GF protocols or wheat-free alternatives. Celiac diners should call ahead and ask specifically about shoyu substitution — cross-contamination risk is high in a traditional Japanese kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options listed. The restaurant's identity centers on suppon (soft-shell turtle), seafood, and kaiseki featuring meat, fish, and bonito-based dashi. The menu can accept ingredient requests at reservation time, but a fully plant-based course would be a major departure from the kitchen's focus. Not a realistic vegan venue without direct negotiation in Japanese.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, foot of Himeji Castle
places.notes: The predecessor business, Shirakura Shokudo, was established in 1916. After World War II, the second-generation owner Kenji Shirakura — who had trained as a naval ship's cook — reopened as Hyotei in 1946 in Himeji's Gofukucho district. In 1958 the current main location at Honmach

chef_bio.background: The predecessor business, Shirakura Shokudo, was established in 1916. After World War II, the second-generation owner Kenji Shirakura — who had trained as a naval ship's cook — reopened as Hyotei in 1946 in Himeji's Gofukucho district. In 1958 the current main location at Honmachi 68, at the base of Himeji Castle, was established as Kappo Hyotei. The third generation, Houso Shirakura, underwent training at over 30 renowned restaurants across Japan before returning to lead the kitchen. In 2005 the fourth generation, Hiromitsu Shirakura (born 1972), took over, having himself trained at Japanese restaurants, ryokan, and Tokyo dining establishments from age 18 before assuming leadership at 33.
chef_bio.philosophy: The current owner's stated mission is to 'deliver emotion through food' and to run a place where connections endure across 100–200 years: 語り継がれる瓢亭という想い出、受け継がれる瓢亭という場所 ('memories of Hyotei passed down through stories; Hyotei as a place inherited across time').
chef_bio.specialty: Suppon (soft-shell turtle) hot pot — the restaurant's most celebrated dish. The suppon is sourced from Anjoin in Oita Prefecture and the broth is simmered for over 8 hours until it turns a distinctive milky-white color. The restaurant claims a 90% repeat-customer rate for this course. Seasonal additions include pike conger (hamo) in summer, pufferfish (fugu) in winter, wagyu sukiyaki, bear nabe, and Seto Inland Sea seafood. Exceptional or unusual requests — whale, wild boar, pheasant — can be sourced on request.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant began serving suppon dishes in 1975 and developed its original hot pot recipe in-house, building a following strong enough that roughly 9 in 10 customers who try the suppon course return for it.

The second-generation owner Kenji Shirakura drew on his experience cooking on naval ships during WWII when he reopened Hyotei in 1946, bringing professional kitchen discipline to what had been a family food business.
The second-floor private room offers a direct view of Himeji Castle and must be reserved specifically in advance — it is treated as a special occasion seat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryers or separated prep surfaces is publicly available. The kitchen handles fried suppon, tempura-style preparations, and other wheat-using techniques across a shared kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese soy sauce (shoyu, which contains wheat) is a foundational ingredient in kaiseki cooking — used in broths, marinades, sauces, and finishing. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere on the official site or review platforms.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All courses are built around animal proteins (suppon, seafood, game, wagyu). Dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi (bonito flake) or similar fish/animal-based stock. No plant-based dashi alternative is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official menu page notes that the omakase kaiseki course 'accepts ingredient requests and accommodates allergies/preferences' — but this must be communicated at the time of phone reservation. There is no online form or written allergy protocol described.

positives.text: Fully private-room dining eliminates cross-contact from other tables and allows the chef to tailor each course to the group's stated needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Formal traditional kaiseki setting with tatami and private rooms; the official site is Japanese-only and reservations must be made by phone in Japanese; the Tabelog English page provides basic info but staff interaction will require Japanese.

places.name: 播州姫路本町たまごや (Banshu Himeji Honmachi Tamagoya)
places.gf_detail: The core dish — plain rice plus raw egg — is inherently wheat-free, but the standard set comes with wheat-containing elements: (1) the house original soy sauce (tare) is wheat-brewed standard shoyu; (2) bonito flakes are sprinkled on rice by default and must be requested omitted; (3) the Harima red-miso soup's dashi and miso paste are unconfirmed for wheat. This is not a dedicated GF facility. Reviewers on FindMeGlutenFree describe staff as accommodating when asked, and one celiac diner ate rice and eggs safely after communicating needs, but also noted the need to bring personal GF soy sauce. Cross-contamination risk from the kitchen cannot be ruled out.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around eggs (tamago kake gohan, thick omelette, conger eel and egg). Miso soup is included with sets and likely uses katsuobushi (bonito) dashi in the Harima red-miso broth. No plant-based alternatives are offered. Egg-eating pescatarians could find the plain rice-and-egg base workable, but strict vegans have nothing here.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:30), open daily (with year-end/New Year and occasional temporary closures)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (Karo Yashiki-kan / in front of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The restaurant is run by Fujihashi Shoten, a Himeji egg-farming family whose chicken operations date to 1925. They raise approximately 350,000 birds across two farms in the naturally rich districts of Yumesaki and Shikitou on the outskirts of Himeji, using custom-blended feed and

chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo Prefecture, Japan — inside the Karoyashiki (historic samurai retainer compound) at Honmachi 68, directly on the approach to Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.background: The restaurant is run by Fujihashi Shoten, a Himeji egg-farming family whose chicken operations date to 1925. They raise approximately 350,000 birds across two farms in the naturally rich districts of Yumesaki and Shikitou on the outskirts of Himeji, using custom-blended feed and — by their own account — playing Mozart for the flock. The Himeji Castle-front shop opened in 2009 as a way to bring those farm eggs directly to the table.
chef_bio.philosophy: Farm-to-bowl simplicity: grow your own chickens, mill your own rice, develop your own soy sauce, and let the quality speak. Fujihashi Shoten sources nearly all ingredients from its own farms or from local Harima-region producers.
chef_bio.specialty: Tamakake meshi — plain cooked rice served with one of two house egg varieties and a drizzle of original blended soy sauce (tare co-developed with a local shoyu maker), with unlimited refills of both rice and eggs. The premium tier features Yume-oh eggs, which won three consecutive national Tamago Kake Gohan Festival competitions and were retired to the hall of fame.

anecdotes.text: The premium 'Yume-oh' egg competed in the national Tamago Kake Gohan Festival three times and won each time, eventually being inducted into the festival's hall of fame and retired from competition.
The chickens on the Fujihashi farms are raised while listening to Mozart — a detail the restaurant itself highlights as part of its low-stress farming philosophy.

A touch-panel ticketing screen outside the shop lets waiting diners take a number and browse the nearby souvenir stalls before their seat is ready — a small quality-of-life touch noted by multiple visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free facility; kitchen cross-contamination risk is unconfirmed. No allergen separation policy is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house original soy sauce (tare) is a custom wheat-brewed shoyu co-developed with a local manufacturer. It is not gluten-free. Coeliacs should bring their own tamari or GF soy sauce.
One FindMeGlutenFree reviewer specifically advises bringing your own soy sauce to this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito flakes (katsuo-bushi) are sprinkled on rice by default; must be requested omitted. Miso soup dashi type is unconfirmed but likely katsuobushi-based given regional Harima red-miso tradition.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reviewers on FindMeGlutenFree describe staff as helpful and accommodating when dietary needs are communicated clearly. One celiac diner reported eating rice, eggs, and pickled plums safely after asking about ingredients.

positives.text: The core ingredients — farm-fresh raw egg over plain white rice — are naturally wheat-free and suitable as a baseline for gluten-avoiders who communicate clearly with staff.

cultural_comfort.note: English and Chinese menus are available; the experience is welcoming to tourists visiting Himeji Castle, and staff have been noted as helpful to dietary queries.

places.name: 千姫茶屋 (Senhime Chaya)
places.gf_detail: Wheat is pervasive: udon (wheat noodles), tempura batter on shrimp and conger eel, and soy sauce used throughout (including the house oyster-dashi soy sauce). The signature shirasu don sets include somen or udon as side dishes. No gluten-free menu, no GF soy sauce alternative, and no dedicated fryer is mentioned. Not suitable for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood — shirasu (whitebait), anago (conger eel), and ebi (shrimp tempura) — with dashi-based udon soups and Himeji oden. No plant-based alternatives are listed, and hidden fish stock (dashi/katsuo) is near-certain in soups and oden broth. Not suitable for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–14:30 (food L.O. 14:30); evenings onward for groups only, by reservation. Irregular closures only around year-end/New Year.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi / Egret Himeji / 3 min walk from Himeji Castle
places.notes: 千姫茶屋 is operated by 輝光ライブフード株式会社 (Kiko Live Food Co., Ltd.), a Himeji-based hospitality company that also runs the Michelin-listed kaiseki counter おもてなしきこう and the tonkatsu specialty restaurant 別邸 谷かつ. The chaya (tea house) concept sits at the more accessible, tourist-friendly en

roles: hospitality group (輝光ライブフード株式会社)
chef_bio.background: 千姫茶屋 is operated by 輝光ライブフード株式会社 (Kiko Live Food Co. , Ltd. ), a Himeji-based hospitality company that also runs the Michelin-listed kaiseki counter おもてなしきこう and the tonkatsu specialty restaurant 別邸 谷かつ. The chaya (tea house) concept sits at the more accessible, tourist-friendly end of the group's portfolio — intended to give visitors from afar not just a meal but a taste of Himeji memory-making.
chef_bio.philosophy: To offer travelers an experience of Himeji's flavours and hospitality — cuisine as souvenir of place, not merely sustenance.
chef_bio.specialty: Shirasu donburi: fresh Harima Nada whitebait served in an oversized bowl shaped to resemble the helmet of local warlord Kuroda Kanbei, alongside Himeji oden (eaten with ginger soy sauce) and Banshu Ibonoito somen noodles.

anecdotes.text: The signature shirasu bowl arrives in a 30 cm black vessel modelled on warlord Kuroda Kanbei's helmet — a theatrical presentation that turns a bowl of whitebait into a piece of Himeji history.

The whitebait comes from the shallow, fast-flowing waters of Harima Nada, fed by five rivers whose nutrients produce fish with notably firm flesh and clean flavour, according to the restaurant's own description.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura (shrimp, conger eel) is a core menu item, meaning wheat flour is in active use in the kitchen; no dedicated fryer or separate prep area is mentioned.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house condiment is an oyster-dashi soy sauce (東丸のカキだし醤油) — a wheat-containing soy sauce used at table and almost certainly in cooking. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is listed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish stock (dashi) is the base of udon soups and oden broth; shirasu, anago, and ebi are prepared throughout the kitchen. No plant-only cooking area exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant notes halal accommodation is available upon special arrangement, suggesting some dietary flexibility, but no mention of gluten or allergen handling protocols appears on any public source.

positives.text: Credit cards accepted (Visa, Mastercard, Amex, JCB, Diners), multilingual menus available, and barrier-free access including wheelchair and sign language services — logistics are well handled even if dietary accommodation is limited.

cultural_comfort.note: Tourist-facing venue steps from Himeji Castle with multilingual menus and international visitor services; staff are accustomed to foreign guests.

places.name: 赤心 本店 (Sekishin Honten)
places.gf_detail: Tonkatsu is fried in wheat-flour breadcrumbs, so gluten-free accommodation is impossible. The miso in the pork soup (tonjiru) may contain wheat, and along with the ketchup rice of the omurice, wheat risk extends to every item. Presence of a dedicated fryer is unknown, but as a breaded-fried specialty shop, cross-contamination is unavoidable. Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The mainstays are tonkatsu (pork tenderloin/loin) and omurice (containing pork). Dishes throughout use pork, egg, and dairy, and there is no vegan menu. The miso-based pork soup is also primarily animal-based dashi. Assessment: not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Wed, Fri–Sun 11:00–15:00, closed Mon & Thu
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho / 3 min walk from JR Himeji Stn north exit
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A long-established Himeji tonkatsu shop in business for over 82 years. The name "Sekishin" is said to derive from words of Katsu Kaishu, whom the founder's great-grandfather reportedly revered, and means "a sincere heart." It once operated four locations in Himeji but now runs only this main shop. It has been featured on television and in various media multiple times.

chef_bio.background: 創業82年以上の姫路の老舗とんかつ店。 店名「赤心」は、創業者の曽祖父が尊敬したとされる勝海舟の言葉に由来するといわれ、「誠実な心」を意味する。 かつては姫路市内に4店舗を展開していたが、現在は本店1軒のみを営業。 テレビや各種メディアに複数回取り上げられた実績を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 長年変わらない味を守り続けるスタイル。 セルフ感のない丁寧なカウンター対応。
chef_bio.specialty: とんかつ定食（パン粉揚げのロース・ヒレカツ）と、とろける玉ねぎと濃厚味噌のとん汁。 オムライスは鶏肉ではなく豚肉を使用するのが特徴。

anecdotes.text: 開店15分前から並んでも2巡目待ちになることがある人気ぶり。著者はとん汁目当てに訪問し「とろけそうな玉ねぎの甘味とコクのある味噌でやみつきになる味」と記している。
創業82年で姫路の老舗として行列ができる店と紹介され、過去4店舗から現在1店舗に集約した経緯が記されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: とんかつ専門店のため、揚げ油は小麦パン粉が常時混入しており、セリアック病・小麦アレルギーの方には極めて高リスク。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: とん汁・とんかつソース・ドレッシングなど複数のアイテムに小麦含有醤油またはソースが使われている可能性が高いが、公式な成分情報は公開されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 豚肉・卵・乳製品が全メニューに使用されており、ヴィーガン向けの交差汚染回避は不可能。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応に関する情報は公式・ブログいずれからも確認できず、現金のみ・予約不可の小規模店のためアレルギー相談体制は期待できない。

cultural_comfort.note: カウンター7席のみの小さな老舗で、英語メニューや英語対応スタッフは期待できない。注文は指差しでも可能。

places.name: 杏もん堂 (Almondou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options confirmed. The signature dish is almond butter toast on wheat bread. The butter chicken curry uses a roux made with 18 spices (likely wheat-thickened). Standard soy sauce is common in Japanese café kitchens. No GF-labeled items, no dedicated fryer information, and no allergy accommodation language found in any source.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options confirmed. The core menu is built around butter — homemade dairy-based almond butter on wheat toast — plus almond milk lattes that likely include dairy. The butter chicken curry uses dairy. No plant-based substitutions or vegan-labeled items found in any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:00–17:00 (food L.O. 16:00, drink L.O. 16:30), closed Wed. From March 2026, evening cafe hours added on Fri & Sat.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (Otemae, in front of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: Opened November 28, 2025 in Otemae Daiichi Building 1F, directly south of Ōtemae Park with a full view of Himeji Castle.

chef_bio.background: Opened November 28, 2025 in Otemae Daiichi Building 1F, directly south of Ōtemae Park with a full view of Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Manager Sugahara set out to elevate Himeji's well-known almond butter local-specialty tradition from souvenir culture into a craft café experience. The café grinds its own almonds and blends almond into as many menu items as possible — lattes, curry, spice toppings — while welcoming the international tourists who visit the castle next door.
chef_bio.specialty: 自家製アーモンドバタートーストセット — homemade almond butter in two styles (smooth Classic and crunchy Crunch), roasted and ground in-house, served on thick toast with almond-milk soup and bagna cauda-style almond sauce dip.

anecdotes.text: From March 2026, the café began Friday and Saturday evening hours so guests could enjoy the illuminated Himeji Castle visible through the front windows.
The butter chicken curry is finished with dukkah — an Egyptian spice blend that incorporates almonds — connecting almond to global culinary traditions.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce information found in any source; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and is common in café kitchens. Assume wheat-containing soy sauce unless confirmed otherwise.

cultural_comfort.note: Manager Sugahara noted a specific desire to engage with foreign visitors who come to see Himeji Castle, and the café is actively developing takeout menus for walking tourists.

places.name: つなぎ (Tsunagi)
places.gf_detail: Standard izakaya with wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) used throughout — in marinades, dressings, and dipping sauces. Oden broth and many sauces are likely wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or allergen labeling advertised. Cross-contamination risk is high in a shared kitchen. Dedicated fryer status unknown. Advance inquiry in Japanese is required; celiac diners should exercise caution.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options advertised. Core identity is conger eel (anago) and seasonal seafood with Japanese dashi stock, which typically contains bonito (katsuobushi) and/or kelp. Hidden animal products in sauces, dashi, and marinades are near-certain. Advance inquiry in Japanese is essential.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Thu, Sun: 17:00–23:00 (LO 22:30); Fri–Sat: 17:00–24:00 (LO 23:30); Closed Monday. Hours subject to change.
places.neighborhood: 南町 (Minami-cho) — 2-minute walk north of Sanyo-Himeji Statio
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A locally rooted izakaya in Minami-cho, a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station, built around Himeji's culinary heritage. The shop has positioned itself as a showcase for Himeji's most distinctive local ingredients and traditions.

chef_bio.background: A locally rooted izakaya in Minami-cho, a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station, built around Himeji's culinary heritage. The shop has positioned itself as a showcase for Himeji's most distinctive local ingredients and traditions.
chef_bio.philosophy: The concept centers on celebrating the Banshu (western Hyogo) food culture: locally sourced Seto Inland Sea seafood, Himeji specialties, and regional sake labels such as Tatsuriki (龍力) and Yaegaki (八重垣), presented in a calm, contemporary Japanese aesthetic.
chef_bio.specialty: Densuké anago (伝助穴子) — a rare variety of conger eel representing only a few percent of all eel catches — served as sashimi, grilled, and in hitsumabushi-style preparations. Also features hineppon (ひねぽん, a Himeji chicken specialty), Himeji lotus root, and Himeji oden.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's own site describes the interior as 'a separate world' upon entry — a modern space where floor-level windows reveal garden-like designs, blending old and new Japanese aesthetics.
Densuké anago is described as accounting for only a few percent of all anago caught, making it a prized local delicacy central to Tsunagi's menu identity.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard izakaya kitchen with no GF segregation advertised. Wheat-based soy sauce and dashi are core to virtually every dish. Cross-contamination risk is high.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦) is the backbone of Japanese izakaya cooking and almost certainly used throughout the menu — in marinades, tare, dipping sauces, and oden broth. No wheat-free shoyu or tamari alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito-based (katsuobushi) dashi is standard in Japanese izakaya broth, sauces, and oden. Seafood is central to the menu concept. No plant-based preparation stream exists.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy handling protocol, or foreign-language dietary communication noted on any source. Direct inquiry by phone in Japanese is the only confirmed route.

positives.text: Cards widely accepted (Visa, MC, Amex, JCB, Diners, e-money); counter seating available for solo diners; reservations accepted by phone and online.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter and table-style izakaya with Japanese-language menus and staff; basic Japanese or phone reservations expected, no English menu confirmed.

places.name: 天空・地酒・おもてなし きこう×龍力 (Tenkuu Jizake Omotenashi Kikou × Tatsuriki)
places.gf_detail: Kaiseki cuisine widely uses soy sauce (contains wheat), tempura batter, and fried foods. There is no mention whatsoever of gluten-free accommodation, and presence of a dedicated fryer is unknown. Celiac accommodation cannot be guaranteed; direct confirmation with the restaurant before visiting is essential. Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The courses are orthodox kaiseki making heavy use of bonito dashi, soy sauce, and seafood. No vegan accommodation is indicated. There is a note that halal accommodation is possible, leaving some room to discuss ingredient changes, but omitting hidden dashi (bonito, kombu) or switching to a fully plant-based course requires advance inquiry. Assessment: not reliably vegan.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–14:00 (LO 13:30), dinner 17:00–21:30 (LO 21:00). Irregular days off. Reservations required.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, Egret Himeji (in front of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: Born in Hyogo Prefecture in 1968. Involved in the food industry since high school and university, and while working at a hotel was responsible for meals for professional baseball players. Convinced that "pre-made dishes cannot move people," he went independent. As a local who knows Himeji's ingredients inside out, he aims to be "a restaurant worth going out of your way for," using seasonal Setouchi and Tajima ingredients. He also serves as chairman of the Hyogo Prefecture Specialty Products Promotion Association.

chef_bio.chef_name: 安田忠正（やすだ ただまさ）
chef_bio.background: 1968年兵庫県生まれ。 高校・大学時代から飲食業に携わり、ホテル勤務でプロ野球選手向けの食事を担当。 「作り置きした料理は感動を与えられない」という確信を得て独立。 姫路の食材を知り尽くした地元出身者として、旬の瀬戸内・但馬食材を活かした料理で「わざわざ来る価値のある店」を目指す。 兵庫県特産品振興協会会長も務める。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「目の前に世界文化遺産を望める景色に負けない料理で、おもてなしをしたい」——姫路城の絶景に見合う料理と空間で、訪れる人を感動させることを信条とする。
chef_bio.specialty: 旬の食材を使ったおまかせ懐石コース（¥11,000〜¥22,000）。 地元・姫路の清酒「龍力」との特別ペアリング。

anecdotes.text: ホテル勤務時代にプロ野球選手向けの食事を担当。時間が経った料理は美味しくないと痛感し、「お客様の目の前で調理するライブクッキング」を自店の核心に据えた。
2024年7月、創業100年を超える姫路の酒蔵「龍力（本田商店）」とコラボし、イーグレひめじ4階に天空レストランをオープン。他では入手できない特別な龍力の酒を提供する。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 天ぷら・揚げ物を含む懐石コースで専用フライヤーの有無は不明。グルテン交差汚染リスクに関する情報は一切公開されていない。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 懐石料理では醤油（小麦含有）が煮物・焼き物・たれ類に幅広く使われる。グルテンフリー醤油（丸大豆醤油・タマリ）使用の記載なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: コースは魚介・鰹だしを基本とする。完全植物性メニューへの対応を示す情報はなく、だし・ソースへの動物性素材の混入リスクが高い。

staff_allergy_handling.text: ハラール・ムスリム対応可能との記載あり（公式サイト）。アレルギー一般については「事前にご相談ください」の案内のみで、具体的な対応プロセスの開示なし。

positives.text: ハラール対応を公式に表明しており、食材・調理への配慮ができるスタッフ体制がある程度整っていると推測される。
おまかせコースは事前相談でカスタマイズ可能（海外からのゲスト向けに神戸牛コース等へ変更実績あり）。食制限も事前連絡で応相談の余地はある。

cultural_comfort.note: 高級懐石コース専門店でメニューは日本語中心。スタッフは英語対応の記載なし。外国人向けメニューカスタマイズ（神戸牛コースなど）は応相談とあるが、要事前連絡。

places.name: 豆の王國 (Mame no Oukoku)
places.gf_detail: The core product range is soy and tofu, which are naturally gluten-free, but the menu includes wheat-containing items (toast, soy milk soup curry base may use standard soy sauce). Conventional Japanese soy sauce (shoyu) contains wheat — there is no published claim of tamari or GF shoyu use here. The doujiang soup and yuba dishes may be safe depending on dashi and seasoning choices, but no dedicated GF protocol or allergen menu has been confirmed. Cross-contamination risk exists in a small single-operator kitchen. Ask staff directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Several menu items are naturally plant-based: housemade yuba (tofu skin) sashimi set, doujiang (Taiwanese soy milk soup), soy milk soup curry with multigrain rice, and soy milk okara donuts. However, the lunch menu also includes a salt-koji chicken set, so this is not a dedicated vegan establishment. No mention of hidden dashi or bonito in the soy-based dishes has appeared in any source, but the restaurant does not explicitly label vegan items and uses conventional Japanese pantry ingredients — fish-based dashi in soups cannot be ruled out without asking staff.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 09:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30); closed Sun, Mon, and public holidays. Morning service 09:00–11:00; Lunch service 11:00–15:00.
places.neighborhood: 福沢町 (Fukusawa-cho), ~10 min walk west of Himeji / Sanyo-Hime
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Housed in a converted warehouse belonging to Umezawa Ryokoku Co., Ltd. (梅澤糧穀株式会社), a grain wholesale business founded in 1920 that has supplied soybeans and grains across Himeji for over a century. An earlier iteration of the restaurant operated at the same Fukusawa-cho address b

chef_bio.background: Housed in a converted warehouse belonging to Umezawa Ryokoku Co. , Ltd. (梅澤糧穀株式会社), a grain wholesale business founded in 1920 that has supplied soybeans and grains across Himeji for over a century. An earlier iteration of the restaurant operated at the same Fukusawa-cho address but closed; in July 2024 the owner's daughter revived it, initially running the entire operation — cooking, counter service, and sourcing — on her own.
chef_bio.philosophy: 自然の力をまるごと、誠実に — 'Capturing the full force of nature, honestly. ' The kitchen avoids artificial processing and draws directly on the wholesale company's access to premium-grade legumes to put the intrinsic flavour of the bean at the centre of every dish.
chef_bio.specialty: Housemade yuba (fresh tofu skin) sashimi set and doujiang (台湾式豆漿) — a Taiwanese-style soy milk soup curdled with vinegar and finished with chilli oil. Soybeans are the 'Yume-hana' (夢華) variety grown without pesticides in Yumesaki-cho, prized for their large grain and concentrated sweetness.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant reopened on 27 July 2024 after a nine-year closure, with the founder's daughter managing everything solo from the kitchen to front-of-house during the initial launch phase.

The doujiang on the menu is a Taiwanese breakfast staple unfamiliar to many Japanese diners: soy milk soup whose protein is curdled by vinegar, topped with chilli oil — a deliberate nod to the bean's culinary breadth beyond traditional Japanese tofu.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or fryer has been confirmed. This is a small single-operator café with a mixed menu including wheat-containing toast and possibly standard soy sauce — cross-contamination cannot be excluded.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms the use of tamari or wheat-free soy sauce. Standard Japanese shoyu (which contains wheat) is the default assumption for all cooked dishes. Ask staff to confirm the specific shoyu used before ordering.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The lunch menu includes a salt-koji chicken set alongside plant-based dishes. In a small kitchen run by one person, shared prep surfaces and cookware are likely. No vegan-specific protocol has been published.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, staff allergy-handling policy, or dedicated allergy procedure has been mentioned in any published source. Direct communication with the owner-operator (who also cooks) is the recommended approach — phone 080-9475-9197.

positives.text: The restaurant's entire identity revolves around minimally processed soybean and tofu cookery, with house-sourced 'Yume-hana' soybeans grown without pesticides — a cleaner ingredient baseline than a general restaurant.
The proprietor runs the kitchen solo, meaning ingredient questions go directly to the person who cooked the food — no intermediary needed.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, solo-run neighbourhood café in a quiet residential-commercial area west of the station; no English signage or menu has been reported, so basic Japanese or a translation app is recommended.

places.name: ベーカリー燈 (Bakery Lamp)
places.gf_detail: This is a wheat-specialist bakery: every product is made with wheat flour (100% Hokkaido "Haruyu Taka Blend" for bagels; wheat also in all sourdough and pastry items). No gluten-free products are offered and there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation area. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or a gluten intolerance.
places.vegan_detail: Most of the natural-yeast bagels are made without eggs or dairy (the plain bagel allergen list shows only wheat), making them the clearest vegan option. Many flavoured bagels, however, include cream cheese, butter, or honey, and sourdough loaves often contain dairy. Several sourdough breads are egg-free but include dairy. Burgers use beef and standard buns; no vegan burger option is listed. Always confirm individually at the counter, as the allergen label per item is your best guide.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 10:00–19:00, Sun 10:00–18:00, closed Mon and the 2nd Tue (if a holiday, closed the following day)
places.neighborhood: 本町 (Honmachi)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Kawahara Aya originally trained in cosmetology before pivoting to the food industry, working in restaurants where she built skills in cooking and desserts. She then moved into bread-making, discovering that it let her apply every culinary skill she had accumulated. She and her hu

chef_bio.origin: Opened April 2010 in a formerly disused building just off Otemae-dori, a 5-minute walk from Himeji Castle. A rainbow umbrella marks the alley entrance. The name 燈 (lamp) reflects Kawahara's wish to create a place with the warmth and glow of a lantern — 'a space where customers feel excited.'
chef_bio.background: Kawahara Aya originally trained in cosmetology before pivoting to the food industry, working in restaurants where she built skills in cooking and desserts. She then moved into bread-making, discovering that it let her apply every culinary skill she had accumulated. She and her husband made repeated research trips to the United States to study New York-style hamburgers before opening their Himeji shop in April 2010.
chef_bio.philosophy: Kawahara's core principle is 'Make bread that I want to eat myself. ' She sources seasonal vegetables from young local Himeji farmers, sometimes commissioning specific crops. She experiments with fermented ingredients including kefir and house-cultivated yeast starters, and prioritises bringing out the quality of each ingredient over complexity.
chef_bio.specialty: Natural-yeast bagels (20+ rotating varieties) made with 100% Hokkaido 'Haruyu Taka Blend' wheat and Hoshino natural yeast for a characteristic chewiness; and NY-style hamburgers featuring coarse-ground Australian beef patties on housemade buns.

anecdotes.text: Kawahara notes that hearing 'delicious!' from customers is what sustains her through the hardships of running the bakery — 'every time I hear it, I feel saved.'
The shop's proprietor reportedly declined to be photographed for a local Himeji blog article ahead of opening the second location, but was described by the writer as 'notably handsome.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Every item in the shop is made with wheat flour. There is no gluten-free preparation area or gluten-free product line. Cross-contamination with wheat is a certainty throughout the kitchen and counter.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce is listed as an ingredient in any bread or bagel product; the primary allergen across all items is wheat flour itself.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bagels are baked alongside dairy-containing varieties in the same oven. Plain and some unflavoured bagels may share trays with cheese or butter bagels. No dedicated vegan production line is indicated.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergy protocol or staff allergy-training information was found. Each product does carry an allergen label on the website, which is the only formal allergen disclosure available. Visitors with serious allergies should ask staff to point to the relevant label.

positives.text: Most plain natural-yeast bagels list only wheat in their allergen declaration — no eggs or dairy — making them one of the clearer egg-free and dairy-free options in Himeji for non-celiac visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: Casual neighbourhood bakery with a small eat-in area; no English menu but pointing at items on the counter works fine, and staff are accustomed to tourists visiting from Himeji Castle.
