places.name: 釜焼鳥本舗 おやひなや 姫路駅前店 (Kamayakitori Honpo Oyahinaya Himeji Ekimae)
places.gf_detail: This is a meat-focused izakaya with no stated gluten-free options. The chain also serves Sanuki udon (wheat noodles), creating high cross-contamination risk. Standard Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat) is used in the izakaya side dishes. No dedicated fryer information confirmed. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around bone-in chicken. No plant-based mains exist, and chicken fat and drippings are integral to the cooking method. No vegan or vegetarian options are listed or confirmed.
places.hours_raw: 13:00–22:00 (per Tabelog, as of the May 2026 opening). Another source lists 11:00–22:00, closed Sun—please verify.
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho — 3 min walk from JR Himeji Stn north exit
places.notes: Kamayakitori Honpo Oyahinaya is a bone-in grilled-chicken izakaya chain operated by Forward Ball LLC, which runs multiple formats across the Kansai region. The Himeji Ekimae branch opened on Wednesday, May 13, 2026. The chain exists to spread "honetsuki-dori" (bone-in grilled chicken), a local dish originating in Marugame, Kagawa Prefecture, nationwide, and is expanding its locations to various areas including Himeji.

chef_bio.origin: 香川県丸亀市。骨付鳥は1953年に丸亀の居酒屋「一鶴」の創業者・近藤貞一・多鶴子夫妻がハリウッド映画のローストチキンシーンからヒントを得て考案した料理が発祥とされる。
chef_bio.background: 釜焼鳥本舗 おやひなやは、関西を拠点に複数業態を展開するForward ball合同会社が運営する骨付き鳥専門居酒屋チェーン。 姫路駅前店は2026年5月13日（水）にオープン。 香川県丸亀市発祥の郷土料理「骨付鳥」を全国に広める目的で展開されており、姫路を含む各地に店舗を拡大している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 高級地鶏などの銘柄鶏にこだわらず、各地域で入手できる鶏を使うことで地域ごとの多様な解釈を大切にする。 「素材より技」という姿勢で、地元の食材資源を香川の産地から奪わないよう意識した運営方針をとる。 ペアリング推奨の日本酒「讃岐くらうでぃ」（白麹三倍量の特別な濁り酒）を各店で提供。
chef_bio.specialty: 骨付鳥「釜焼き」。 親鶏（180日以上の成鶏）と若鶏（60日未満）の2種を提供。 秘伝のスパイスで仕込み、専用釜でじっくり焼き上げる「釜焼き」製法が特徴。 スパイス調合はミリグラム単位で厳格に管理され、全店で味の一貫性を保つ。

anecdotes.text: 「骨付鳥」の起源は1953年に丸亀市の一鶴で生まれた。創業者夫妻がハリウッド映画のローストチキンに着想を得て独自開発した料理が、現在は香川県を代表する郷土料理として全国に普及している。
開店から間もない2026年5月の訪問者レビューでは「皮はパリッと香ばしく焼き上げられており、ガブリとかぶりつくと中から溢れ出す肉汁と鶏の強烈な旨味がたまらない」と5点満点の高評価が付けられた。

gf_cross_contamination.text: The chain serves Sanuki udon (wheat noodles) alongside the chicken menu. A shared kitchen environment with wheat-based noodles presents a significant cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned. Standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat is almost certainly used in izakaya side dishes and marinades throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Chicken fat and drippings are integral to the signature dish; the entire concept is meat-centered with no plant-based menu items or vegan accommodation noted anywhere.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling procedures, allergen menus, or staff training for dietary restrictions were mentioned on any official or third-party source reviewed.

positives.text: English, Chinese, and Korean menus are available, making it easier to communicate basic preferences — though allergy communication in a foreign language remains the diner's responsibility.

cultural_comfort.note: English, Chinese, and Korean menus are available, and the casual izakaya format is accessible to non-Japanese speakers, though staff may have limited English conversation ability.

places.name: すし一 (Sushi Ichi)
places.gf_detail: Traditional sushi and kaiseki means wheat is present throughout: wheat-based shoyu on nigiri and in dashi-tare, flour-thickened sauces (nitsume/tare on the signature anago), and potential tempura on kaiseki courses. The restaurant has never been marketed as gluten-free and publishes no allergen matrix. There is no dedicated GF fryer confirmed. A celiac diner must contact the restaurant in advance (phone 079-222-6961, in Japanese) to discuss tamari substitution and wheat-free dashi; success is not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: This is an omakase-style sushi and kaiseki restaurant built around fish, shellfish, and Setouchi conger eel. Dashi is almost certainly katsuobushi- or niboshi-based; standard shoyu likely contains wheat. There are no advertised vegan courses and no public mention of plant-based adaptation. A diner with strict vegan requirements should call ahead in Japanese; partial accommodation (e.g., vegetable dishes from the seasonal menu) may be possible but cannot be assumed.
places.neighborhood: Uomachi, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born 22 January 1975 in Himeji and raised partly in nature-rich Shisou City, Hyogo, Hisamune Ryuichi is the second-generation owner of すし一, which his family founded in 1970 (Showa 45). After training at respected restaurants in Kyoto and Kobe for more than two decades, he returne

chef_bio.background: Born 22 January 1975 in Himeji and raised partly in nature-rich Shisou City, Hyogo, Hisamune Ryuichi is the second-generation owner of すし一, which his family founded in 1970 (Showa 45). After training at respected restaurants in Kyoto and Kobe for more than two decades, he returned to Himeji to take over the family counter.
chef_bio.philosophy: Hisamune's guiding principle is to avoid diminishing ingredients' natural flavour while coaxing out their full umami through careful technique. He sources rice from the family's own paddies in Shisou City and uses only domestic, traceable ingredients, describing the goal as food that is 'safe and delicious' from children to the elderly.
chef_bio.specialty: Steamed wild Setouchi conger eel (蒸し穴子) nigiri — using exclusively wild-caught eel from the Seto Inland Sea, prepared by steaming rather than grilling to preserve the ingredient's delicate umami and give it a characteristically fluffy texture, finished with the house nitsume reduction.

anecdotes.text: Wild Setouchi anago now costs five to ten times more than imported varieties due to overfishing and coastal development, yet すし一 refuses to switch — the chef maintains that foreign eel from regions with inadequate sewage treatment poses food-safety risks he is unwilling to accept.
Hisamune Ryuichi is described by the restaurant itself as 'a very serious man' — a non-smoker, non-gambler who has given up alcohol on doctor's orders, dedicating himself entirely to the kitchen.
The restaurant was featured in the Michelin Guide Hyogo Prefecture edition and on Kansai TV's popular dining show 'Yoi Don', bringing wider attention to Himeji's conger-eel tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination protocols has been published. The kitchen handles wheat-based shoyu, tare, and potentially tempura batter — standard risk for a traditional sushi-kaiseki kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese shoyu (wheat-containing) is used for nigiri and dipping; the house-made nitsume (anago glaze) is soy-sauce based and therefore also contains wheat. No tamari or GF shoyu option is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi underpins the kaiseki cooking. Conger eel, sea bream, octopus, and other marine proteins are handled throughout the kitchen with no plant-only preparation zone documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant states it aims for 'safe and reliable meals' for all ages and uses domestic traceable ingredients, but publishes no formal allergen matrix or allergy protocol. Same-day phone reservations are accepted, making it possible to communicate needs in advance.

positives.text: Exclusive use of wild-caught Seto Inland Sea conger eel (no farmed or imported substitutes) and rice from the owner's own paddies — traceability of key ingredients is unusually strong for a restaurant of this type.

cultural_comfort.note: Fully private tatami rooms, Japanese-language menus; staff are attentive but English communication is limited — phone ahead or use a translation app for special dietary requests.

places.name: 姫路 タコピィ (Himeji Takopii)
places.gf_detail: Takoyaki and akashi-yaki batter is made with wheat flour; this is a core, non-substitutable ingredient. The dashi may also contain soy sauce (wheat). Other items on the menu — soba-yaki (wheat noodles), omurice — also contain gluten. There are no gluten-free menu options and no dedicated GF preparation area. Not safe for celiac or wheat-allergy diners.
places.vegan_detail: Every menu item depends on animal products. The signature akashi-yaki batter contains egg. The dashi dipping broth is made from four fish-based stocks — sand eel, bonito, skipjack tuna, and mackerel. The ika-yaki is grilled squid. No plant-based substitutions are available at this counter.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–20:00 (follows the operating schedule of GranFesta)
places.neighborhood: Himeji Station (姫路駅) underground shopping arcade Gran Festa
places.notes: Originally founded in 1959 under the name タコピア (Takopia) in the basement of the Himeji Station building, the shop spent six decades as one of Himeji's most beloved soul-food counters. Longtime fixture Matsumoto Emiko — affectionately known as a local 'obachan' personality — worke

roles: Operated by Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社)
Head cook position requires a minimum three months of dedicated training
chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo Prefecture; originally 1959 in the old Himeji Station basement, current location since 2020
chef_bio.background: Originally founded in 1959 under the name タコピア (Takopia) in the basement of the Himeji Station building, the shop spent six decades as one of Himeji's most beloved soul-food counters. Longtime fixture Matsumoto Emiko — affectionately known as a local 'obachan' personality — worked there for decades and became inseparable from the shop's identity. After the original operator retired following a bereavement in late 2019, Himeji's Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a company already famous across the region for 'eki-soba' railway noodles, took over the recipe and tradition. They relaunched the counter in March 2020 as タコピィ (Takopii) — essentially the same name with one syllable changed — in the Gran Festa underground arcade where it still operates today.
chef_bio.philosophy: Preserve the 60-year-old taste exactly as it was, down to the ticket vending machine format and the four-fish dashi blend that is never made from commercial stock concentrate.
chef_bio.specialty: Akashi-yaki-style takoyaki: fluffy balls of yamato-yam-enriched batter around octopus, first brushed with Worcestershire-style sauce and then dipped into a four-stock dashi — the combination eating style that the shop's customers invented organically and that has since become 'Himeji style'

anecdotes.text: The famous double-dip eating style — sauce first, then dashi — was not invented by the shop. The founder placed sauce bottles on tables so customers could choose between sauce or broth. Eventually some patrons started doing both, and that habit slowly became the house norm.

At peak season the counter sells over 1,500 portions a day, with queues forming outside the arcade entrance. The head-cook role is physically demanding enough that repeated hand movements reportedly cause tendonitis or permanent changes to finger shape.

When Maneki Foods took over, one of the first challenges was sourcing the same model of ticket-vending machine that dispenses plastic meal cards — the supplier was difficult to find, but staff insisted on keeping the format identical to what longtime regulars expected.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is a compact open counter producing wheat-flour takoyaki continuously all day. There is no separation of gluten-containing and gluten-free cooking surfaces; cross-contact is certain.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No ingredient list is published, but standard Japanese takoyaki batter and dashi tsuyu both typically incorporate wheat-containing soy sauce. The shop has not disclosed a wheat-free soy sauce substitute.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi broth is made from four fish stocks (sand eel, bonito, skipjack tuna, mackerel) and is present on every table and in every order. There is no vegan dashi option and no way to avoid fish contact.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, staff allergy protocol, or allergy inquiry option has been noted in any review or official source. The counter operates a high-volume self-service ticket machine format with limited staff interaction.

positives.text: Extremely straightforward menu — only a handful of items — so what you see is what you get; no hidden complex sauces beyond the house dashi and Worcestershire-style sauce.

cultural_comfort.note: Ticket-vending-machine ordering, counter service, all Japanese signage; staff interaction is minimal but the format is intuitive once you understand the machine.

places.name: 炭焼あなご やま義 (Sumiyaki Anago Yamayoshi)
places.gf_detail: All core dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) and a proprietary tare sauce brushed repeatedly on the eel during grilling — wheat exposure is certain in the sauce. Dashi broth served alongside is fish-based but likely prepared with standard (wheat) soy sauce. Anago tempura on the menu is battered (wheat). No gluten-free claims or adaptations are mentioned anywhere. Celiacs should not eat here without detailed staff consultation, and cross-contamination in a 14-seat open kitchen is a real risk.
places.vegan_detail: Entirely an eel/fish specialist. Every dish centres on conger eel (anago). Dashi broth is fish-based. There are no plant-based or vegan options.
places.neighborhood: 駅前町 / 山陽姫路駅近く (Omizo-dori shopping arcade, 3-minute walk fro
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Born 1985 in Hyogo Prefecture. Began cooking at an Italian restaurant in Himeji, gaining experience across Italian, pastry, and Japanese cuisine before joining Yamayoshi in 2018.

chef_bio.origin: Claimed locally as Himeji's first anago-meshi (conger eel rice bowl) specialty restaurant, operating inside the Omizo-dori covered shopping arcade a short walk from Himeji Station. The shop has built a following over roughly twelve years and is frequently featured on regional TV.
chef_bio.background: Born 1985 in Hyogo Prefecture. Began cooking at an Italian restaurant in Himeji, gaining experience across Italian, pastry, and Japanese cuisine before joining Yamayoshi in 2018.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uncompromising sourcing — only fresh raw eel (rare among Himeji shops) — and the belief that exceptional ingredients, treated simply, bring smiles to diners.
chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled conger eel (炭焼あなご) — each eel grilled individually over charcoal, brushed 2–4 times with a house tare built up from a long-standing stock, then steamed to finish. Signature anago-wappa presents three preparations: sweetly-simmered tsukudani, soft-steamed, and charcoal-grilled, served over tea-seasoned rice with dashi soup and pickles.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor has stated: 'I want people to taste the true deliciousness of anago, so I spare no compromise in selecting premium rice, eel, and all other ingredients.' (本来のあなごの美味しさを食べてもらいたい気持ちで、妥協なく厳選されたお米やあなごを初めとする素材にこだわって作っています)

The anago-wappa is designed to be eaten three ways: plain with the house tare, with wasabi for contrast, and finally as a tea-over-rice (ochazuke) with the dashi soup poured over the remains — a tasting ritual unique to the restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: 14-seat open kitchen with no separation between preparation areas; no information about dedicated fryers or wheat-free cooking zones.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House tare — applied repeatedly to eel during charcoal grilling — is made with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat. Dashi soup served alongside also likely uses wheat-containing soy sauce. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are fish/eel-based; the kitchen has no plant-based cooking track and vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling information is published. Given the tiny size (14 seats) and single-specialty format, detailed allergy inquiries would need to be made by phone in Japanese before visiting.

positives.text: Single-ingredient specialty kitchen (conger eel only) means no cross-contact from unrelated proteins or cuisines found in multi-concept restaurants.

cultural_comfort.note: A beloved local institution in a covered shotengai; staff are welcoming but the restaurant is very small and popular, so basic Japanese communication helps — no English menu confirmed.

places.name: ビストロ ウスケ (Bistro Usuke)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information found. French bistro cooking routinely uses wheat flour in roux, pan sauces, bread courses, and pasta. With only ~10 seats and set course menus, cross-contamination risk is significant and celiac safety cannot be assumed. Inquire in Japanese before booking — confirmation of safe preparation is not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: Classic French bistro cooking centered on meat, fish, butter, and cream — no vegan menu or options noted anywhere. Hidden animal products are the norm in French sauces (stocks, butter, cream). Inquiry in Japanese before booking is essential; customization is unlikely in a 10-seat course-only format.
places.hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu-Sun 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 13:00), 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 20:00); Wed closed. Dinner availability may vary by date — call ahead.
places.neighborhood: 神屋町 (Kamiyacho), ~12 min walk north of Himeji Station near A
places.notes: Owner-chef Uchida is originally from Tokyo and trained in French cuisine at multiple establishments before relocating to Himeji following his marriage. He pursued his longstanding goal of running his own restaurant in his wife's hometown, opening Bistro Usuke on July 27, 2012. Af

chef_bio.background: Owner-chef Uchida is originally from Tokyo and trained in French cuisine at multiple establishments before relocating to Himeji following his marriage. He pursued his longstanding goal of running his own restaurant in his wife's hometown, opening Bistro Usuke on July 27, 2012. After nearly a decade at the original location in 北平野, the restaurant relocated in June 2021 to its current intimate space in 神屋町, near Aclie Himeji.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen's guiding belief is that pursuing genuine deliciousness ultimately leads back to seasonal, fresh ingredients. Uchida sources from trusted local suppliers and adapts the menu continuously to what is best at the market rather than fixing a permanent carte.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal French-Italian course meals built around fresh, locally sourced ingredients — fish preparations such as flounder carpaccio and fresh oysters appear in documented menus.

anecdotes.text: When searching for the new premises, the chef found the process difficult but formed valuable connections along the way. The distinctive animal-motif cutlery rests from the original location were carried over to the new space as a continuing tradition.

cultural_comfort.note: Phone reservation in Japanese is required; no English-language interface or international outreach found, though the cuisine style is Western.

places.name: 紀の川 (Kinokawa)
places.gf_detail: The sashimi itself uses no wheat, but garnishes and simmered dishes use ordinary soy sauce (contains wheat). If there are tempura or fried items, wheat flour is used. There is no record of a dedicated fryer or gluten accommodation. Celiacs need to confirm, and there is no guarantee they can be accommodated. Assessment: not reliably celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: A traditional washoku/sashimi specialty shop built on bonito-flake and kombu dashi. The menu centers on soy-sauce-based preparations (e.g., water eggplant with shiso, ginger, and soy sauce) and seafood, with no record of or track record for plant-based accommodation. Ordering without dashi or soy sauce is realistically difficult. Assessment: not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 18:00– (closing time unknown), closed Sun
places.neighborhood: Honmachi area (south side of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The sashimi itself uses no wheat, but garnishes and simmered dishes use ordinary soy sauce (contains wheat). If there are tempura or fried items, wheat flour is used. There is no record of a dedicated fryer or gluten accommodation. Celiacs need to confirm, and there is no guarantee they can be accommodated.

chef_bio.specialty: 新鮮な刺身・活魚料理（活アジ造り、紀の川盛など）

anecdotes.text: かつて訪れたビジネス客が「フレッシュで美味しい刺身、牡蠣、珍しい魚（ぬれそれ）、踊るタコ、〆のラーメンまで全て最高」と評した。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテン交差汚染に関する情報は一切公開されていない。刺身中心の業態だが、厨房での小麦使用状況は不明。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油ベースの調理が複数メニューに使われており（水ナスしそしょうが正油など）、通常の小麦入り醤油が使用されていると考えられる。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 鰹節・魚介だしを使用した伝統的な日本料理店。植物性への配慮・切り替えに関する記録はなし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応・グルテンフリー対応についての記載は食べログ・ヒトサラ・エキテンいずれにも見当たらない。訪問前に電話（079-223-1129）で直接確認を推奨。

positives.text: 刺身そのものは基本的に無添加・小麦不使用で提供されることが多く、醤油を避ければ選べる品がある可能性はある。

cultural_comfort.note: 小さな地元向け割烹居酒屋で、英語メニューや外国語対応の記載は一切なく、日本語のみの環境。

places.name: 吉香麻辣燙 (Kikkou Maala Tang)
places.gf_detail: The mala tang soup is likely beef-bone-based and uses Chinese soy sauce and seasonings, and soy sauce commonly contains wheat. The noodles are standard wheat noodles (availability of gluten-free noodles unconfirmed). There is no official information on gluten-free accommodation or a dedicated cooking environment. Celiacs and those with severe wheat allergy must confirm directly before visiting. Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: Ingredients can be chosen from about 60 varieties including vegetables and mushrooms, but the base soup is beef-bone broth (using about 20 kinds of medicinal herbs), so it does not accommodate full veganism. Tomato mala tang and soupless mala ban are also offered, but whether they are free of animal-based broth is unconfirmed. Those wanting vegan accommodation must confirm directly in advance. Assessment: not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (last order 21:30), open daily (no regular closing day)
places.neighborhood: Sakamotocho, along National Route 2 (southwest of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A genuine mala tang specialty shop—Himeji's first—run by a chef who spent 10 years training in authentic mala tang in Weihai, China. Opened April 2026. It sits in a historic location near Shirasagi Bridge, across from the site of Himeji Castle's Uzumi Gate.

chef_bio.background: 中国・威海市で10年にわたり本場の麻辣湯修行を積んだシェフが手がける、姫路初出店の本格麻辣湯専門店。 2026年4月にオープン。 白鷺橋そば、姫路城の埋門跡向かいという歴史的なロケーションに位置する。
chef_bio.philosophy: 本場10年の修行で培った本格的なシビ辛体験を、自分好みにカスタマイズできる形で提供する。
chef_bio.specialty: 牛骨スープに約20種類の中国産漢方薬味を加えた麻辣湯。 四川山椒（花椒）の清涼感ある痺れと唐辛子の辛みが調和した本場の味。 約60種類の具材からセルフで選ぶスタイル。

anecdotes.text: オープン前から行列ができるほどの注目を集め、プレオープン時には半額クーポンを提供。中国人スタッフも在籍し、本場の雰囲気を醸し出している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: セルフ式でお客が具材をトレーに盛るスタイルのため、小麦含有食品（小麦麺・揚げパンなど）との交差汚染リスクが考えられる。専用グルテンフリー調理環境の情報なし。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 中国式の麻辣湯スープには醤油・豆板醤等の調味料が使用される可能性が高く、これらには通常小麦が含まれる。スープの具体的な原材料リストは公開されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: ベーススープは牛骨出汁であるため、同じ鍋・調理器具で野菜具材を調理する場合、ビーガン非対応となる。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応・グルテンフリー対応に関する公式情報は一切公開されていない。来店前に電話（080-5301-5588）またはX（@umaimaratan）で確認することを強く推奨する。

positives.text: 約60種類の具材から自分で選べるセルフスタイルのため、野菜・きのこ類のみを選択することはできる。辛さは5段階から調整可能。

cultural_comfort.note: 中国人オーナー・スタッフによる運営で、日本語対応は可能だが英語メニューや英語スタッフは期待しにくい。中国語堪能なスタッフも在籍。

places.name: 佳肴 ひさ松 (Kakou Hisamatsu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free information is disclosed anywhere. Standard kappo: wheat soy sauce is almost certainly used in grilled and simmered dishes; tempura batter and breaded items (wagyu katsu, oyster fry) contain wheat. Fish dashi is standard. Dedicated fryer status unknown. Guests with celiac disease should call ahead and inquire carefully — accommodation is not guaranteed.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu is built around seafood — sashimi, conger eel, fugu, oysters — with standard fish-based dashi throughout. No plant-based or vegan-friendly course is offered or advertised.
places.hours_raw: Tue–Sat 17:30–22:00, closed Sun, Mon & holidays
places.neighborhood: 塩町 (Shiocho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small hidden kappo (隠れ家割烹) tucked into the Leo Building on Shiocho, a short walk from Himeji Station. The chef selects seasonal ingredients daily and tailors the meal to guests' preferences, offering everything from sake-pairing small plates to full kaiseki courses.

chef_bio.background: A small hidden kappo (隠れ家割烹) tucked into the Leo Building on Shiocho, a short walk from Himeji Station. The chef selects seasonal ingredients daily and tailors the meal to guests' preferences, offering everything from sake-pairing small plates to full kaiseki courses.
chef_bio.philosophy: Seasonal ingredients, honest conversation across the counter, and flexible hospitality for couples, families, and business diners alike.
chef_bio.specialty: Conger eel (穴子/anago) — served both in salt-grilled and white-grilled preparations — alongside fresh sashimi platters and seasonal seafood from Seto Inland Sea waters.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note the chef is friendly and willing to prepare off-menu items on request, reflecting the counter-kappo ethos of cooking to each guest's mood.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items — squid tempura, fugu fry, oyster fry, wagyu katsu — are all listed on the menu, making shared fryer cross-contamination a realistic concern. No dedicated fryer information is disclosed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-based soy sauce is standard in kappo cuisine and is expected in grilled (yakimono) and simmered dishes. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi (likely bonito/katsuo or kombu-bonito blend) is standard in Japanese kappo; no plant-based dashi option is mentioned. Virtually all dishes will contain animal products.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy policy or staff allergy-handling procedures are published. The chef is known to accommodate special requests when informed in advance — contact by phone (079-288-1302) before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: A small hidden kappo with counter seating and traditional private rooms; Japanese-speaking environment with no English menu or signage evident, though the chef is noted as personable and approachable.

places.name: おでん いち佳 (Oden Ichika)
places.gf_detail: Himeji oden uses standard Japanese soy sauce (含む小麦 — contains wheat) both in the broth and as the table condiment (ginger soy sauce). A house condiment called "karazou" (唐三), a fermented chili-soy paste, also contains wheat via soy sauce. Tsumire fish balls may include wheat-based binders. No gluten-free accommodation, dedicated equipment, or allergy awareness noted in any source. Not safe for celiacs.
places.vegan_detail: Traditional oden broth is dashi-based — almost certainly made with konbu and katsuo (bonito), making it non-vegan. Himeji oden is served with ginger soy sauce as a condiment, which adds fish-adjacent dashi flavour to the broth. Tsumire (fish-paste balls) and other fish-derived ingredients are mainstays of the 30-item pot. No plant-based or vegan options identified in any source.
places.hours_raw: 17:30–22:00, closed Sun & holidays, with closures in August
places.neighborhood: 白銀町 (Shirogane-cho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] いち佳 occupies a low-key spot in a back alley of Himeji's 白銀町 drinking district, inside the Nonaka Building — a location that previously housed another oden counter called Nakamoto. The current proprietress, a woman reportedly with prior experience at a Himeji hotel, took it on and

chef_bio.background: いち佳 occupies a low-key spot in a back alley of Himeji's 白銀町 drinking district, inside the Nonaka Building — a location that previously housed another oden counter called Nakamoto. The current proprietress, a woman reportedly with prior experience at a Himeji hotel, took it on and built a tight circle of regulars who fill the roughly six counter seats on most evenings.
chef_bio.philosophy: A "empty the pot" ethos: the proprietress replenishes ingredients throughout the evening so the pot is always fresh, and she pairs the food with local Hyogo sake such as Naruto Tai and Toibijin.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji-style oden: about 30 rotating ingredients simmered in a soy-flavoured kombu dashi and eaten with ginger soy sauce — the regional hallmark. Yuba, tsumire, ginkgo nuts, roasted spring onions, and lotus root appear regularly.

anecdotes.text: A blogger notes that the spot occupies the former address of a predecessor oden shop called Nakamoto, and that the current proprietress seems to have inherited some of its long-standing regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All-in-one communal oden pot with no separation between ingredients; wheat-containing items such as tsumire (which may contain binders) and soy-sauce-marinated components share the same broth.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used both in the oden broth and in the Himeji-style ginger soy sauce condiment served at the table.
The house condiment 'karazou' (唐三) is described as a fermented chili-soy paste, meaning it also contains wheat via soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito-based dashi is the foundation of the oden broth; fish-paste tsumire and other animal-derived ingredients share the pot with any vegetable items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy or dietary accommodation information was found in any source. The restaurant operates as a solo-run counter with no documented awareness of gluten or vegan requirements.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny counter with a regular local crowd; no English signage or menu, and the solo proprietress runs everything alone — basic Japanese helps a lot.

places.name: 貝屋 マルホウ (Kaiya Maruhou)
places.gf_detail: The restaurant specializes in grilled, steamed, and raw shellfish — the core proteins are naturally gluten-free. However, karaage (fried oysters / カキフライ) are battered with wheat flour and feature prominently on the menu, including the popular lunch sets. Shoyu (wheat-based soy sauce) is standard in Japanese izakaya seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce is mentioned anywhere. Dashi sources are unspecified. No dedicated fryer information is available; fried items and grilled items likely share kitchen space. Celiac diners should call ahead and focus strictly on raw (生牡蠣) or simply steamed/grilled shellfish (浜焼き) with no sauces — but cross-contamination risk is unconfirmed and staff allergy protocols are not documented.
places.vegan_detail: This is a shellfish and seafood izakaya with oysters as its core identity. Virtually all dishes center on oysters, clams, shrimp, scallops, and other marine life. A small number of meat dishes are listed for non-shellfish diners, but there are no plant-based offerings and dashi/broth in Japanese-style cooking here would almost certainly contain bonito or other fish stock. Vegans have no viable options.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sun, holidays, and days before holidays: 11:00–22:00 (food LO 21:30, drinks LO 21:30)
places.neighborhood: Station-front area (Grand Festa underground mall)
places.notes: Kaiya Maruhou is a shellfish-specialty seafood izakaya opened in Bangai 6 of the Grand Festa underground mall in front of Himeji Station, as a directly managed outlet of Yoshida Suisan, based in Banshu Murotsu (Mitsucho, Tatsuno, Hyogo). Because the fishery company runs it directly, it has a system for offering just-caught, fresh oysters and shellfish sourced daily from Murotsu Port at direct-from-source prices.

chef_bio.origin: 兵庫県姫路市（姫路駅直結グランフェスタ地下街）
chef_bio.background: 貝屋 マルホウは播州室津（兵庫県たつの市御津町）を拠点とする吉田水産の直営店として姫路駅前地下街グランフェスタ6番街に出店した貝専門の海鮮居酒屋。 水産会社が直接運営することで、室津港から毎日仕入れる獲れたて・新鮮な牡蠣や貝類を産地直送価格で提供できる体制を持つ。
chef_bio.philosophy: 「貝に出会えてよかった」をキャッチコピーに掲げ、水産会社直営の強みを活かして産地から食卓まで最短距離で新鮮な貝を届けることを理念とする。 生簀で活かしたまま仕入れ、注文を受けてから調理する鮮度重視のスタイル。
chef_bio.specialty: 浜焼きスタイルで提供される播州室津産の牡蠣。 テーブルで自ら焼く体験型の浜焼きセットと、90分食べ放題（11月〜5月）が看板。 生牡蠣、蒸し牡蠣、カキフライ、アヒージョなど幅広い調理法で展開する。

anecdotes.text: ランチの牡蠣定食（¥1,000）を注文するとInstagramフォローで蒸し牡蠣2個が無料サービスされる。「信じられない量」と地元ブロガーが驚くほどのコストパフォーマンスが話題。
姫路駅地下街という立地のため雨の日でもアクセス可能で、駅から徒歩1分という利便性がビジネスマンや旅行者に支持されている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: カキフライ（小麦粉パン粉衣）がメニューの主力であり、同じ厨房でグリル・生食・揚げ物が並行して提供される。専用フライヤーの有無は非公開。小麦を使用しない浜焼き・生牡蠣との調理動線上の分離も未確認。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 醤油・たれ類の使用が想定されるが、グルテンフリー醤油（タマリ等）の提供は公式サイト・各種予約サイトいずれにも記載なし。焼き貝に添えるタレに小麦含有醤油が使われる可能性が高い。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 全メニューが海鮮ベース。植物性食材との分離調理の概念はこの業態では想定しにくく、ヴィーガン対応の実績・表記なし。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応・スタッフへの指示プロトコルについていずれの媒体にも記載なし。セリアック・重篤アレルギーの場合は来店前に電話（079-262-6325）で直接確認が必須。

positives.text: 生牡蠣・蒸し牡蠣・浜焼き（単品でソースなし）は本来グルテンフリーの調理法。水産会社直営で添加物を使わない素材重視のコンセプトは、シンプルに食べられる料理を頼む場合にはプラス材料。

cultural_comfort.note: Menu and signage are in Japanese only; no English-language materials noted, though the station-adjacent location means staff may have basic tourist experience.

places.name: はりかい 姫路城店 (Harikai Himeji-jo-ten)
places.gf_detail: A signature item is fish and chips (フィッシュアンドチップス), which is almost certainly wheat-battered. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is standard in Japanese seafood preparations. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen protocols are documented. Celiacs should ask carefully before ordering; dedicated GF safety cannot be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is seafood-centric — grilled oysters, raw oysters, octopus rice, conger eel, fish and chips. No documented vegan dishes. Hidden fish dashi is extremely likely throughout. Visitors should inquire directly, though options are expected to be very limited.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00 (irregular days off)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi 68, Nino Yashiki (inside Himeji Park, along Otemae-dori)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The shop is operated by 有限会社播磨海洋牧場 (Harima Marine Ranch Co., Ltd.), an aquaculture and seafood company founded in 2001 by Akihiro Mukai. After years in fish wholesale and online seafood sales (launched 2004), the company opened this Himeji Castle-front storefront in 2011 to bring

chef_bio.background: The shop is operated by 有限会社播磨海洋牧場 (Harima Marine Ranch Co. , Ltd. ), an aquaculture and seafood company founded in 2001 by Akihiro Mukai. After years in fish wholesale and online seafood sales (launched 2004), the company opened this Himeji Castle-front storefront in 2011 to bring their catch directly to consumers and tourists.
chef_bio.philosophy: The company motto is 'challenge without fearing failure' (失敗を恐れずに挑戦する精神). Mukai-san aims to shorten the chain between sea and table, combining sustainable aquaculture with direct retail so visitors to the World Heritage castle can taste the same fish landed by Harima fishermen.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal fresh seafood from the Harima-nada (播磨灘) stretch of the Seto Inland Sea — especially their own-brand 播磨の白珠 (Harima White Pearl) virgin oysters, prized for low bitterness, plus grilled oysters, raw oysters, octopus rice balls, conger eel kabayaki, and fish and chips.

anecdotes.text: Japan's Ministry of Agriculture (近畿農政局) selected はりかい姫路城店 as a model case study for inbound tourism best practices — one of very few small food stalls in the Kinki region to receive this recognition.

TripAdvisor reviewers ranked it #1 of 67 bars and pubs in Himeji, with repeated praise for Mukai-san personally working the counter alone during busy periods while still managing to engage foreign visitors warmly.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fish and chips is a signature menu item and almost certainly uses wheat batter; no dedicated fryer is documented. Cross-contamination from shared frying oil is plausible.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is the baseline seasoning in a Japanese seafood bar. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are documented at this establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen handles fish, shellfish, eel, and octopus exclusively. No plant-only preparation area is documented; vegan cross-contact is highly probable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner speaks English and is described as attentive and personally engaged with customers. While no formal allergy protocol is documented, the owner's language ability means direct communication about dietary needs is more feasible than at most casual seafood stalls.

positives.text: English-speaking owner makes it possible to have a real conversation about ingredients, which is significantly better than the norm for casual Japanese seafood bars.
English photo menu is available, reducing ordering uncertainty for foreign visitors.

cultural_comfort.note: The owner speaks English and the shop provides a photo menu in English; it was specifically featured by Japan's Ministry of Agriculture as a model for welcoming international visitors at Himeji Castle.

places.name: 喫茶ラヴリ (Kissa Lavuri)
places.gf_detail: The kitchen fries breaded chicken cutlets and karaage in the same oil as everything else — no dedicated gluten-free fryer. Signature dishes are heavily wheat-dependent: breaded katsu, fried rice with standard soy sauce (wheat-containing), napolitan spaghetti, curry roux. No allergen menu or GF claims appear on any source. Not appropriate for celiac or serious wheat allergy.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat-centric dishes: signature chicken katsu fried rice, karaage, ginger pork, omelette rice, and mapo tofu. No plant-based or dairy-free options are identified anywhere. Hidden animal products (dashi, lard, egg in fried batters) are highly probable throughout.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30) as Kissa Lavuri; in the evening it transforms into bistro bar YORU NO MARUMASA 16:00–23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Irregular days off.
places.neighborhood: 駅前地下街グランフェスタ (JR Himeji Station Underground)
places.notes: Operated by 株式会社ATE-LINK (MARUMASA Group), a restaurant operator active across the Harima area of Hyogo Prefecture. The concept draws lineage from 喫茶ラヴリ, a beloved mega-portion café originally established in Fukusaki Town, roughly 30 minutes north of Himeji, known for its faithfu

roles: operator: 株式会社ATE-LINK / MARUMASA Group
Instagram manager: 高橋恵美 (Takahashi Emi)
chef_bio.origin: Fukusaki Town, Hyogo Prefecture (original location); Himeji branch opened March 20, 2026
chef_bio.background: Operated by 株式会社ATE-LINK (MARUMASA Group), a restaurant operator active across the Harima area of Hyogo Prefecture. The concept draws lineage from 喫茶ラヴリ, a beloved mega-portion café originally established in Fukusaki Town, roughly 30 minutes north of Himeji, known for its faithful following among locals who prize value and volume over refinement.
chef_bio.philosophy: Volume as a virtue: every item is made to order from scratch — sauces, dressings, batters all housemade — on the principle that generous portions and careful preparation are equally important. Reusable containers (¥50) are offered for those who cannot finish.
chef_bio.specialty: チキンカツ焼き飯 — a thick house-breaded chicken cutlet laid over 400g of fried rice with housemade char siu, the whole plate tipping the scales above 1 kg. The challenge item 'ラヴリスペシャル' reaches approximately 2 kg.

anecdotes.text: The Himeji branch doubles as a completely different restaurant after 3 pm: lighting drops, the menu pivots to roast beef, bruschetta, and mapo tofu, and the space reopens as YORU NO MARUMASA — a bistro bar sharing the same underground address.

When the Fukusaki original first opened under the name 喫茶ラヴリ, its 'bug-level' (バグ盛り) portions quickly made it a regional talking point; the Himeji outpost carries the same outsized ambition into the heart of the city.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen fries breaded chicken cutlets (panko-coated), karaage, and other wheat-battered items together. No source mentions a separate fryer or GF protocols.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is almost certainly used in fried rice, sauces, and marinades; no tamari or GF soy sauce alternative is mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes observed involve meat, egg, or dairy. The kitchen is not set up for plant-based preparation; cross-contact with animal products in every dish is essentially certain.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy-handling protocol, or staff training for dietary restrictions is mentioned in any source. The restaurant does not market itself to diners with dietary needs.

cultural_comfort.note: Underground kissaten catering to local commuters and shoppers; no English signage or staff language accommodation indicated, but the casual counter-seat format is welcoming to solo diners.

places.name: 姫路タンメン (Himeji Tanmen)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat noodles are the core of every dish. The soup is seasoned with conventional soy sauce (wheat-containing) via a table-side ginger-soy condiment. No gluten-free noodle substitute or tamari option has been identified. Gyoza wrappers and karaage batter add further wheat exposure. This shop is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a clear chintan made from chicken and pork stock. No plant-based broth option is listed. Gyoza and karaage side dishes also contain animal products. The restaurant does not advertise any vegan menu items.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:30), irregular days off (closed New Year's Day)
places.neighborhood: 駅前町 (Himeji Station north exit, 1-minute walk)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] 姫路タンメン opened in February 2017 a one-minute walk from JR Himeji Station's north exit, quickly earning a loyal local following with its vegetable-forward take on tanmen. The shop expanded with a second branch in Fukusaki (December 2019) and a third in Tohori (June 2022, closed Jan

chef_bio.background: 姫路タンメン opened in February 2017 a one-minute walk from JR Himeji Station's north exit, quickly earning a loyal local following with its vegetable-forward take on tanmen. The shop expanded with a second branch in Fukusaki (December 2019) and a third in Tohori (June 2022, closed January 2026), while the original Ekimae-cho counter remains the flagship.
chef_bio.philosophy: Volume and freshness first — every bowl packs a full day's worth of vegetables cooked at high heat in a wok to preserve crunch before being ladled into the soup.
chef_bio.specialty: The house tanmen: 360 g of stir-fried vegetables in a clear chicken-and-pork chintan broth, served with table-side minced ginger, house ginger-soy sauce, and garlic oil — the restaurant's tagline is 野菜たりてますか？ (Are you getting enough vegetables? )

anecdotes.text: When a Fukusaki branch opened in December 2019 with 80 seats and triple the menu items of the original, the Ekimae-cho flagship stayed small at 17 seats, preserving the counter-ramen intimacy.
In July 2025 the shop closed for about two weeks for interior air-conditioning work — a rare disruption for a shop that otherwise runs nearly every day of the year.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles are boiled in shared water for every bowl; karaage (fried chicken) and gyoza are also on the menu, adding further wheat exposure throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature table-side condiment is a house-made ginger soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative has been mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is chicken-and-pork chintan; animal stock is present in every soup bowl. There is no plant-based broth option on the menu.

cultural_comfort.note: Compact counter-heavy ramen shop steps from Himeji Station; no English menu known, but ordering by pointing at Japanese menu is feasible.

places.name: オーガニックダイニングvegeta (Organic Dining Vegeta)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or documented allergen protocols. The restaurant prominently uses JAS-certified organic rice and organic vegetables, which are inherently GF, but standard Japanese cooking relies on wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) in marinades, sauces, and dressings. One TripAdvisor reviewer (July 2024) noted staff were "lovely and accommodating" for a gluten-free diner, but no specifics about substitutions or cross-contamination controls were described. Celiac diners should call ahead and ask specifically about shoyu use in each dish.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is omnivore-oriented and prominently features meat dishes (pork kimchi, chicken karaage, hambagu steak). Organic vegetable sides and rice dishes may be ordered, and the lunch sets include organic salads and mixed-grain rice, but no dedicated vegan menu exists. Hidden dashi (bonito/katsuo) in miso soup and sauces is likely — ask before ordering.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu, Sun, holidays & pre-holidays 11:00–21:00; Fri & Sat 11:00–22:00 (irregular days off). Note: earlier information at opening listed two seatings (lunch 11:00–14:30, dinner 17:00–20:45), but current information on HotPepper indicates it has changed to continuous all-day service.
places.neighborhood: Around Sanyo-Himeji Stn / Omizosuji shopping street
places.notes: Owner Shimotada Masayuki brought 17 years of food-service industry experience when he opened Organic Dining Vegeta in September 2023 along Himeji's Omizo shopping arcade (おみぞ筋商店街). He identified a gap in the local market as health consciousness and dietary awareness among diners

chef_bio.background: Owner Shimotada Masayuki brought 17 years of food-service industry experience when he opened Organic Dining Vegeta in September 2023 along Himeji's Omizo shopping arcade (おみぞ筋商店街). He identified a gap in the local market as health consciousness and dietary awareness among diners began to shift.
chef_bio.philosophy: Vegetables are the main event. Shimotada sources directly from Migita Farm in Tatsuno City (a circular-agriculture operation) and a network of organic specialists nationwide, aiming to put the best seasonal produce on the plate and refine the menu continuously in response to customer feedback.
chef_bio.specialty: Creative lunch sets built around seasonal organic vegetables — such as a tofu-hijiki burger plate or salmon-and-mushroom tofu set — served with organic salad, miso soup, and a choice of brown rice or multi-grain rice.

anecdotes.text: The opening was covered by Himeji Keizai Shimbun one month after launch, highlighting that Shimotada chose the Omizo arcade location because he saw a clear need for organic-vegetable-led cuisine in central Himeji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about dedicated fryer or separate preparation areas for gluten-free orders. The kitchen serves both wheat-containing dishes (chicken karaage uses breading, hambagu steak may use breadcrumbs) and the organic vegetable dishes in the same space.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is almost certainly used in marinades, dressings, and cooked dishes. No tamari or GF shoyu alternative is documented on any source reviewed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Miso soup is served with lunch sets; dashi base (likely bonito/katsuo) is undisclosed. Meat dishes such as pork kimchi and chicken karaage are cooked in the same kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One TripAdvisor reviewer from July 2024 described staff as 'lovely and accommodating' for a gluten-free diner and called the food 'well presented', but no specifics about protocols, substitutions, or dedicated handling were given.

positives.text: All rice uses JAS-certified pesticide-free organic cultivation (milky queen or mixed grain), which is inherently gluten-free. Organic vegetable sides and salads form the core of the menu.
Ingredients are sourced direct from Migita Farm (circular agriculture, Tatsuno City) and vetted organic suppliers — likely lower additive load than conventional restaurants.

cultural_comfort.note: No English signage or menu confirmed; staff may speak limited English, but one traveller review in English suggests the visit was manageable.

places.name: 姫路 酒肴魚寿司 (Himeji Shukosaku Uozushi)
places.gf_detail: Standard wheat-based soy sauce (tamari is not listed) is used across the sushi and sashimi menu. Fried items (fried oysters, fried squid tentacles, fried octopus, croquettes, karaage) are on the menu, implying a shared fryer with wheat flour. Miso soup with sets contains dashi. No gluten-free menu, no allergen declarations published. A coeliac diner would need to confirm tamari availability and cross-contamination policy by phone (079-222-0158) before visiting — do not assume safety.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built entirely around seafood — sushi, sashimi, grilled shellfish, fried fish. Fish-based dashi underlies miso soup served with most set meals. No plant-only dishes were identified; vegan eating here is not practicable.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–23:00 (food L.O. 22:00, drink L.O. 22:30). Dec 31 until 18:00; closed Jan 1.
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho, 1 min walk from JR Himeji Stn south exit
places.notes: Opened in September 2017 inside Himeji Norengai, a covered izakaya alley occupying the first floor of the Shin Festa Building one minute from JR Himeji Station's south exit. The concept was conceived as a place where high-quality Edomae-style sushi — normally associated with form

chef_bio.background: Opened in September 2017 inside Himeji Norengai, a covered izakaya alley occupying the first floor of the Shin Festa Building one minute from JR Himeji Station's south exit. The concept was conceived as a place where high-quality Edomae-style sushi — normally associated with formal counter dining — could be enjoyed casually alongside sake in a lively, affordable neighborhood setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: Uncompromising freshness: fish is sourced daily from across Japan, and the kitchen insists on preparations unavailable elsewhere. The pairing of carefully chosen regional sake with creative sushi is central to the experience.
chef_bio.specialty: Signature pieces include 'Maguro Rossini' (seared fatty tuna with a rich topping inspired by the classic French preparation) and 'Noméru Shizen Shirozake' (dubbed 'drinkable natural white salmon'), a piece so silky and delicate diners joke they could drink it. The kitchen also offers hamayaki — table-top grilling of fresh shellfish — giving the menu a dual identity as both sushi bar and seafood izakaya.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's article introduction calls it a place where 'smiles naturally overflow' — crediting the combination of quality sushi and curated sake as the cause.
The 'Maguro Rossini' and 'drinkable natural white salmon' are flagged as dishes 'unavailable anywhere else', reflecting the kitchen's drive toward invention within the Edomae tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Fried items (fried oysters, fried squid tentacles, fried octopus, croquettes, anko karaage, beef menchi katsu) appear on the menu. A dedicated fryer separate from wheat-flour batters is not confirmed; shared-fryer cross-contamination must be assumed absent explicit confirmation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is the default seasoning for sushi and sashimi. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is listed anywhere on the menu or official sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is used in miso soup served alongside set meals. All menu sections feature seafood; no plant-only dishes identified. Cross-contact with fish products throughout the kitchen is unavoidable.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen declarations or allergy-handling policy found across Tabelog, Hotpepper, or the Festa Building shop guide. Diners with serious allergies should call 079-222-0158 before visiting.

positives.text: Fresh fish sourced daily from across Japan, prepared to order at the counter — individual pieces rather than pre-made rolls reduces some risk of hidden sauces, though soy sauce use remains standard.

cultural_comfort.note: Casual izakaya-style sushi bar in a lively station-front arcade; English menu not confirmed, but counter seating lets you point at dishes — basic Japanese or a phone translation app is helpful.

places.name: おでん処 能古 (Oden-dokoro Noko)
places.gf_detail: The signature ginger soy sauce dipping condiment is conventional Japanese shoyu, which contains wheat. Dashi uses bonito (safe) and kelp (safe), but the soy sauce applied at the table is wheat-bearing. Fish cakes and processed oden ingredients may also contain wheat starch as binders. No gluten-free options or tamari substitution are mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for celiac diners without direct confirmation from the restaurant.
places.vegan_detail: Dashi is made from Rishiri kelp and bonito flakes (katsuo), making the broth non-vegan. The ginger soy sauce dipping condiment is conventional wheat-based shoyu blended with broth. Oden ingredients include fish cakes, octopus, beef tendon, and other animal products throughout the menu. No plant-only options are indicated.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Sat 17:00–22:00 (L.O. around 21:30), closed Sun & holidays
places.neighborhood: 白銀町 (Hakugin-cho), 山陽姫路駅から徒歩2分
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A second-generation oden specialist in Himeji's Hakugin-cho drinking district, operating from the ground floor of a small neighbourhood building a 2-minute walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The current proprietress, Hitomi Ohashi, took over the shop from her mother-in-law and has c

chef_bio.origin: Opened approximately 2005; the shop's roots go back further through the founding generation.
chef_bio.background: A second-generation oden specialist in Himeji's Hakugin-cho drinking district, operating from the ground floor of a small neighbourhood building a 2-minute walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The current proprietress, Hitomi Ohashi, took over the shop from her mother-in-law and has continued its dedication to a single dish: Himeji-style oden.
chef_bio.philosophy: Quality over quantity: Ohashi-san sources every ingredient from trusted Himeji suppliers — tofu from Nishimoto Tofu Shop, freshly pounded mochi from Kineya, processed items from Hatoya, and seasonal seafood such as Harima-Nada octopus and conger eel at market price.
chef_bio.specialty: Himeji oden — around 25 varieties of seasonal ingredients simmered in a delicate kelp-and-bonito dashi and eaten with a house ginger soy sauce made by blending shoyu with cooking sake and a ladleful of the warm broth.

anecdotes.text: The kelp used for dashi has become increasingly precious; Ohashi-san adjusts grades and blends varieties to maintain consistent flavour as supplies tighten — a quiet testament to how seriously she takes the stock that underpins every bowl.

A personal creation on the menu — white cabbage wrapped around bacon ('白菜巻き') — sits alongside the traditional Himeji line-up, showing the proprietress's willingness to add her own touch without abandoning local identity.
The restaurant earned a Michelin Bib Gourmand listing in the 2016 Hyogo special edition, bringing wider attention to Himeji's ginger-soy oden tradition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination controls is available. The kitchen handles fish cakes and processed items that routinely contain wheat starch, and the shared oden pot means any wheat-bearing ingredient contaminates the broth for all diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house ginger soy sauce is described as conventional Japanese shoyu blended with cooking sake and oden broth. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat. No tamari or gluten-free shoyu option is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shared oden pot contains bonito-based dashi throughout, as well as fish cakes, beef tendon, and seafood. There is no plant-only broth or separate cooking vessel noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or allergen menu are mentioned on any source reviewed. Diners with celiac disease or severe allergies should contact the restaurant directly before visiting (tel: 079-284-7345).

positives.text: Dashi base is kelp and bonito — no hidden soy sauce in the broth itself. Individual pieces can in principle be chosen to avoid certain proteins (e.g., opting for daikon, konnyaku, or tofu pieces) if wheat in the dipping sauce is the only concern.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-heavy small oden bar in a traditional drinking district; no English menu or signage noted, and staff likely communicate primarily in Japanese.

places.name: 杵屋 姫路山陽百貨店 (Kineya Himeji Sanyo Department Store)
places.gf_detail: Udon noodles are made in-house from wheat flour — the core product is wheat. Soba noodles also carry cross-contamination risk from shared equipment. Soy sauce (used in broth and dipping sauces) contains wheat. The company's allergen documentation lists wheat as a primary allergen and states that cross-contamination during cooking cannot be fully prevented. No gluten-free noodle alternatives or wheat-free soy sauce are mentioned. This restaurant is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: Udon and soba broth at Kineya is made with katsuobushi (bonito) and kombu dashi — not vegan. Soy sauce used in seasoning contains wheat. No dedicated vegan menu is offered. Some vegetable tempura or cold noodle side items may exist but the broth base will almost certainly contain animal-derived dashi unless staff confirm a specific item otherwise.
places.neighborhood: 南町 (Minamimachi)
places.notes: Kineya is one of the flagship udon brands of Gourmet Kineya Restaurant Co. (グルメ杵屋レストラン), a large-scale Japanese noodle chain founded in 1967 (then as Ryokoku Foods). The first Kineya shop opened in 1971 inside a Daiei supermarket in Nara; the Himeji Sanyo Department Store locatio

chef_bio.origin: Osaka (corporate headquarters); Himeji branch since opening of the department store location.
chef_bio.background: Kineya is one of the flagship udon brands of Gourmet Kineya Restaurant Co. (グルメ杵屋レストラン), a large-scale Japanese noodle chain founded in 1967 (then as Ryokoku Foods). The first Kineya shop opened in 1971 inside a Daiei supermarket in Nara; the Himeji Sanyo Department Store location sits on the 6th floor of the city's main station-adjacent department store, a few steps from Sanyo-Himeji Station.
chef_bio.philosophy: Fresh noodles prepared by hand every day using wheat flour, served with a Kansai-style katsuobushi-kombu dashi broth.
chef_bio.specialty: Sanuki-style hand-pulled udon made daily on-site — staff demonstrate the noodle-making process in the open kitchen (実演手打うどん).

anecdotes.text: The parent company, Gourmet Kineya, operates 200+ locations across Japan under multiple concepts including Kineya (udon), Soji-bo (soba), and Mugi Maru, and went public on the Osaka Securities Exchange in 1989.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gourmet Kineya's corporate allergen documentation states that cross-contamination of allergens during cooking cannot be completely prevented across all menu items.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce containing wheat is used in the broth and dipping sauces. All udon noodles are made in-house from wheat flour. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative is offered.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broth is made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) and kombu dashi — no plant-based broth option is listed. Tempura items are fried in shared oil with shrimp tempura on the menu.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The company publishes a per-item allergen PDF covering the 8 mandatory Japanese allergens (wheat, buckwheat, egg, dairy, peanut, shrimp, crab, walnut), but explicitly notes that full prevention of cross-contamination cannot be guaranteed.

positives.text: Gourmet Kineya publishes a downloadable allergen chart for all menu items on their corporate website, making it possible to check each dish before ordering.

cultural_comfort.note: Standard chain restaurant inside a department store; English menus are unlikely but picture menus and counter-pointing should work for basic orders.

places.name: お食事処 千姫 (Oshokujidokoro Senhime)
places.gf_detail: Tempura and other fried foods use standard wheat-flour batter. Noodle dishes (udon) contain wheat. Soy sauce used in the anago donburi tare and other seasonings almost certainly contains wheat. Miso soup is a staple accompaniment. No gluten-free options, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation information was found on any source. This is an entirely unsafe venue for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu centers on anago (conger eel) donburi, fried/tempura dishes, and noodles — all seafood or meat-based. Miso soup accompanies most sets. No vegetarian or vegan options identified across any source. Dashi in soups and sauces is almost certainly fish-based (katsuobushi or niboshi). Hidden animal ingredients are expected throughout.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi 68, Karo Yashiki Bldg B (along Otemae-dori leading to the castle)
places.cuisine: Fried foods / conger eel / tempura / rice bowls
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small eatery that opened on May 1, 2020, at the foot of Himeji Castle in Building B of the Karo Yashiki (former samurai residence area) at Honmachi 68. Located in a corner of the historic building cluster below the castle, it serves as a lunch spot where tourists and locals drop in between castle sightseeing.

chef_bio.origin: 姫路市本町68 家老屋敷B棟
chef_bio.background: 2020年5月1日、姫路城の麓・本町68の家老屋敷B棟（かつての武家屋敷エリア）にオープンした小さな食事処。 城下の歴史的な建物群の一角に位置し、観光客や地元客が城見物の合間に立ち寄るランチスポットとして機能している。
chef_bio.specialty: 姫路の名物・穴子（アナゴ）を使った穴子丼。

gf_cross_contamination.text: 揚げ物（天ぷら・フライ）を主力とする店で、小麦粉の衣を使用する調理が常時行われている。フライヤーの共用状況は不明だが、グルテン汚染のリスクは極めて高い。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: 穴子丼のたれや各種料理には通常の醤油（小麦含有）が使用されていると推定される。グルテンフリー醤油の使用を示す情報は皆無。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 魚介（穴子・白魚）を中心とするメニュー構成で、動物性食材を使わない料理の選択肢はない。みそ汁のだしも動物性と推定される。

cultural_comfort.note: 券売機で食券を購入する日本式の食堂スタイル。英語メニューや英語スタッフの情報はなく、訪日観光客向けの対応は限定的と思われる。

places.name: カフェ ソル (Cafe SOL)
places.gf_detail: This is a bread-and-bakery café whose signature attraction is an all-you-can-eat bread spread featuring 20+ wheat-based items from sister bakery BAKERY 燈 LAMP. Burgers, bagels, bánh mì, donuts, and cinnamon rolls all contain gluten. No gluten-free alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen management information was found on any source. Not appropriate for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options identified. The menu is built around bread, butter, and meat-based items: all-you-can-eat bread from the sister bakery (includes tuna sandwiches, butter/jam spreads), NY-style beef hamburgers, chicken bánh mì, and American-style sweets. No plant-based or dairy-free alternatives mentioned on any source reviewed.
places.hours_raw: Sat, Sun & holidays 9:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:00), closed Mon–Fri
places.neighborhood: Honmachi / south of Himeji Castle
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Cafe SOL opened on February 23, 2019 as the third concept location of popular Himeji bakery BAKERY 燈 LAMP (Bakery Lamp). The name SOL is Spanish for 'sun,' reflecting the owner's intention to create a bright, relaxed space. The café occupies a wood-framed building with high ceili

chef_bio.background: Cafe SOL opened on February 23, 2019 as the third concept location of popular Himeji bakery BAKERY 燈 LAMP (Bakery Lamp). The name SOL is Spanish for 'sun,' reflecting the owner's intention to create a bright, relaxed space. The café occupies a wood-framed building with high ceilings, large windows, and a courtyard terrace — an unusual retreat tucked behind the Honmachi shopping arcade, a five-minute walk from Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Bright and comfortable; an everyday escape for castle-district locals and visitors alike, blending baked goods from the sister bakery with international café fare.
chef_bio.specialty: パン食べ放題モーニング（20種以上のパンが食べ放題）とNYスタイルのハンバーガー

anecdotes.text: The all-you-can-eat bread morning service — over 20 varieties baked fresh at BAKERY 燈 LAMP and offered in mini sizes — became the café's breakout attraction, drawing weekend queues despite its tucked-away location.
Cafe SOL was billed as 'the third location of the popular Bakery Lamp chain,' and opened in 2019 as a full sit-down café experience built around bread culture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is wheat-centric — bread, pastries, burgers, and sandwiches are the core offering. No allergen labelling or cross-contamination management information was found on any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bread spread includes butter and egg-based items; American-style sweets contain dairy. No dairy-free or vegan cross-contact protocols mentioned.

cultural_comfort.note: One early review noted English signage and a relaxed tourist-friendly atmosphere near Himeji Castle, but the café operates entirely in Japanese and no dedicated English menu or English-speaking staff is confirmed.

places.name: 未十 (Mijuu)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free certification or dedicated GF menu found. Traditional soy sauce (contains wheat) is standard in Japanese kaiseki and sushi preparations, including dipping sauce, marinades, and seasoned rice vinegar. Reviews mention dashi made from "5 types of natural kombu and bonito," which is GF, but wheat-bearing soy sauce appears in most courses. No allergen menu or documented accommodation protocol found. Celiacs should contact the restaurant in advance.
places.vegan_detail: Tabelog lists vegan/vegetarian/halal options as available, but this is a platform checkbox with no corroborating menu or review evidence. Standard kaiseki and sushi formats rely heavily on bonito dashi (katsuo), soy sauce, mirin, and fish roe — none inherently vegan. Any accommodation would need to be pre-arranged directly with the restaurant.
places.neighborhood: Uomachi, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened May 21, 2024 in the Hanamura Building in Uomachi, Himeji's historic fish-market district, a short walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The space is fitted entirely in hinoki cypress wood with an open counter kitchen designed so diners watch every step of preparation.

chef_bio.background: Opened May 21, 2024 in the Hanamura Building in Uomachi, Himeji's historic fish-market district, a short walk from Sanyo Himeji Station. The space is fitted entirely in hinoki cypress wood with an open counter kitchen designed so diners watch every step of preparation.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen focuses on drawing out the natural umami of each ingredient — sourcing fish from Nagasaki, condiments from Kyoto — and pairs roughly 20 rotating local sake varieties at a fixed price alongside the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal omakase courses built around Setouchi seafood, with a lunch sushi service (鮨 未十) and an evening kaiseki program — late night shifts to à la carte including creative dishes such as a Mijuu-style Korean-seasoned crab and a Japanese-inflected Peking duck.

anecdotes.text: A blog visit documented the signature counter atmosphere: red-vinegar shari (rice seasoned with aged red vinegar) crafted individually for each piece, with sea urchin served hand-to-mouth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No allergen menu, dedicated GF prep area, or cross-contamination protocols were found across Tabelog, HotPepper, or any review sources.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce is integral to Japanese kaiseki and sushi — used in dipping sauce, shari seasoning, and course marinades. No wheat-free soy sauce alternative (tamari/GF shoyu) is documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito (katsuo) dashi is a core building block of the menu per diner reviews; all courses likely contain fish-based stock unless a special accommodation is arranged in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No documented allergy-handling protocol or staff allergy training was found. The restaurant's online reservation notes ask guests to avoid strong fragrances, but no dietary restriction pre-screening process is mentioned.

positives.text: Dashi base is made from natural kombu and bonito — kombu portions are inherently gluten-free and iodine-rich; advance notice to the kitchen may allow adjustment of fish-stock components.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-only omakase format with a young Japanese chef working in an open kitchen; no English menu or signage noted across any sources, though online reservations via Tabelog/HotPepper are accessible to non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 十割そば 橙 (Juwari Soba Daidai)
places.gf_detail: The noodles are declared 100% buckwheat (十割そば) with no wheat flour added — a positive signal. However, soba tsuyu typically uses koikuchi soy sauce which contains wheat, and no allergen management protocols or dedicated GF claims are documented for this shop. Cross-contamination risk is unknown. Celiac diners should confirm tsuyu ingredients and kitchen practices directly before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The soba noodles themselves are 100% buckwheat and vegan, but soba tsuyu (dipping broth) at Japanese soba restaurants almost always contains dashi made from katsuobushi (bonito flakes), which is not vegan. No vegan-specific options or dashi alternatives are mentioned in any source. Ask the kitchen whether a plant-based tsuyu is possible.
places.hours_raw: Lunch only (exact times not published in any confirmed source); evening bar operation. Closed Mondays confirmed via Yahoo Maps.
places.neighborhood: 西駅前町 (Sanyo-Himeji Station area)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened April 2022 under the Sanyo Electric Railway overpass in the Nishi-Ekimae-cho district, a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The shop operates as a soba counter at lunch and shifts to a bar in the evening.

chef_bio.background: Opened April 2022 under the Sanyo Electric Railway overpass in the Nishi-Ekimae-cho district, a short walk from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The shop operates as a soba counter at lunch and shifts to a bar in the evening.
chef_bio.philosophy: Make-to-order from raw flour: customers watch their noodles born from buckwheat powder at the counter. This results in longer waits but fresher noodles.
chef_bio.specialty: Juwari soba — noodles made to order from 100% domestic buckwheat flour, kneaded and extruded by machine right in front of the customer, with strong chewy texture. Cold zaru soba with optional toppings (including plum-grated yam) is the signature.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers note that because noodles are made from scratch using a machine after each order, the wait is considerable — those without time flexibility are advised to plan accordingly.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No source confirms what soy sauce brand or style is used in the tsuyu; standard koikuchi soy sauce contains wheat. Celiac diners must ask.

positives.text: Noodles are stated to be 100% buckwheat (十割) with no wheat flour blended in, which is a prerequisite for any gluten-free consideration.

cultural_comfort.note: Tiny counter-only local shop with no detected English presence; expect Japanese-only menus and staff interaction.

places.name: 縁（エニ） (Eni)
places.gf_detail: This is a shared-kitchen space with 10+ rotating vendors; no vendor has published gluten-free menus or dedicated allergen information. The building owner is Tanabiki Seimen, a dried-noodle (somen/wheat) manufacturer, and noodle-based dishes are among the offerings. Standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-based) is likely used in obanzai cooking. No dedicated fryer status is known. Diners with celiac disease should contact the specific vendor operating on their intended visit day well in advance.
places.vegan_detail: The cafe emphasizes vegetable-forward, healthy cuisine. The anchor tenant obann (オバン) serves obanzai-style Japanese home cooking centered on organic vegetables. Individual vendors rotating through the space vary — some offer bibimbap, Dutch baby pancakes, and somen noodle dishes. Hidden dashi (fish stock) is common in obanzai and Japanese home cooking and is not ruled out here; no vendor has explicitly advertised vegan or dashi-free options. Somen noodles (a Tanabiki Seimen specialty) typically contain wheat. Ask each vendor directly on the day of your visit.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Tue 08:00–17:00 / Wed 12:00–16:00 / Thu 09:00–17:00 / Fri 11:00–17:00 / Sat 09:00–20:00 / Sun 09:00–16:00. Closed the 2nd Saturday of each month. Varies by tenant (check Instagram).
places.neighborhood: Yashiro Honmachi (5 min walk from Shimizubashi bus stop, north side of Himeji Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Eni is a traditional-house share cafe created by renovating the former head-office building of Tabiki Seimen, a long-established dried-noodle maker founded in 1897 (Meiji 30). The building is a Western-style wooden structure from the Meiji–Taisho era, and after roughly 8 months of preservation-renovation work retaining features of the time—plaster walls, stained glass (6 panels depicting the company's history), and coffered ceilings—it opened in September 2020. It won the 12th Himeji City Urban Landscape Award (architecture category).

roles: share cafe anchor tenant: obann（有機野菜おばんざい）
building owner: 田引製麺株式会社
chef_bio.background: 縁（エニ）は、明治30年（1897年）創業の老舗乾麺メーカー「田引製麺」の旧本社社屋をリノベーションした古民家シェアカフェ。 建物は明治〜大正期の洋風木造建築で、漆喰壁・ステンドグラス（6枚、同社の歴史を描く）・格天井など当時の意匠を保ちながら約8ヶ月の保存改修工事を経て2020年9月にオープン。 第12回姫路市都市景観賞（建築部門）を受賞。
chef_bio.philosophy: 建物オーナーは「先人や地域への愛情のかたち」として歴史建造物の保存を重視し、地域の人々が集まる場づくりを目指している。 食においては農薬不使用・有機野菜を重視した「体に優しい」料理を志向するテナントが多い。
chef_bio.specialty: テナント各社が日替わりでおばんざいランチ・ダッチベイビー・クロッフル・そうめん料理などを提供するシェアカフェ形式。 固定アンカーテナントのobann（オバン）は有機野菜を使ったおばんざいランチで知られる。

anecdotes.text: 田引製麺は明治30年創業の123年超の老舗乾麺メーカー。旧工場・本社を「歴史的建造物保存プロジェクト」として自社でリノベし、地域の食文化発信拠点に転換した。
2024年7月、第12回姫路市都市景観賞（建築部門）を受賞。漆喰・窓・木製建具など当時の素材を活かした改修が評価された。
2020年11月のオープン記念イベントでは子どもたちが親のためにそうめん料理を作る「キッズレストラン」を開催し、大人は姫路城見学へ招待するという地域交流型企画を実施。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Building owner Tanabiki Seimen is a wheat noodle (somen) manufacturer; noodle-based dishes are served in the space. Cross-contamination risk from wheat flour in a shared kitchen environment cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Obanzai-style Japanese home cooking (the anchor tenant's specialty) routinely uses wheat-based soy sauce and mirin. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative has been confirmed by any vendor.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vendor has published vegan certification or confirmed separation of animal and plant ingredients. Japanese dashi (fish or bonito stock) is standard in obanzai cooking and is not explicitly excluded.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu or staff allergy-handling protocol has been published by any rotating tenant. Contact the specific vendor operating on your planned visit day via Instagram (@cafe_eni_himeji) well in advance.

positives.text: The anchor tenant obann emphasizes organic, pesticide-free vegetables and a 'body-kind' cooking philosophy, suggesting some sensitivity to ingredient quality and dietary awareness.
Vegetable-forward menus and seasonal produce are consistently highlighted across multiple tenants, indicating plant-based options are likely available on most days.

cultural_comfort.note: English signage is not confirmed; communication likely in Japanese, though the rotating share-cafe format with multiple small vendors may vary by operator.

places.name: 炉端とおでん 呼炉凪来 姫路店 (Robata to Oden Koronagirai Himeji)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free declarations anywhere on the menu or restaurant materials. Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu) is used throughout — in robata glazes, yakitori tare, and almost certainly the oden broth seasoning. Udon (wheat noodles) is on the menu. Fried items are present (fried rice, karaage likely). No dedicated fryer information available. Celiacs should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; safe options cannot be confirmed without direct staff inquiry.
places.vegan_detail: A handful of vegetable robata items exist — grilled corn with butter and soy, whole grilled peppers, mountain yam, young corn, edamame — but these are incidental and not flagged vegan. Oden broth is fish-based (bonito/konbu dashi) and most oden pieces contain fish paste or shellfish. The kitchen is not oriented toward plant-based diners and hidden animal ingredients (dashi, soy-glazes) are pervasive. Eat-all-you-can oden contains octopus, shumai, fish cake, and hanpen as standard items.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 16:00–01:00 (next day), Fri 15:00–01:00 (next day), Sat 14:00–01:00 (next day), Sun & holidays 14:00–23:00
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn (about 4 min walk)
places.notes: 呼炉凪来 is a chain brand operated by Foolish Co., Ltd., which opened this Himeji location in March 2025 as an expansion of a concept that originated in Shinjuku, Tokyo. The Shinjuku original reportedly ranked first in access rankings on reservation platforms, driving the brand to ex

chef_bio.origin: Shinjuku, Tokyo (parent brand); Himeji location opened March 13, 2025.
chef_bio.background: 呼炉凪来 is a chain brand operated by Foolish Co. , Ltd. , which opened this Himeji location in March 2025 as an expansion of a concept that originated in Shinjuku, Tokyo. The Shinjuku original reportedly ranked first in access rankings on reservation platforms, driving the brand to expand regionally.
chef_bio.specialty: The signature draw is an all-you-can-eat oden counter at the center of the dining room, offered at ¥550 per person as a built-in appetizer service. Oden items include daikon, konnyaku, egg, thick fried tofu, shirataki, octopus, shumai, fish cake, chikuwa, and hanpen. Charcoal robata grilling of seasonal seafood and vegetables rounds out the menu.

anecdotes.text: The Shinjuku original location ranked #1 in access rankings on major Japanese reservation platforms before the chain expanded to Himeji.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF preparation area or allergen separation is mentioned. Standard izakaya kitchen handles wheat-containing items (udon, yakitori tare, soy-glazed robata) alongside all dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) is presumed throughout — used in robata glazes (corn butter-soy, yam isobe-yaki), yakitori tare, and oden broth seasoning. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternatives are mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth is fish-based (bonito and/or konbu dashi). All vegetable robata items are cooked on the same grill used for meat and fish. No separation of cooking surfaces or utensils for plant-based diners is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling policy is published on any platform reviewed. Direct contact with the restaurant (079-280-8684) is recommended before visiting with serious dietary restrictions.

positives.text: Plain sashimi (tuna, salmon, yellowtail, sea urchin) is available and would be naturally wheat-free and gluten-free if served without soy sauce or with tamari requested — confirm with staff.
Several vegetable robata items (whole grilled peppers, grilled corn, young corn) could theoretically be requested without soy glaze — ask staff about preparation method.

cultural_comfort.note: Standard Japanese izakaya environment with Japanese-language menus; some online reservation platforms may show English, but in-house communication is Japanese.

places.name: WA!DINING MANEKI 姫路城前店 (WA! Dining Maneki Himejijoumae)
places.gf_detail: The signature ekisoba noodle is made from wheat flour with kansui (alkaline water) — it is a wheat noodle product. The broth contains wheat via powdered soy sauce. Tempura coatings add additional wheat. No gluten-free alternatives, dedicated fryer, or allergen-accommodation procedures are documented. The product ingredient list confirms wheat in noodles, broth, and tempura; allergens listed as wheat, shrimp, soy, pork, mackerel, sesame. This venue is not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: All core dishes contain animal products. The ekisoba broth is built on bonito and mackerel powder dashi; the noodles are Chinese-style wheat noodles. The oyakodon uses chicken and eggs. No plant-based or vegan alternatives are listed anywhere on the menu or official materials. Hidden fish-based dashi is present in every savoury item.
