places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:00 (L.O. food 17:45); open year-round except on days when Himeji Castle is closed; hours may extend during castle events
places.neighborhood: Honmachi / in front of Himeji Castle
places.notes: Opened July 25, 2025 in the Karoyashiki-kan building directly in front of Himeji Castle, this dining room is the newest outpost of Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a company founded in Meiji 21 (1888) that invented the Himeji ekisoba concept in 1949. The castle-front venue was conceived

chef_bio.background: Opened July 25, 2025 in the Karoyashiki-kan building directly in front of Himeji Castle, this dining room is the newest outpost of Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社), a company founded in Meiji 21 (1888) that invented the Himeji ekisoba concept in 1949. The castle-front venue was conceived partly to extend the company's popular 'Ultimate Ekisoba' dish that debuted at the 2025 Osaka-Kansai Expo.
chef_bio.philosophy: Celebrate Himeji's food heritage for both residents and overseas visitors while partnering with artisans recovering from the 2024 Noto Peninsula earthquake — the Expo edition of the dish was served in Wajima lacquerware by Taya Shikki.
chef_bio.specialty: Ekisoba — Himeji's local soul food of Chinese-style wheat noodles in Japanese-style dashi broth, served here in premium forms including a limited Kobe beef sukiyaki version and a Banshu Hyakka chicken oyakodon set.

anecdotes.text: The 'Ultimate Kobe Beef Sukiyaki Ekisoba' that launched the restaurant was first served at the Osaka-Kansai Expo 2025 in special Wajima lacquerware bowls made by craftspeople rebuilding after the earthquake. The castle-front version substitutes standard tableware but keeps the same broth recipe.

Ekisoba — the unlikely pairing of Chinese ramen-style noodles with Japanese bonito dashi — has been sold by Maneki at Himeji Station since 1949 and is considered a point of local pride, with residents describing it as 'soul food' that feels completely natural despite the culinary mismatch.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares tempura (wheat-battered) alongside all other dishes. No dedicated fryer or allergen-separated preparation area is documented. Cross-contamination with wheat must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The ekisoba broth contains powdered soy sauce as a listed ingredient. All savoury dishes served at this venue use the same broth base, meaning wheat via soy sauce is present throughout the menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bonito and mackerel powder are core broth ingredients listed in the ekisoba product; there is no plant-based broth option. Every savoury dish contacts fish-based dashi.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen-handling procedures or staff allergy training information is published on the official website or in any press materials reviewed.

positives.text: Accepts credit cards and QR payments; non-smoking throughout; child-friendly; takeout available for packaged ekisoba and souvenir items.

cultural_comfort.note: Located inside a tourist-oriented facility directly in front of a UNESCO World Heritage site, with QR-code payments, takeout, and a menu clearly aimed at international visitors; English signage and menus are likely present.

places.name: 串かつ もぐらや (Kushikatsu Moguraya)
places.gf_detail: Kushikatsu by definition uses panko (wheat breadcrumbs) as its coating — gluten is a core ingredient in every skewer. The restaurant fries all items in a single shared fryer (no dedicated GF fryer mentioned or implied). Dipping sauces and any seasoned accompaniments almost certainly contain wheat-based soy sauce. No GF options or allergy-awareness statements appear anywhere on the official site or in any review. This venue is not safe for celiac disease or serious gluten sensitivity.
places.vegan_detail: Kushikatsu is deep-fried in a wheat-breadcrumb batter and the core menu is built around meat and seafood skewers. No vegetable-only or plant-based options are advertised, and the shared frying oil means animal products are present throughout service. Dashi or bonito-based seasoning cannot be ruled out in side items. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
places.hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 / dinner 17:00–21:00 / closed Sun, holidays & Mon
places.neighborhood: 西二階町商店街 (Nishi-Nikaimachi Shopping Street), Himeji castle-to
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] もぐらや opened in 1998 in Himeji's Nishi-Nikaimachi covered shopping arcade, a narrow two-storey streetscape that has served the castle-town's residents for generations. According to one blog account, the shop's mole-themed name (もぐら = mole) is said to have been borrowed from a prev

chef_bio.background: もぐらや opened in 1998 in Himeji's Nishi-Nikaimachi covered shopping arcade, a narrow two-storey streetscape that has served the castle-town's residents for generations. According to one blog account, the shop's mole-themed name (もぐら = mole) is said to have been borrowed from a previous owner's favourite Osaka restaurant, though whether that forerunner also served fried skewers is uncertain. Owner Hashizume Tsunanori has run the kitchen for over two decades.
chef_bio.philosophy: The house motto is to fry each skewer in front of the guest and plate it as fast as possible — heat and crunch are everything. Lunch is brisk and affordable; dinner moves at a more leisurely pace, with beer, sake and shochu on hand.
chef_bio.specialty: 串かつ (kushikatsu) — wheat-battered, freshly fried skewers served piping hot straight from the oil to the customer across the counter. The lunch set (串かつ定食, ¥800) offers seven skewers with rice, miso soup, potato salad and pickles; à la carte skewers range ¥100–¥300 at dinner.

anecdotes.text: A 2017 blogger noted the owner's warm hospitality — offering to time the beer pour to arrive with the first skewer — and described a serving plate decorated with a rabbit motif despite the restaurant's mole name, a small quirk that charmed regulars.
The shop's name is reportedly derived from a previous owner's wife's favourite restaurant in Osaka, though the connection to fried skewers is unclear.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All kushikatsu are coated in wheat panko and fried in a shared fryer. No dedicated GF fryer exists. Cross-contamination with gluten is certain for every item on the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy-sauce specifics are disclosed, but Worcestershire sauce is used as the standard dipping condiment (contains wheat) and conventional Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat) is standard in this type of establishment.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and seafood skewers are fried continuously in shared oil. No plant-only fryer or cooking station is available.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or dietary-accommodation statement appears on the official website or in any review found. Phone enquiry in Japanese would be required.

cultural_comfort.note: A long-running neighbourhood kushikatsu counter in a traditional covered shopping arcade; mostly local regulars; English signage is not expected, and phone reservations would be in Japanese.

places.name: 天ぷらと小鉢料理 だん乃 (Tempura to Kobachi Ryori Danno)
places.gf_detail: Tempura batter is wheat-flour-based — the core of every dish. Dashi is almost certainly made with katsuobushi (bonito), and standard soy sauce contains wheat. No gluten-free batter alternative, dedicated fryer, or allergen menu has been mentioned in any source. This restaurant is not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around seafood and fish-based preparations. Dashi likely uses katsuobushi (bonito flakes). Vegetable tempura items exist, but they share fryer oil with seafood and are cooked in the same batter. No plant-based or vegan menu track is offered.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30 Fri/Sat; L.O. 20:30 Sun–Thu); lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30); irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho (directly below JR Himeji Stn, GranFesta)
places.notes: Head chef Oba Takuya brings 30 years of Japanese cuisine experience to the kitchen. The restaurant is managed by Matsuoka Kanako. The name 'だん乃' derives from the owner's family name.

chef_bio.origin: Opened November 27, 2025 in the Grand Festa underground shopping street directly beneath JR Himeji Station (5番街).
chef_bio.background: Head chef Oba Takuya brings 30 years of Japanese cuisine experience to the kitchen. The restaurant is managed by Matsuoka Kanako. The name 'だん乃' derives from the owner's family name.
chef_bio.philosophy: The owner's stated vision is a place 'where both customers and staff can spend time smiling,' with an open counter so diners can watch ingredients — displayed in a showcase — being fried to order.
chef_bio.specialty: Freshly fried tempura of same-day-caught seafood from nearby Murotsu, Boze, and Shikama fishing ports, including the signature 鯛の姿天 (whole sea bream tempura). Small dishes (kobachi) such as Himeji oden with conger eel and ginger soy sauce round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: Fresh seafood is sourced daily from Murotsu Port, Boze Port, and Shikama Port near Himeji; the name 'だん乃' comes from the owner's own surname.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All tempura — seafood and vegetable alike — is fried in the same oil at an open counter. No dedicated GF fryer is mentioned in any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No wheat-free soy sauce (tamari) is mentioned. Standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, is the baseline ingredient across sauces, dipping broths, and oden broth.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura shares fryer oil with shrimp, fish, and other seafood items cooked to order in the same vessel at the counter.

positives.text: Open counter seating lets diners see exactly what is being fried and in what order, which at least allows direct conversation with the chef about specific requests.

cultural_comfort.note: Counter-style tempura izakaya with a convivial atmosphere; staff have been described as welcoming, but no English-language service or English menus are mentioned anywhere.

places.name: カフェ&レンタルスペース カリカリちゃん (Cafe & Rental Space Karikari-chan)
places.gf_detail: Snacks and sweets are explicitly made from brown rice flour (玄米粉) and oatmeal instead of wheat flour, making them gluten-free by ingredient. However, the lunch menu changes daily and may include wheat-containing items (soy sauce, grains). There is no dedicated GF kitchen and no formal celiac protocol confirmed. Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. For celiac safety, stick to the labeled GF sweets and ask staff about daily lunch ingredients.
places.vegan_detail: Lunch is frequently served in vegan style (ベジ仕様) using plant-based proteins such as soy meat and oatmeal patties, with no animal products on those days. However, the menu changes daily and animal ingredients occasionally appear; confirming ahead is advised. Sweets (brown rice flour muffins, okara-oat cookie sandwiches) are plant-based. Hidden dashi/bonito risk exists on non-vegan lunch days — ask when ordering.
places.hours_raw: Wed–Sat 11:30–17:30, closed Sun, Mon, Tue. Rental space 9:00–21:00 (closed Tue).
places.neighborhood: 東延末 / 飾磨街道沿い (Higashi-Nobesue / Kazarima-kaido)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner of Karikari-chan began making sweets around 2010, inspired by an encounter with macrobiotics (a traditional Japanese-food philosophy centered on grains and vegetables). In 2017 she operated a workshop-sales venue inside the Himeji Children's Theater office, and on April 1, 2020, she reopened at the current address along the Shikama-kaido (1-69 Higashinobusue) as a cafe and rental space converted from a former inn, marking her 10th anniversary. Under the concept of "a place where people connect and both mind and body can become healthy," she offers handmade lunches using seasonal vegetables sourced from the local organic-farmer group 'Himeji Organic Vegetable Association' and additive-free snacks.

chef_bio.background: カリカリちゃんのオーナーはマクロビオティック（穀物・野菜を中心とした伝統的な日本食の考え方）との出会いをきっかけに2010年頃からお菓子作りをスタート。 2017年には姫路子供劇場の事務所内でワークショップ販売の場として活動し、2020年4月1日に飾磨街道沿いの現住所（東延末1-69）に旧旅館を改装したカフェ&レンタルスペースとしてリニューアルオープンし、10周年の節目を迎えた。 「人と人がつながり、心も体も元気になれる場所」をコンセプトに、地元の有機農家グループ『姫路有機野菜の会』から仕入れた旬野菜を使った手作りランチと無添加おやつを提供している。
chef_bio.philosophy: 素材の味を活かしたシンプルな味付け、無添加、マクロビオティックの考え方に基づいた「体への負担が少ない」食を大切にしている。
chef_bio.specialty: 玄米粉・おからのクッキーサンド（グルテンフリー）と日替わり野菜ランチ

anecdotes.text: 旧旅館の客間をリノベーションした靴脱ぎ式のレンタルスペースは、赤ちゃん連れでも安心して使えるファミリー向けの空間として地域に愛されており、コンサートや展覧会など多目的に活用されている。
ランチはある日、人参と塩麹の豆乳スープ、もち麦サラダ、ヤングコーンの塩焼き、重ね煮野菜のカレー、オートミールハンバーグという動物性食品不使用のベジ仕様で提供された。

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF kitchen or formal cross-contamination protocol has been confirmed. The café is a small space handling both wheat-free snacks and daily-changing lunch menus that may include wheat-based ingredients. Celiac diners should exercise caution.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No explicit information found on whether wheat-based soy sauce (醤油) is used in the lunch cooking. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; macrobiotic kitchens sometimes use tamari but this has not been confirmed here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The lunch menu changes daily and animal products (dairy, egg, fish) occasionally appear in dishes. On vegan-style days the menu is fully plant-based, but cross-contact from shared equipment on other days is unconfirmed.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as willing to address food allergy and vegetarian preferences upon request, but no formal allergen management system or written allergen menu has been confirmed.

positives.text: All food and sweets are additive-free (無添加) since the business began — no artificial preservatives, colorings, or flavor enhancers.
The signature snacks — brown rice flour (玄米粉) mini muffins and okara-oat cookie sandwiches — are made without wheat flour and are explicitly gluten-free by ingredient.
Lunch frequently uses organic vegetables from the local Himeji Yuuki Yasai no Kai (姫路有機野菜の会) cooperative, with simple seasoning to let ingredients shine.

cultural_comfort.note: A welcoming, community-oriented neighborhood spot run by a maternal team; counter service with daily specials written in Japanese only — some basic Japanese or a translation app is helpful, but the relaxed atmosphere and simple ordering make it approachable.

places.name: こばやし茶店（抹茶喫茶 存外） (Kobayashi Chamise (Matcha Kissa Zongai))
places.gf_detail: Matcha tea is inherently gluten-free. However, some wagashi types (manju, dorayaki) are made with wheat flour, and the shop sources sweets from three local confectioners whose ingredient lists are not published. The matcha soft serve's stabilizers/mix are unknown. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen disclosure is documented anywhere. Celiac-level safety cannot be assumed — ask staff about each specific wagashi item before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: Matcha itself is vegan. Traditional wagashi sweets are generally plant-based (rice flour, adzuki bean paste, agar), but exact recipes from the three local confectioners (Kineya, Ichifuji, Iseya) are not published — hidden egg or dairy is possible in some types. The matcha soft serve (Green Soft) is ice cream and almost certainly contains dairy. No vegan labeling or staff allergy-communication policy is documented. Confirm with staff on the day.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–18:30; closed Tuesdays
places.neighborhood: 二階町商店街 (Nikaimachi Shopping Arcade)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Founded in Meiji 8 (1875), Kobayashi Chamise — operating as 株式会社小林松涛園 (Kobayashi Shoutouen Co., Ltd.) — has devoted itself exclusively to Japanese tea for 150 years, making it one of Himeji's oldest specialty tea shops. The main store sits in the Nikaimachi covered shopping arcad

chef_bio.background: Founded in Meiji 8 (1875), Kobayashi Chamise — operating as 株式会社小林松涛園 (Kobayashi Shoutouen Co. , Ltd. ) — has devoted itself exclusively to Japanese tea for 150 years, making it one of Himeji's oldest specialty tea shops. The main store sits in the Nikaimachi covered shopping arcade, roughly halfway between Himeji Station and Himeji Castle. Inside, a tiny tearoom called 存外 (Zongai — meaning 'unexpectedly good') offers just six seats where guests can slow down, choose their own tea bowl from a rotating display of styles from across Japan, and have matcha whisked for them tableside.
chef_bio.philosophy: お茶一筋 — devoted entirely to tea, serving the Japanese tradition of matcha without pretension, so anyone can walk in from the castle road and experience a quiet moment with a bowl of frothy tea
chef_bio.specialty: Matcha and wagashi set (¥660) featuring seasonal sweets from three local Himeji confectioners, rotated daily; Green Soft — a matcha soft-serve ice cream that has been sold for over 50 years and occasionally sells out before closing

anecdotes.text: The Green Soft matcha ice cream was developed collaboratively among regional tea shops seeking new ways to use matcha beyond drinking. It has been sold for over 50 years and on busy days sells out before the shop closes.
The name 存外 (Zongai) is a Japanese idiom meaning 'unexpectedly / better than expected' — the tearoom's name is itself an invitation: come in expecting nothing fancy, leave pleasantly surprised.
Guests select their own tea bowl — shapes, colors, and clay types vary — before staff whisk the matcha, giving each visit a subtly personal character without requiring any knowledge of tea ceremony.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination practices, shared surfaces, or separation of gluten-containing wagashi from other items is available from any source.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is not a feature of this menu. Primary concern is wheat in some wagashi types (manju skin, dorayaki batter). Ingredient lists for the rotating sweets from local confectioners Kineya, Ichifuji, and Iseya are not publicly available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No information on shared equipment between dairy-containing soft serve and other items. Matcha is prepared with whisking in selected bowls — no cross-contact concern for matcha tea itself, but wagashi ingredients are unverified.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy communication policy or allergen menu is documented at this establishment. The shop does not appear to have any published process for handling dietary restriction inquiries. Direct verbal inquiry at the counter is the only avenue.

positives.text: Matcha tea itself (plain, whisked) is naturally gluten-free and vegan — the signature experience of this tearoom can be enjoyed as matcha alone.
Many traditional wagashi are plant-based (adzuki bean paste, rice flour, agar), and the matcha-and-wagashi set at ¥660 may be suitable — but verification of each day's specific sweets is required.

cultural_comfort.note: Primarily a Japanese-language environment in a local shopping arcade; no English menu is documented, though the experience is simple (point to bowl, receive matcha) and non-intimidating for non-Japanese speakers.

places.name: 白鷺堂本舗 (Shirasagido Honpo)
places.gf_detail: This is a mixed wagashi shop — not a dedicated gluten-free establishment. Several products are naturally wheat-free (kuzu mochi, warabi mochi, plain mochi, rice-based daifuku), but wheat-containing items are also sold in the same space: taiyaki and obanyaki use wheat-flour batter, and fu manju (麩まんじゅう) is made from wheat gluten. Mitarashi dango glaze uses soy sauce, which typically contains wheat. No dedicated GF kitchen or fryer separation confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is real; celiac diners should ask individually about each product and exercise caution.
places.vegan_detail: Several items are incidentally plant-based: kuzu mochi (kuzu starch, sugar, water), warabi mochi (bracken starch), and amazake (fermented rice drink) contain no animal products. However, the shop also sells taiyaki, obanyaki, and mitarashi dango; mitarashi glaze uses soy sauce which typically contains wheat and may contain hidden animal-derived ingredients. No vegan labeling or staff communication about vegan status confirmed. Diners should ask specifically about each item.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–17:00, closed Thursdays (TripAdvisor; official site unreachable for verification — confirm before visiting)
places.neighborhood: 本町 (Himeji Castle front)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Shirasagido Honpo is a small independent wagashi (Japanese traditional sweets) shop set within the 家老屋敷跡 ろの屋敷 — the restored grounds of a former chief retainer's residence directly in front of Himeji Castle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The location gives the shop an atmospheric

chef_bio.background: Shirasagido Honpo is a small independent wagashi (Japanese traditional sweets) shop set within the 家老屋敷跡 ろの屋敷 — the restored grounds of a former chief retainer's residence directly in front of Himeji Castle, a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The location gives the shop an atmospheric, old-town character that fits the historic setting.
chef_bio.philosophy: Seasonal, castle-front wagashi using quality regional ingredients, particularly prized Tamba black beans from Hyogo's hinterland, presented in a setting that echoes Himeji's feudal heritage.
chef_bio.specialty: Kuzu mochi and warabi mochi made with Tamba black beans (丹波黒大豆) — plump, earthy, chewy sweets served chilled; also known for kuzu manju, matcha sets, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice).

anecdotes.text: Customers describe the kuzu mochi as 'プルプル' (bouncy and gelatinous) with the gentle sweetness of Tamba black beans — a classic cool-down snack after walking Himeji Castle.
One visitor noted the shop's 'refined sweetness' (上品な甘さ) and described it as having a traditional confectionery feel with a clean, elegant storefront directly facing the castle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen confirmed. The shop prepares and sells wheat-containing items (taiyaki, obanyaki, fu manju) alongside naturally wheat-free items (kuzu mochi, warabi mochi). Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi dango is listed as a menu item; mitarashi glaze is made with soy sauce, which in Japan typically contains wheat. No tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternative noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No information on whether dairy or egg is used in any products. Taiyaki batter traditionally contains egg and milk. No vegan-specific handling protocol found.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen handling information, staff training documentation, or allergy communication protocols found across any reviewed source. Recommend calling ahead at 0120-11-3977 to ask about specific items.

positives.text: Core items like kuzu mochi and warabi mochi are made from naturally gluten-free, plant-based starches (kuzu root, bracken fern) with sugar and water — inherently free of wheat, dairy, and eggs when made traditionally.

cultural_comfort.note: A compact traditional wagashi counter with very limited English; menus and signage are in Japanese only, but the sweets are visual and pointing/gesturing works fine for ordering.

places.name: マーガレット スプーン (Marguerite Spoon)
places.gf_detail: The flagship scones all use wheat flour. The one exception mentioned in a single article is a gluten-free canele made with rice flour, but whether it is always available is unknown. There is no information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination measures. Not suitable for celiacs. Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: The scones all use Hokkaido wheat flour, Yotsuba cultured butter, and heavy cream—dairy-based. No vegan menu is noted. Assessment: not vegan-friendly.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays & Sat 10:00–17:00, Sun & holidays 11:00–17:00. Closes when sold out. Closed Mon and the 2nd Tuesday.
places.neighborhood: Honmachi, Otemae-dori (the castle-town main street leading to Himeji Castle)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner discovered scones about 25 years ago in an English cookbook and researched the method self-taught. After establishing her recipe through imported recipe books and hundreds of test batches, she began by selling baked goods at a local farm-produce direct-sales shop, then opened a physical store in December 2014 at Honmachi 144 along Otemae-dori leading to Himeji Castle.

chef_bio.background: オーナーは約25年前に英語の料理本でスコーンに出会い、独学で製法を研究。 輸入レシピ本と何百回もの試作を重ねてレシピを確立した後、地元の農産物直売所で焼き菓子を販売する形で始め、2014年12月に姫路城へ向かう大手前通り沿いの本町144に実店舗をオープン。
chef_bio.philosophy: 生地をこねすぎず、水分を少なめに保つことで独特のザクザク食感を生み出す。 素材は小麦粉・バター・ナッツ・ドライフルーツを自ら吟味して仕入れる。 英国式アフタヌーンティーの格式ではなく、ニューヨーク流のカジュアルで遊び心あるスコーンを目指す。
chef_bio.specialty: 厚さ5cmを超えるニューヨークスタイルの極厚スコーン。 定番のプレーンスコーンはよつ葉発酵バターの香りと風味を前面に出したベストセラー。 約20種類のスコーンが日替わりで並ぶ。

anecdotes.text: 店名「マーガレット」（デイジー）を選んだ理由は、「目立たないけれど、確かな存在感がある花」だから。この哲学が菓子づくりの姿勢にも表れている。
姫路城下町という歴史的な街並みの中に、ブラックを基調とした都会的な外観とオレンジのネオン管が映え、通りかかった人が焼き菓子の甘い香りに引き寄せられる光景が名物になっている。

gf_cross_contamination.text: スコーンはすべて小麦粉使用の厨房で製造。専用グルテンフリー設備や交差汚染対策についての記述は確認されていない。

vegan_cross_contact.text: 全製品に乳製品（発酵バター・生クリーム）が使用されており、ヴィーガン向け製品との分離製造は行われていないとみられる。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー対応や食物制限に関するスタッフ対応についての情報は公式・ブログ・レビューいずれにも見当たらない。来店前にInstagram DMで問い合わせることが推奨される（電話注文不可）。

positives.text: 米粉を使ったグルテンフリーのカヌレ（外はカリッと、中はふわふわ）が少なくとも一時期提供されていたことが確認されている。

cultural_comfort.note: テイクアウト専門の小さな焼き菓子店で、注文はInstagram DMのみ受付。英語対応の記述なし。

places.name: 八べえ (Hachibee)
places.gf_detail: Standard izakaya kitchen with no gluten-free declaration. Soy sauce (wheat-containing) is foundational to virtually every dish including oden broth, sashimi accompaniments, and marinades. Karaage (fried chicken) and anago tempura involve wheat-flour coatings. No dedicated fryer confirmed. Cross-contamination risk is high in a small izakaya kitchen. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; safe options cannot be assumed.
places.vegan_detail: A seafood-forward izakaya with no declared vegan menu. Nearly every dish centers on fish (anago, sashimi), shellfish (shrimp shumai), or meat (Tajima chicken). Himeji oden is simmered in a dashi broth almost certainly made from fish stock. No plant-based or vegan option is advertised anywhere. Inquire before visiting; realistic prospects are slim without advance arrangement.
places.hours_raw: Mon–Thu 17:00–01:00 (next day, L.O. 00:30) / Fri, Sat & pre-holidays 17:00–03:00 (next day, L.O. 02:30) / closed Sun (open if Mon is a holiday)
places.neighborhood: 立町 (Tatemachi), ~262m from Sanyo-Himeji Station
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] A small, independently run izakaya in Himeji's Tatemachi district, a short walk from both the JR and Sanyo Himeji stations. The restaurant positions itself as a showcase for 播磨うまいもん — the authentic flavors of the Harima region — sourcing seafood directly from Murotsu fishing port

chef_bio.background: A small, independently run izakaya in Himeji's Tatemachi district, a short walk from both the JR and Sanyo Himeji stations. The restaurant positions itself as a showcase for 播磨うまいもん — the authentic flavors of the Harima region — sourcing seafood directly from Murotsu fishing port on the Seto Inland Sea and vegetables from contracted local farms.
chef_bio.philosophy: Direct sourcing from a contract fishing port and local farms to bring genuine Harima flavors to the table at an accessible price point, in a lively late-night izakaya setting.
chef_bio.specialty: Anago (conger eel) prepared multiple ways — grilled, as tempura, and as ochazuke (tea-poured rice) — alongside Himeji oden served in the local style with ginger soy sauce, and house-made shrimp shumai.

anecdotes.text: The interior is lined with autographed celebrity photos, giving the small space the feel of a well-loved local institution that has welcomed a wide cross-section of guests over the years.
The proprietor personally recommends the house-made shrimp shumai as the restaurant's signature item — a dish visitors consistently single out alongside the anago preparations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Small izakaya kitchen serving fried dishes (karaage, anago tempura, tuna-skin karaage) alongside non-fried items. No dedicated fryer confirmed; shared-oil cross-contamination with wheat-coated items is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Wheat-based soy sauce is central to virtually all cooking here: oden broth, sashimi dipping sauce, marinades for grilled anago, and karaage batter all conventionally contain wheat. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternative is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi is the backbone of Himeji oden and likely used in multiple dishes. Shared cooking surfaces in a small kitchen handling fish, shellfish, and meat throughout service make vegan isolation impractical without direct advance arrangement.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy, allergen menu, or dietary accommodation statement is published on the official site, Tabelog, HotPepper, or Gurunavi listings. Direct phone inquiry (079-224-3999) before visiting is strongly advised for any dietary restriction.

positives.text: Fresh seafood sourced directly from Murotsu fishing port means dishes are made to order rather than pre-processed, which in principle allows ingredient-level discussion with staff for guests who can communicate in Japanese.

cultural_comfort.note: A local izakaya catering primarily to Japanese regulars and domestic tourists; no English menu or English-speaking staff is advertised, though the atmosphere is friendly and the menu is approachable for curious visitors.

places.name: koriki (koriki)
places.gf_detail: As a standard Japanese omakase course, use of wheat-containing seasonings such as soy sauce (contains wheat), mirin, and miso is assumed. There is no mention of gluten-free accommodation on either the website or in reviews. Because it is a single fixed course, the flexibility for substitutions may be low. Celiacs and those with severe wheat allergy must confirm in advance by phone. Assessment: not celiac-safe.
places.vegan_detail: An omakase lunch only, all-or-nothing. The miso soup has been confirmed to contain myoga, and use of typical washoku dashi (bonito, dried sardine) is strongly presumed. Whether plant-based-only dashi can be accommodated is not stated officially at all. Confirm in advance by phone. Assessment: not reliably vegan.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Wed–Sun 11:30–15:00 (LO 14:00), closed Tue plus irregular days off
places.neighborhood: Korikimachi (west of the castle, along the Semba River)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] As a standard Japanese omakase course, use of wheat-containing seasonings such as soy sauce (contains wheat), mirin, and miso is assumed. There is no mention of gluten-free accommodation on either the website or in reviews. Because it is a single fixed course, the flexibility for substitutions may be low. Celiacs and those with severe wheat allergy must confirm in advance by phone.

chef_bio.philosophy: 「日常のなかにある、ゆったりとした落ち着いた時間」を提供することを理念とし、食材の旨みを引き出す調理を重視する。
chef_bio.specialty: おまかせランチ（¥3,300税込）— 旬の素材を少量多皿で盛り合わせたプレート形式。 漬け物・ドレッシングはすべて手作り。

anecdotes.text: 2024年6月15日オープン。船場川沿いの古民家を改装した空間で、カウンター席からは姫路城の天守閣とお堀を望む。姫路城のビューを楽しめるランチとして開店直後から人気を集めた。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: おまかせ和食コースのため醤油・みりん・味噌など小麦含有調味料の使用が一般的に想定されるが、具体的な使用調味料の情報は公式・口コミいずれにも開示なし。

vegan_cross_contact.text: みそ汁にみょうがを使用した記録あり。出汁はかつお等の動物性素材の可能性が高いが未確認。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギー・食事制限対応についての情報は一切公開されていない。問い合わせは電話（079-297-8388）のみ受付。

positives.text: 漬け物・ドレッシングをすべて手作りし、食材の風味を引き立てることを意識した調理スタイルを採用。添加物への意識が伺える。

cultural_comfort.note: 公式サイトおよびメニューは日本語のみ。英語対応の記載なし。電話予約のみのため、日本語または通訳が必要。

places.name: 大衆酒場 蒼 (Taishū Sakaba Sou)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or accommodations documented. Wheat is present in standard soy sauce used throughout Japanese izakaya cooking, and most sauces, marinades, and dashi-based dishes will contain gluten. No dedicated fryer information available. Not suitable for celiac without direct staff consultation.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu exists. Small plates (yamaimo, assorted appetizers, chorizo) are typical izakaya fare; hidden dashi (katsuobushi/bonito) is standard in Japanese cooking and very likely present in most dishes. No vegan claims found anywhere.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri 17:00–00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30), Sat & Sun 14:00–00:00 (same L.O.), holidays 15:00–00:00 (same L.O.), closed Wed
places.neighborhood: Ekimaecho, near Sanyo-Himeji Stn
places.cuisine: Izakaya / Harima Local Sake & Small Plates
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened October 3, 2025 on the second floor of the Hashimoto Building in Himeji's Ekimae-cho. The venue is run by a wife-and-husband team: the wife manages the izakaya upstairs while her husband operates a ramen shop on the floor below, creating a small family food complex in the

chef_bio.background: Opened October 3, 2025 on the second floor of the Hashimoto Building in Himeji's Ekimae-cho. The venue is run by a wife-and-husband team: the wife manages the izakaya upstairs while her husband operates a ramen shop on the floor below, creating a small family food complex in the heart of the station neighborhood.
chef_bio.philosophy: A casual, down-to-earth neighborhood bar — the name 蒼 (sou, meaning 'blue/azure') reflects an understated simplicity. The atmosphere leans relaxed, with Okinawan-inflected music and a counter where regulars can drink well for little money.
chef_bio.specialty: Harima local sake (播磨地酒) served at affordable prices alongside simple izakaya small plates. Signature sake pours include Okuharimanma Junmai (¥500) and Senchuuhassaku (¥600).

anecdotes.text: An early visitor noted the opening 'didn't feel like a typical restaurant launch' — the entrance was low-key, almost residential in feel, yet inside a curated slate of Harima sake awaited at prices well below city-center bars.

A German guest happened to be present during one early visit, prompting a spontaneous conversation about why Japanese sake is priced so accessibly compared to Europe — the owner served as impromptu cultural guide.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information about cross-contamination controls is available. This is a 12-seat counter izakaya with a compact kitchen; dedicated fryer or allergen segregation is not documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce containing wheat is used throughout Japanese izakaya cuisine. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned anywhere in available sources.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Typical izakaya kitchen where fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/bonito) is standard in sauces, soups, and cooking bases. No vegan preparation protocols documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or English-language accommodation is documented. Reservations are made via Instagram DM in Japanese; allergen queries would need to be raised there in advance.

positives.text: Small, owner-operated venue where direct communication with the proprietor is possible, making advance allergy enquiries via Instagram DM (@sou.taishusakaba) feasible before visiting.

cultural_comfort.note: Reservations are made via Instagram DM in Japanese; no English signage or menu is documented, and the setting is a small local counter bar where Japanese is the working language.

places.name: たまごや (Tamagoya)
places.gf_detail: The core dish (plain rice with raw egg) could in principle be wheat-free, but the restaurant's proprietary tamago kake shoyu (custom-developed soy sauce) almost certainly contains wheat, as standard Japanese soy sauce does. The thick omelet uses bonito-and-kombu dashi — dashi ingredients are typically gluten-free, but cross-contamination risk in a shared kitchen is unknown. No gluten-free claims, no dedicated fryer information, and no allergen menu have been found on any source. Anyone with celiac disease should contact the restaurant before visiting.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around eggs — TKG (tamago kake gohan), thick omelets, egg custard, egg sandwiches. The signature thick omelet is made with bonito broth, mackerel flakes, and Rishiri kombu dashi. The miso soup served with sets almost certainly uses fish-based dashi. No plant-based dishes are offered. This is not a viable stop for vegans.
places.hours_raw: 11:00–17:00 (last order 16:30), open daily (varies by season)
places.neighborhood: Honmachi (Otemae, in front of the Himeji Castle gate)
places.cuisine: Tamago kake gohan (egg over rice), breakfast
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Tamagoya is operated by Fujihashigumi, a poultry farm that has raised chickens in the Yumesaki and Shikito districts of Himeji since 1925. The farm opened this egg-dedicated dining room in 2009 to put its own produce directly in front of diners at the gates of Himeji Castle.

chef_bio.background: Tamagoya is operated by Fujihashigumi, a poultry farm that has raised chickens in the Yumesaki and Shikito districts of Himeji since 1925. The farm opened this egg-dedicated dining room in 2009 to put its own produce directly in front of diners at the gates of Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.philosophy: Raise the chickens carefully on custom 31-ingredient feed, then let the egg speak for itself with minimal cooking and unlimited refills so every guest leaves satisfied.
chef_bio.specialty: Tamakake meshi — a bowl of warm Himeji rice topped with a raw Yumeo or Yume-sodachi egg from the farm's own flocks, served with their proprietary tamago kake shoyu, pickles, and miso soup; eggs and rice are refillable.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's 'Yumeo' eggs won three consecutive national championships at the Tokyo Tamago Kake Gohan Festival and became the inaugural entrants into its Hall of Fame.
Celebrity visitors dine incognito; signed photographs from Japanese celebrities cover the interior walls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on cross-contamination practices is available from any source reviewed. Kitchen setup and shared equipment are unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant uses a proprietary tamago kake shoyu co-developed with a manufacturer. Standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat; no source confirms a wheat-free or tamari version is used.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Not applicable — the entire menu is egg-based and includes fish-derived dashi; no vegan options exist to assess cross-contact for.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No information found on allergy handling procedures. English-language menu is available but no allergen chart or staff allergy-handling protocol has been documented by any reviewer.

positives.text: The core raw egg on plain rice is a simple preparation; if the soy sauce can be confirmed wheat-free, the base dish has minimal gluten risk.

cultural_comfort.note: English and Chinese menus are available; touch-panel queue system at the entrance; popular with international tourists visiting Himeji Castle, so staff are accustomed to non-Japanese visitors.

places.name: イルリッチョ (Il Riccio)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free certification and no dedicated GF protocol documented anywhere. The kitchen explicitly states food is "not allergy-free" and asks guests with allergies to inform staff in advance. At least one menu item (車麩 kuruma-fu, a wheel-shaped wheat gluten) is a recurring main dish, confirming wheat is actively used on the menu. The soy sauce type (wheat-bearing standard shoyu vs. wheat-free tamari) is unspecified in all sources reviewed. Cross-contamination risk cannot be ruled out. Celiacs and those with serious wheat allergy should contact the restaurant before visiting: 079-288-2439.
places.vegan_detail: Strictly 100% plant-based — no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or honey in any dish. The kitchen follows macrobiotic principles and sources organic, naturally farmed, pesticide-reduced local vegetables and additive-free seasonings. Both the café menu and the attached shop stock only plant-based goods. Note: some menu items use fu (wheat gluten), so purely wheat-free vegans should check the day's menu.
places.hours_raw: Shop 10:30–18:30; Café 11:30–18:30 (last order 17:30, lunch last order 15:30); Closed Mondays and 3rd Sunday of each month
places.neighborhood: 呉服町 (Gofukucho), near Himeji Castle
places.notes: The story behind il riccio stretches back roughly half a century. The predecessor natural food shop traded on Himeji's Miyuki-dori shopping street for around 48 years before closing that location in November 2019 and reopening — reimagined as a plant-based café with an attached r

chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo Prefecture; relocated from Miyuki-dori after ~48 years
chef_bio.background: The story behind il riccio stretches back roughly half a century. The predecessor natural food shop traded on Himeji's Miyuki-dori shopping street for around 48 years before closing that location in November 2019 and reopening — reimagined as a plant-based café with an attached retail shop — on November 27, 2019. The date was deliberately chosen: it fell on a new moon and an auspicious day (新月の大安). The new home is a two-storey space in Gofukucho 18, steps from the historic arcade near Himeji Castle, in what was previously the C. T. CLUB premises.
chef_bio.philosophy: Macrobiotics meets 100% plant-based cooking: every ingredient is organic or naturally farmed, pesticide-reduced, and seasoned with additive-free condiments sourced — and also sold — in the shop downstairs. The guiding idea is that food should nourish 'beauty and energy from within. ' The owner also teaches seasonal macrobiotic cooking classes on Italian, side-dish, and bento themes.
chef_bio.specialty: Seasonal lunch set (一汁三菜) — one miso soup and three vegetable dishes over organic Hyogo-grown rice or brown rice, with a rotating plant-based main such as soy-meat cutlet, miso demi-glace hamburger, or glazed wheat-gluten; vegan desserts (gateau chocolat made with tofu and rice flour, soymilk pudding with blueberry sauce) change monthly

anecdotes.text: The café's name comes from the Italian word for hedgehog (il riccio), and a hedgehog sculpture greets guests at the entrance — a nod to the slow, deliberate pace the kitchen takes with its ingredients.
The reopening on November 27, 2019 was timed to coincide with a new moon falling on an auspicious day (新月の大安), reflecting the owners' holistic sensibility.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The café explicitly states its food is not allergy-free and that guests with allergies must notify staff in advance. No information on dedicated preparation surfaces or fryers is published.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soy sauce type used in cooking is not disclosed in any published source. Standard Japanese shoyu contains wheat; whether the kitchen uses wheat-free tamari is unknown and must be confirmed directly with staff.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All menu items are 100% plant-based by design — no animal products enter the kitchen — so cross-contact with meat, fish, dairy, or eggs is not a concern.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant asks guests with any allergy to inform staff before ordering. No structured allergen matrix or written protocol is publicly available.

positives.text: Entirely animal-product-free kitchen eliminates the hidden-dashi and hidden-katsuo risks that are common pitfalls for vegans at Japanese restaurants.
Ingredients are organic, naturally farmed, and free from synthetic additives — the same goods are sold in the attached shop, giving transparency into sourcing.

cultural_comfort.note: English menus are available and the café is accustomed to international visitors, making it accessible without Japanese language skills.

places.name: レガーレ (Regare)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options confirmed in any source. The menu includes multiple high-gluten items: katsu-meshi (breaded pork cutlet), akashi-yaki (wheat batter), somen/nyumen noodles, and oden broth almost certainly seasoned with regular wheat-containing soy sauce (shoyu). No dedicated fryer information available. Cross-contamination risk is high in this cafeteria kitchen. Not safe for celiac diners.
places.vegan_detail: The menu is built around traditional Japanese comfort food — Himeji oden (simmered in a dashi broth typically made with katsuobushi/bonito), anago don (conger eel), bokkake don (braised beef tendon), katsu-meshi (breaded pork cutlet), akashi-yaki (octopus balls), and somen/nyumen noodles. No vegan options are listed or mentioned in any source. Hidden animal-derived dashi is near-certain in oden broth and noodle soups.
places.hours_raw: 10:00–15:30 (L.O. 15:00), irregular days off
places.neighborhood: 本町68（Honcho）- 姫路城前
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] レガーレ (Regare) opened in April 2003 as the restaurant arm of ひめじ授産振興センター — a vocational and welfare support center run by NPO法人姫路地区手をつなぐ育成会, an NPO established to support people with intellectual disabilities and their families in the Himeji area. The center occupies はの屋敷 (Hano Ya

chef_bio.background: レガーレ (Regare) opened in April 2003 as the restaurant arm of ひめじ授産振興センター — a vocational and welfare support center run by NPO法人姫路地区手をつなぐ育成会, an NPO established to support people with intellectual disabilities and their families in the Himeji area. The center occupies はの屋敷 (Hano Yashiki), a historic building on the grounds of the former chief retainer's (家老) residence, directly opposite the main gate approach to Himeji Castle. The 1st floor serves as a welfare goods shop selling crafts and products made by local disability support facilities; the 2nd floor is the cafeteria restaurant.
chef_bio.philosophy: Community welfare through employment: the restaurant provides a meaningful workplace for people with disabilities while serving local heritage cuisine to castle visitors.
chef_bio.specialty: 姫路おでん (Himeji oden) — simmered oden served with a distinctive ginger-soy sauce dipping condiment unique to this city; also anago don (conger eel rice bowl) using local Seto Inland Sea eel

anecdotes.text: The center officially opened as 'レガーレ' in April 2003 (Heisei 15) under the NPO 姫路地区手をつなぐ育成会, combining a welfare goods retail space on the first floor with a local-cuisine restaurant on the second.
Diners on the 2nd floor can look directly out at Himeji Castle's white walls while eating — described by visitors as the closest place to the castle where you can eat Himeji oden.
The shop counter on the 1st floor sells 20+ flavours of soft-serve ice cream, with apple mango noted as a standout; the ice cream is takeout-only and popular with castle tourists.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available on cross-contamination practices. This is a small cafeteria kitchen serving wheat-heavy dishes including battered akashi-yaki and breaded katsu-meshi — assume shared surfaces and utensils.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Himeji oden is served with ginger-soy sauce as a condiment; standard Japanese soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu) contains wheat. Oden broth itself is also likely seasoned with soy sauce. No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth is typically made with katsuobushi (bonito flakes) dashi. Somen and nyumen soups will contain fish-based broth. No plant-based broth option mentioned in any source.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling information found in any source. The center is run as a social welfare facility; there is no indication of allergy-aware staff training or menus.

positives.text: Soft-serve ice cream sold on the 1st floor (plain flavours such as vanilla or sesame) may be suitable for some dietary needs — but ingredients are unconfirmed.

cultural_comfort.note: Cafeteria-style service with no English signage confirmed; staff are part of a Japanese welfare organization — basic Japanese or pointing at a menu will be necessary.

places.name: 本町 ミヨシ (Honmachi Miyoshi)
places.gf_detail: No GF claims made anywhere. Wheat is pervasive: standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat-containing) is used as a glaze on sashimi; tempura (seaweed tempura noted) appears on the menu; wonton uses wheat wrappers; fried items share a common fryer. No dedicated fryer and no allergen menu. Celiac diners should phone ahead; the kitchen cannot currently be considered safe without direct staff confirmation on each dish and potential cross-contamination from a busy one-person counter operation.
places.vegan_detail: Standard izakaya menu built around fish-based dashi, animal proteins, and egg. Documented dishes include shrimp mayo, chicken liver, sashimi, hamburger, wonton, and anchovy potato fries — no plant-only section is advertised. Some items may be incidentally plant-based (fried eggplant with grated yam appears on the menu), but hidden dashi and shared prep surfaces make vegan verification essential. Staff must be consulted dish-by-dish; full vegan accommodations are unlikely.
places.hours_raw: Weekdays 17:00–24:00 (L.O. 23:00), Sat & Sun 15:00–24:00, closed Thu
places.neighborhood: 本町 (Honmachi shopping arcade, east side of Eagle Himeji, Ote
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened 24 April 2023 in the covered Honmachi shopping arcade in central Himeji, a short walk from Otemae-dori toward the castle. The restaurant is run by a single proprietor who manages the counter alone, cooking and serving simultaneously even during full-house sittings.

chef_bio.background: Opened 24 April 2023 in the covered Honmachi shopping arcade in central Himeji, a short walk from Otemae-dori toward the castle. The restaurant is run by a single proprietor who manages the counter alone, cooking and serving simultaneously even during full-house sittings.
chef_bio.philosophy: Creative izakaya cooking that prizes original flavour combinations and careful presentation without pretension; the atmosphere is intentionally dim and relaxed, with classic Japanese punk and new-wave playing in the background.
chef_bio.specialty: The kitchen leans into inventive small plates — standouts noted across multiple visits include fried soft tofu in dashi (胡麻豆腐の揚げだし), black fried rice (黒チャーハン), a three-item omakase set that changes with the season, and a rotating sashimi special finished with soy glaze and seaweed tempura. Sake and shochu pairings are a particular strength, with rare unfiltered and ori-garami selections.

anecdotes.text: On opening evening the seats filled immediately; one blogger described the energy as 'an energetic festival bar atmosphere' with the lone proprietor fielding a full counter's worth of orders without help.
A spring visit captured the omakase trio: fluffy shrimp tempura, chicken liver stew, and dried persimmon with mascarpone — paired with Kōeikiku 'Anastasia Green' unfiltered raw sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated fryer confirmed. Tempura (seaweed tempura documented) and other fried items appear to share cooking oil. Cross-contamination from wheat flour is a realistic risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (wheat-containing) used as a glaze on sashimi and likely throughout the menu. No tamari or GF soy sauce alternative has been noted in any source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish-based dashi is standard in Japanese izakaya cooking and is presumed present in broths, fried tofu dishes, and sauces. Shared prep surfaces in a one-person kitchen increase cross-contact risk.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergen menu, allergy card acceptance, or allergy-handling protocol has been documented in any review or listing. Restaurant contact: 079-263-7973.

positives.text: Owner-operator cooks everything from scratch to order, meaning ingredients are freshly combined rather than prepackaged — in principle this allows ingredient-level questions to be answered by the person who actually cooks the dish.

cultural_comfort.note: Solo owner-operated counter bar with a lively local regulars' atmosphere; no English signage or menu has been noted in any source, but the welcome is warm and non-intimidating for respectful visitors.

places.name: ごはんとおバンザイ ジグザグ (Gohan to Obanzai Ziguzagu)
places.gf_detail: Traditional Japanese obanzai cooking relies heavily on soy sauce (shoyu, which contains wheat) and dashi. No gluten-free claims or accommodations are mentioned in any source. Dishes vary daily, making it impossible to predict wheat-free options without asking. Fried items appear on the rotating menu (cross-contamination risk from shared fryer likely). Those with celiac disease should ask staff in detail before ordering.
places.vegan_detail: The 16-item rotating obanzai set includes around 10 vegetable-based small dishes alongside 4–6 meat or fish preparations. No vegan designation exists; miso soup is made with dashi likely containing bonito (katsuo), and several dishes will include fish, meat, or fish-based seasoning. Individual vegetable-only obanzai may be plantbased but are not labelled as such and shared kitchen contact is likely.
places.hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Sun 11:00–15:00; Wed, Fri, Sat 11:00–15:00 / 18:00–22:00; closed Thu (occasional irregular days off); takeout 11:00–16:00
places.neighborhood: Nishinikaimachi
places.cuisine: Obanzai (Kyoto-style home cooking), home-style cuisine, diner
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Opened July 14, 2023 in Himeji's Nishinikaimachi shopping street — the second location from the team behind ごはんとプレート ジグザグ in the Higashiyama district. While the first shop leaned toward Western-style plate lunches, this branch pivots fully to obanzai, the Japanese tradition of ho

chef_bio.background: Opened July 14, 2023 in Himeji's Nishinikaimachi shopping street — the second location from the team behind ごはんとプレート ジグザグ in the Higashiyama district. While the first shop leaned toward Western-style plate lunches, this branch pivots fully to obanzai, the Japanese tradition of home-style small dishes.
chef_bio.philosophy: The kitchen describes its ethos as 'housewives pursuing the possibilities of obanzai with enthusiasm' — emphasising that ingredient selection alone can expand the dish infinitely. The owner sources produce by visiting vegetable vendors directly and uses inherited Bizen pottery from the family home to serve the meal.
chef_bio.specialty: 16種日替わりおばんざい定食 — a set of 16 rotating seasonal small dishes with rice and housemade miso soup, priced at around ¥1,980 and offered in limited quantities each day.

anecdotes.text: One blogger reported that the shop sign is invisible from the street — the entrance sits deep inside a building — yet on a weekday morning the room was already over 90% full, with an overwhelmingly female clientele.
The miso soup is made from a blend of three different miso varieties combined with fresh dashi prepared daily, a detail highlighted by the Kiss Press review of the tofu hamburger set.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF fryer or preparation area is mentioned in any source. Fried items (e.g. fried chicken) appear on the rotating obanzai menu, making shared-fryer cross-contamination a realistic concern.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce (shoyu, containing wheat) is a baseline ingredient in obanzai cooking; no tamari or wheat-free soy sauce alternatives are mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Miso soup dashi is prepared daily and described as rich-flavoured, most likely containing bonito (katsuo). Meat and fish dishes share the kitchen with vegetable obanzai; no separation is noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No sources mention staff allergy training, allergen menus, or any protocol for dietary requests. Lunch operates without reservations, limiting pre-visit communication.

positives.text: The menu is built around seasonal vegetables, with roughly 10 of the 16 daily dishes being vegetable-based, giving plant-leaning diners more choice than a typical teishoku restaurant.

cultural_comfort.note: A casual neighbourhood shokudo with no English signage or menu; Japanese literacy or pointing at dishes is needed, though the relaxed atmosphere makes solo visitors feel welcome.

places.name: エグレット (Aigrette)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claim or accommodation is mentioned anywhere. French bistro cooking relies on wheat flour in sauces (roux), pastry (canelés are a signature item), and bread service. Ethnic-influenced dishes such as Bánh Mì and Nasi Goreng may add further gluten exposure. No dedicated fryer information found. Soy sauce use is plausible in the ethnic-fusion element of the menu but not confirmed. Guests with celiac disease should contact the restaurant directly before visiting; cross-contamination risk in a 16-seat open kitchen is non-trivial.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan menu or options are advertised. The kitchen centers on French bistro cooking with prominent meat and fish dishes — Himeji wagyu, conger eel, smoked salmon, chicken, pâté. Hidden animal products (butter, cream, fish stock) are routine in this style. A guest with vegan needs would need to contact the restaurant well in advance; the omakase format may allow some flexibility but nothing is confirmed.
places.neighborhood: 本町 (Honcho), near Himeji Castle
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Asami grew up in Himeji and began his culinary career at an ethnic cuisine restaurant in the city. He then spent approximately eight years training at a Michelin-starred French restaurant in Osaka (reported as ad hoc). A mentor's advice — "young chefs should return to their homet

roles: sommelier (partner/wife, trained under Osaka natural-wine specialists)
chef_bio.origin: Opened January 22, 2020, in the Honmachi district immediately south of Himeji Castle.
chef_bio.background: Asami grew up in Himeji and began his culinary career at an ethnic cuisine restaurant in the city. He then spent approximately eight years training at a Michelin-starred French restaurant in Osaka (reported as ad hoc). A mentor's advice — "young chefs should return to their hometown, go to its market, and cook for its people" — drew him back to Himeji to open his own place rather than remain in Kobe or Osaka.
chef_bio.philosophy: Asami aims to fulfill hearts as well as stomachs, offering a moment of genuine joy. He resists orthodox categorisation, blending French structure with international flavour references so that no single style dominates. Menus are justified course by course — each dish must have a reason for existing.
chef_bio.specialty: Herb- and spice-forward neo-bistro cooking that layers French technique with ethnic influences. Omakase courses change daily around peak-season local produce: vegetables from Yahomina Farm in Yumesaki, fish from Harima-nada waters, Tajima chicken, and Himeji wagyu. Signature items include pâté de campagne with candied kumquat, grilled conger eel, and Bordeaux-style canelés.

anecdotes.text: The name Aigrette comes from the French word for white heron, a direct homage to Himeji Castle's poetic name Hakurojo (White Heron Castle). Asami drew a parallel to chef Fernand Point, who named his legendary La Pyramide after the Roman ruins beside his restaurant — rooting the restaurant's identity in the landscape it inhabits.
The restaurant shares the famous Honmachi 68 address with Himeji Castle itself — historically significant real estate that places the dining room on the edge of the castle's own grounds.
Each month on the 22nd (the restaurant's opening anniversary date) a special eggplant curry dish appears on the menu, and on the 20th a natural wine evening event is held.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information is available about cross-contamination controls. With only 16 seats and an open counter kitchen, shared preparation surfaces for wheat-containing dishes (pastry, flour-thickened sauces, Bánh Mì bread) are likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu blends French and ethnic Asian influences (Hainanese chicken rice, Nasi Goreng, Bánh Mì), making standard soy sauce a plausible ingredient, though its specific use has not been confirmed in any source reviewed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All reviewed sources confirm the menu is built around meat, fish, and classical French dairy. No vegan-safe preparation protocol is mentioned.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling procedures or staff training information appears in any source. Reservations are made by phone the day before, which is the logical opportunity to communicate dietary needs.

positives.text: Omakase course format (menu decided day-of based on freshest available ingredients) may allow ingredient-level customisation if communicated clearly at reservation time.

cultural_comfort.note: A small, reservation-only French bistro where prior booking by phone is standard; some English may be possible but the environment and menus are primarily Japanese.

places.name: 酒ト飯 みすず (Sake to Meshi Misuzu)
places.gf_detail: This is a conventional izakaya. Wheat appears throughout: standard soy sauce (含小麦) is almost certainly used in marinades and sauces, tempura batter is wheat-based, and tatsuta-age (fried chicken) uses wheat starch. No dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer information was found in any source. Celiac diners must enquire specifically before ordering; cross-contamination risk is significant in a small open kitchen.
places.vegan_detail: Standard izakaya cooking with dashi-based broths, soy-based sauces, sashimi, tempura, and grilled fish all prominent. No vegan menu or labelling found. Chawanmushi (egg custard) and katsuobushi-likely dashi are standard. Vegan diners should enquire dish-by-dish; hidden fish-based stock is the main risk.
places.neighborhood: Nishinikaimachi, Omizosuji Shotengai arcade (about 423m from Sanyo Himeji Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Sake to Meshi Misuzu is a small izakaya that opened on December 8, 2023, in the Omizosuji Shotengai arcade in Himeji. A small 11-seat space with 3 counter seats and two 4-person tables, it also offers a daily lunch special (1,000 yen) on weekdays.

chef_bio.background: 酒ト飯 みすずは2023年12月8日、姫路市のお溝筋商店街にオープンした小さな居酒屋。 カウンター3席と4人テーブル2卓という11席の小空間で、平日は日替わりランチ（1,000円）も提供する。
chef_bio.specialty: 日替わりランチ（刺身・天ぷら・茶碗蒸しなど）と夜の居酒屋メニュー

anecdotes.text: When draft beer ran out during one visit, staff offered bottled beer at the same draft price — a small gesture that earned reviewer praise.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura and tatsuta-age (wheat-battered or wheat-starch fried chicken) are menu staples; no dedicated fryer information was found, so shared-fryer cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No tamari or gluten-free soy sauce mentioned in any source; standard wheat-containing soy sauce is the baseline assumption for marinades, dipping sauces, and braised dishes.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Chawanmushi (egg custard) and dashi-based broth dishes are standard on the menu; fish-based dashi stock is the default in Japanese izakaya cooking and likely present across multiple dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy-handling policy or allergen menu was found in any source reviewed. Diners with dietary restrictions should call ahead (079-263-7936) or enquire in Japanese on arrival.

positives.text: Reservations accepted by phone or online, giving diners the opportunity to ask about ingredients in advance.

cultural_comfort.note: A small neighbourhood izakaya tucked into an arcade; no English menu noted and staff interaction is casual Japanese — a few words of Japanese and pointing will get you through.

places.name: はまもとコーヒー (Hamamoto Coffee)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free options. The signature almond toast is wheat bread topped with almond butter, almond powder, diced almonds, and sliced almonds. The morning set includes wheat toast. This is a wheat-centric kissaten with no GF alternatives noted anywhere. Celiac diners should avoid all food items; siphon-brewed black coffee is the only naturally GF option on the menu.
places.vegan_detail: No vegan options. The morning set (7:00–11:00) includes a boiled egg with any drink order. Almond toast uses butter in the almond spread. Standard kissaten menu with no plant-based alternatives noted. Coffee itself is vegan, but all food items contain eggs or dairy.
places.hours_raw: 7:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:30), closed Thu (open if a holiday). Official site lists closing at 17:00; some sources list 17:30 or 18:00 — verify on official site before visiting as hours may vary seasonally.
places.neighborhood: 二階町 (Nikacho) — Miyuki Street shopping arcade
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Hamamoto Coffee opened in 1975 on Himeji's Miyuki Street arcade. The shop began not as a cafe but as a greengrocer — the founding family ran a vegetable stall until the spread of supermarkets made it unviable. Rather than close, they pivoted to coffee, bringing the same emphasis

chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — family business now in its second generation
chef_bio.background: Hamamoto Coffee opened in 1975 on Himeji's Miyuki Street arcade. The shop began not as a cafe but as a greengrocer — the founding family ran a vegetable stall until the spread of supermarkets made it unviable. Rather than close, they pivoted to coffee, bringing the same emphasis on fresh sourcing and quality materials that defined their vegetable trade. The second-generation owner, Takuya Hamamoto, has carried this philosophy forward, summarising it as: "materials matter, just as with vegetables. "
chef_bio.philosophy: Light-roast, fresh beans sourced globally to preserve the coffee's native bitterness, acidity, and sweetness. Over 200 seasonal original blends have been rotated quarterly across 30-plus years. The owner adjusts water temperature daily so that each cup tastes consistent yet never tiring.
chef_bio.specialty: Siphon-brewed Hamamoto Blend coffee and Himeji's iconic almond toast — wheat bread spread with a house-made almond butter of almond powder, diced almonds, and sliced almonds, then toasted to a fragrant finish

anecdotes.text: The almond toast now beloved across Himeji traces its origins to Narita Coffee, a local bean retailer that obtained the recipe from a pastry shop over 30 years ago and shared it with kissaten clients — Hamamoto Coffee became one of its most celebrated interpreters.

The L-shaped marble counter and a world map on the wall set against copper engravings of Brazilian coffee farms give the interior a distinctly Showa-era atmosphere that regulars say feels unchanged across decades.

The owner is said to recognise regulars' coffee preferences by sight alone, adjusting their cup before they order, embodying a "trust with customers above all else" ethos that has sustained the shop for 50 years.
Selected for the national "Kissaten 100 Famous Shops 2021" (名曲喫茶100選) list, cementing its status as one of Japan's benchmark traditional coffee houses.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated GF preparation area or allergen segregation noted anywhere. This is a bread-and-toast-centric kissaten; wheat flour is central to the menu and cross-contamination must be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No soy sauce in the menu items identified. Coffee shop menu (coffee, toast, egg) does not appear to use soy sauce. However, no allergen declaration is publicly available.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eggs and dairy (almond butter spread) are used in core menu items throughout the kitchen; no plant-based separation noted.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy, allergen menu, or staff allergy training information was found across any source. Phone contact (079-282-2233) is the only avenue to inquire.

positives.text: Siphon-brewed black coffee (no milk, no soy sauce, no gluten ingredients) is naturally gluten-free and vegan as a standalone item — the safest choice for restricted diners who want to experience the shop.

cultural_comfort.note: An English menu is available; the counter-seat format with visible siphon brewing is visually self-explanatory and welcoming to first-time visitors.

places.name: spiceスエヒロ (Spice Suehiro)
places.gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free menu or certification. The curries are spice-based rather than roux-based, which reduces some GF risk, but no explicit claims are made about wheat-free preparation. Soy sauce (containing wheat) may be used in side items such as hinepon (chicken ponzu) or pickles. Shared kitchen with no stated dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac diners should call ahead or ask staff directly.
places.vegan_detail: One of the three rotating daily curries — a thick fried tofu and bean/spinach curry — is explicitly made without any animal products and was developed with vegan and allergy-conscious tourists in mind. The chicken and shrimp green curries are not vegan. No hidden-dashi or bonito details are confirmed for accompaniments (rice, pickles, papad), so ask on arrival.
places.hours_raw: 11:30–16:00 (closes when sold out), closed Thu & Fri
places.neighborhood: South of Himeji Castle (10 min walk from Sanyo-Himeji Stn)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] The owner trained across Italian, creative Japanese cuisine, and yakitori before finding his calling in spice curry. He opened spiceスエヒロ in 2015 near Himeji Castle, handcrafting every piece of the interior himself — counter, tables, wall-mounted spice racks — and designing the sh

chef_bio.background: The owner trained across Italian, creative Japanese cuisine, and yakitori before finding his calling in spice curry. He opened spiceスエヒロ in 2015 near Himeji Castle, handcrafting every piece of the interior himself — counter, tables, wall-mounted spice racks — and designing the shop's signage and silk-screen printed T-shirts. The name 'Suehiro' was not chosen for style but to honor an elderly woman who ran a modest bread and grocery shop in the Aboshi district of Himeji for 40 years; the owner wanted no pretension in the name. He has travelled to Sri Lanka but not India, and that South Asian sensibility filters into his South Indian-inflected spice style.
chef_bio.philosophy: Curry that hits with impact but can be eaten every day without tiring of it — built on slow-caramelised Awaji Island onions and over ten hand-blended spices, balanced so sweetness arrives first and the heat follows.
chef_bio.specialty: 三種スパイスカレー（チキン・グリーン・豆カレー）のあいがけ

anecdotes.text: The owner described his creative process with disarming simplicity: 'I just cooked it in my head, tried it, and made it so I could eat it.' The resulting chicken curry uses South Awaji caramelised onions as its base, cooked long and slow before the spice blend is added.

The vegan tofu and bean curry was deliberately developed to serve foreign tourists visiting Himeji Castle who have dietary restrictions or follow a plant-based diet — a rare act of intentional inclusivity for a tiny 11-seat shop.
Everything in the dining room was made by the owner's own hands: counter, tables, and the wall of spice bottles that greets you at the door, giving the compact space a handmade warmth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information on dedicated fryer or separate preparation surfaces. Papad (Indian lentil cracker) is served as a topping — traditional papad is naturally gluten-free, but shared kitchen risk is unknown.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Hinepon (chicken ponzu) and pickled accompaniments may contain soy sauce with wheat. No explicit wheat-free soy sauce or tamari use is stated anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan bean curry is served alongside chicken and shrimp curries in a shared kitchen. No statement about separate utensils or prep surfaces for the vegan option.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling policy or staff training is documented. The shop is a tiny owner-run counter with 11 seats — direct communication with the owner-chef is the realistic path for dietary questions.

positives.text: The tofu and bean curry was explicitly created without any animal products for vegan and allergy-aware tourists — demonstrating owner awareness of dietary needs, unusual for a small Japanese curry counter.
Spice-based curries (rather than Japanese roux blocks) avoid the wheat-laden roux found in most Japanese curry restaurants, reducing one common gluten source.

cultural_comfort.note: Small counter shop with limited English; welcoming to foreign tourists (vegan bean curry was explicitly created with international visitors in mind), but staff communication will be in Japanese.

places.name: 炉ばた焼きあらい (Robata-yaki Arai)
places.gf_detail: Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is the base condiment for both the robata glazes and the oden broth. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative, no dedicated fryer data, and no staff allergy-handling history is documented anywhere. Celiac or seriously wheat-sensitive diners should avoid.
places.vegan_detail: The entire menu is built around charcoal-grilled fish, meat, and chicken skewers, plus oden simmered in a dashi broth almost certainly made from bonito and kombu. No plant-only dishes were mentioned in any review or listing, and the kitchen has no track record of accommodating vegan requests.
places.hours_raw: 17:00–23:00 daily; closed days not confirmed across any source
places.neighborhood: 魚町 (Uocho)
places.notes: [Mom & Pop] Standard Japanese soy sauce (containing wheat) is the base condiment for both the robata glazes and the oden broth. No gluten-free soy sauce alternative, no dedicated fryer data, and no staff allergy-handling history is documented anywhere. Celiac or seriously wheat-sensitive din

chef_bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled fish (hokke/atka mackerel, saury, shishamo) and oden, served counter-side by the grill

anecdotes.text: A blogger described the counter seats around the hearth as perfect for groups of eight, with grilled rice balls glazed in ginger-soy sauce as a regular order and dishes delivered theatrically on the long robata paddle.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No information available; a shared kitchen grill environment handling soy-marinated fish and meat presents high cross-contamination risk for wheat-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Robata-yaki marinades and oden broth in Japanese izakayas standardly use wheat-containing soy sauce (koikuchi shoyu); no tamari or GF alternative has been documented for this restaurant.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All grilling surfaces and broth pots are shared with fish and meat; no vegan cross-contact mitigation documented.

cultural_comfort.note: A no-frills neighbourhood izakaya filled with Himeji regulars; staff unlikely to speak English and menus are Japanese only — pointing or gesturing at other diners' dishes works fine.

places.name: まねきのえきそば 在来線下り店 (Maneki no Ekisoba Zairaisen Kudari Ten)
places.gf_detail: The signature noodle is a Chinese-style alkaline (kansui) noodle made with wheat flour — wheat is listed as the primary ingredient. The broth contains powdered soy sauce, which is wheat-based. Tempura toppings are battered in wheat. Shrimp tempura cross-contact is noted even in the retail version. There is no gluten-free alternative on the menu. This is not safe for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
places.vegan_detail: The broth is a fish-based Japanese dashi made with bonito (katsuobushi) and mackerel (saba) powder, confirmed in the frozen retail product ingredient list. Soy sauce (which contains wheat) is also in the broth. There are no plant-based broth options, and no vegan menu items are listed. Kitsune (aburaage tofu) topping exists but sits in the fish dashi.
places.hours_raw: 06:00–24:00 daily (no regular closing day); source: Maneki Foods official shop list and Tabelog listing
places.neighborhood: Inside the station (JR Himeji Stn, conventional-line down platform)
places.notes: Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社) has operated at JR Himeji Station since 1888, originally as a railway bento seller. In the immediate post-war years of 1949, with wheat flour tightly rationed, the company's cooks experimented with konnyaku and buckwheat powders to make a noodle that coul

chef_bio.origin: Himeji, Hyogo, Japan — JR Himeji Station platforms since 1949
chef_bio.background: Maneki Foods (まねき食品株式会社) has operated at JR Himeji Station since 1888, originally as a railway bento seller. In the immediate post-war years of 1949, with wheat flour tightly rationed, the company's cooks experimented with konnyaku and buckwheat powders to make a noodle that could be served quickly to passengers. Through trial and error they landed on an unconventional pairing: yellow Chinese-style alkaline (kansui) noodles served in a traditional Japanese fish dashi. The dish was named 'ekisoba' (station soba) and launched on October 19, 1949 on the station platforms.
chef_bio.philosophy: Speed, affordability, and a sense of place: a bowl for the commuter with minutes to spare and a long journey ahead.
chef_bio.specialty: Ekisoba — yellow kansui noodles in a proprietary bonito-and-mackerel dashi broth, priced under ¥500. Tempura ekisoba and kitsune (fried tofu) ekisoba are the perennial favourites.

anecdotes.text: The dish is often described as a deliberate 'mismatch' — Chinese noodles in a Japanese broth — yet that unlikely combination became Himeji's most enduring soul food, outlasting fashions for 75 years.
To celebrate 75 years in March 2024, the Zairaisen Kudari store reopened after renovation and offered a limited gold-leaf shrimp tempura ekisoba for ¥750, capped at 75 bowls a day for seven days.
Afternoon discounts (14:00–17:00) shave ¥40 off the standard bowl, a legacy perk that has quietly survived every renovation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is the primary ingredient in the noodles (wheat flour, starch, salt per retail ingredient declaration). The broth contains powdered soy sauce, which is also wheat-based. Tempura toppings are wheat-battered. Gluten is present throughout every component of the dish.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dashi broth contains 粉末醤油 (powdered soy sauce), confirmed in the product ingredient list: '食塩、粉末醤油、砂糖、節粉末（かつお、さば）、昆布粉末'. Standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is made from bonito (katsuobushi) and mackerel (saba) powder; no plant-based broth alternative exists. Kitsune tofu topping is cooked in the same fish dashi. Shrimp (ebi) is listed as a declared allergen in the product.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No allergy handling information, allergen substitution, or dietary accommodation policy has been found for this counter. It is a high-throughput standing noodle station with no à la carte modification culture.

positives.text: The shop has been transparent about ingredients through its retail product line, which publishes a full allergen declaration (wheat, shrimp, egg) and a complete ingredient list, giving diners a clear picture of what is in the bowl.

cultural_comfort.note: A fast-paced standing noodle counter inside the train station; ordering is self-service at the counter, no English menu, and the format assumes familiarity with Japanese station dining conventions.

places.name: オーガニックラボ リベルテ (Organic Lab Liberte)
places.gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made. Menu includes pasta (wheat), fried chicken (likely wheat-battered), and daily soups of unknown composition. Soy sauce is almost certainly used in a restaurant at this level without explicit GF labeling. Counter-only 10-seat format offers an opportunity to ask, but no dedicated GF protocols are documented.
places.vegan_detail: Game meat (venison) is the signature and central focus of the menu — deer menchi-katsu, deer hamburger, deer meatball cream pasta, deer spice tomato curry. Not a vegan or vegetarian restaurant. Organic vegetable sides are present but accompany meat-based dishes.
places.neighborhood: 本町（Honmachi）
places.notes: Opened August 5, 2022 in the Honmachi district of Himeji, roughly 583 meters from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The restaurant operates out of a small 10-seat counter space with a hidden-gem character.

chef_bio.background: Opened August 5, 2022 in the Honmachi district of Himeji, roughly 583 meters from Sanyo-Himeji Station. The restaurant operates out of a small 10-seat counter space with a hidden-gem character.
